name: THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB

topics.title: Study of 4 Stroke and 2 Stroke IC Engine Components Using Cut Section Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of 4 Stroke and 2 Stroke IC Engine Components Using Cut Section Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cut Section Model?

A **Cut Section Model** of an Internal Combustion (IC) Engine is a specially prepared, **sacrificial demonstration engine** in which the cylinder block, cylinder head, crankcase, and other major assemblies have been **sliced (cut through) along their centreline planes** using a bandsaw or wire-EDM, and the cut faces are **polished, colour-coded, and lacquered** to permanently expose the internal working components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Lab Manual Standard):**
> A cut section model is a *static, non-functional teaching aid* used to study the **internal construction, arrangement, relative motion, and component identification** of a 4-stroke or 2-stroke spark-ignition/compression-ignition engine, by physically exposing its reciprocating and rotating parts through a longitudinal or transverse cut.

## 1.2 Purpose of a Cut Section Model

| Aspect | Why it is studied |
|---|---|
| **Visual learning** | Replaces opaque cast-iron blocks with a transparent cross-section view |
| **Component identification** | Allows naming of more than **40 individual parts** in one glance |
| **Motion study** | Shows the relative position of piston, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves, tappets, and flywheel |
| **Comparison** | Side-by-side study of 4-stroke (4 events in 2 revolutions) vs 2-stroke (2 events in 1 revolution) |
| **Valve timing visualization** | Helps correlate theoretical P-V and T-s diagrams with actual mechanical hardware |

> [!NOTE]
> A cut section model is **NOT meant to run**. It is a **disassembled-reassembled teaching aid** where all the subassemblies (valve mechanism, lubrication system, cooling system, ignition system) are exposed for inspection.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have a sealed metal box (the engine block) with mysterious moving parts inside that you can only hear but not see. Now imagine slicing the box neatly in half like a loaf of bread and painting the inner surfaces in bright colours. Suddenly you can see the **piston sliding up and down**, the **crankshaft rotating**, the **camshaft lifting valves**, the **spark plug sitting over the combustion chamber**, and the **connecting rod converting linear motion to rotary motion** — all frozen at a chosen crank angle. The cut section model does exactly this for an engine.

## 1.4 Types of Cut Section Models Used in KTU Labs

1. **Longitudinal Cut (Centreline Cut)** – The block is cut along the axis of the cylinder. This is the most common type and exposes the **piston, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves, and tappets** in a single plane.
2. **Transverse Cut (Cross Cut)** – The block is cut perpendicular to the cylinder axis. Useful for studying **valve arrangement (I-head, F-head, L-head)** and **combustion chamber geometry**.
3. **Half-Section Models** – One half of the engine is removed entirely, leaving a "half-engine" with full visibility of internal parts.
4. **Sectioned Sub-Assemblies** – Individual cut sections of the **carburettor, fuel pump, oil pump, distributor, and governor** are also displayed alongside the main engine model.
5. **Working Cut Models (with crank handle)** – Some advanced lab models have an external **crank handle** that manually rotates the crankshaft, allowing the student to physically observe valve opening/closing, piston travel, and the four strokes in real time.

## 1.5 Why KTU Includes This Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Identify, label, and explain the function of major components of SI and CI engines using cut section models and disassembled assemblies."*
> It also covers **CO3**: *"Correlate theoretical cycles (Otto, Diesel, Dual) with the actual mechanical hardware that realizes them."*

The marks distribution in the KTU 2024 continuous evaluation for this lab experiment typically includes:
- **Identification and labelling of components (10 marks)**
- **Sketch and explanation of working cycles (15 marks)**
- **Viva voce (5 marks)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Stroke Spark Ignition (SI) Engine — Components as Seen in the Cut Section

A 4-stroke SI engine cut section reveals the following component groups:

### A. Stationary (Fixed) Components
- **Cylinder Block** – The main cast-iron or aluminium alloy body bored to receive the cylinder liner.
- **Cylinder Liner (Sleeve)** – A replaceable wear-resistant sleeve pressed into the block; it forms the working surface for the piston.
- **Cylinder Head** – Bolted to the top of the block; houses inlet valve, exhaust valve, spark plug, and sometimes the camshaft (in OHC engines).
- **Crankcase** – The lower housing that encloses the crankshaft and holds the lubricating oil sump.
- **Manifolds** – Inlet manifold (carries air-fuel mixture) and exhaust manifold (carries burnt gases away).

### B. Reciprocating Components
- **Piston** – A cylindrical aluminium alloy part that slides in the liner. Usually marked with **3 ring grooves** (2 compression rings + 1 oil control ring).
- **Piston Rings** – Spring-loaded metallic rings fitted in the piston grooves that seal combustion gases and scrape excess oil.
- **Wrist Pin (Gudgeon Pin)** – A hardened steel pin that connects the piston to the small end of the connecting rod.
- **Connecting Rod (Con Rod)** – A forged steel I-section member with a **small end** (connected to piston via gudgeon pin) and a **big end** (connected to crankshaft via crank pin).

### C. Rotating Components
- **Crankshaft** – The main rotating shaft that converts the reciprocating motion of the piston into rotary motion. Carries **crank webs, crank pins, counterweights, and main journals**.
- **Flywheel** – A heavy disc mounted on the crankshaft end that stores rotational inertia and smooths out power delivery between power strokes.
- **Camshaft** – A shaft carrying **cams (eccentric lobes)** that actuate the valves. Driven by the crankshaft through a **timing chain, timing belt, or gear train** at exactly **half the crankshaft speed** in a 4-stroke engine.

### D. Valve Train Components
- **Inlet Valve** – Larger diameter; allows fresh air-fuel mixture into the cylinder.
- **Exhaust Valve** – Smaller diameter; allows burnt gases to escape.
- **Valve Springs** – Helical springs that close the valves when the cam lobe rotates away.
- **Tappets (Lifters)** – Cylindrical elements that ride on the cam lobes and transmit motion upward.
- **Push Rods** (in OHV engines only) – Transfer motion from tappet to rocker arm.
- **Rocker Arms** – Pivoted levers that push the valve down when actuated.
- **Valve Guides and Valve Seats** – Precision-machined bores and rings that guide and seal the valves.

### E. Other Sub-Assemblies Visible in Cut Section
- **Spark Plug** – Screwed into the cylinder head, ignites the compressed mixture.
- **Distributor** (in older engines) – Routes high-voltage pulse to the correct cylinder.
- **Oil Pump and Oil Sump** – Visible in the cut crankcase; supplies pressurized oil.
- **Water Pump and Cooling Jackets** – Visible as passages around the cylinder.

## 2.2 The 2-Stroke Engine — Components as Seen in the Cut Section

A 2-stroke engine cut section looks similar to a 4-stroke model in the upper cylinder region but differs significantly in the **crankcase, port arrangement, and absence of a dedicated valve train**.

### Key Differences Visible in Cut Section
| Feature | 4-Stroke | 2-Stroke |
|---|---|---|
| **Valves** | Poppet valves in head | Usually **ports** in the cylinder walls |
| **Camshaft** | Present, geared to crank at 1:2 | Absent (port timing governed by piston skirt) |
| **Crankshaft revolutions per cycle** | 2 (720°) | 1 (360°) |
| **Power strokes per revolution** | 1 in every 2 | 1 in every 1 |
| **Crankcase** | Sealed only for oil | Acts as a **scavenging pump** (in crankcase-scavenged designs) |
| **Inlet** | Through inlet valve | Through **inlet port** uncovered by piston |
| **Exhaust** | Through exhaust valve | Through **exhaust port** uncovered by piston |
| **Transfer** | N/A | Through **transfer ports** feeding fresh charge from crankcase to cylinder |

### Components Unique to 2-Stroke (Visible in Cut Section)
- **Inlet Port** – Rectangular window on the cylinder wall, covered/uncovered by the piston skirt.
- **Exhaust Port** – Similar window on the opposite side, opened slightly earlier than transfer ports.
- **Transfer Ports** – Usually 2 or 3 ports that direct the fresh charge from the crankcase up into the cylinder above the piston.
- **Reed Valve or Rotary Disc Valve** – In the crankcase inlet, prevents backflow of fresh charge to the carburettor.
- **Spark Plug** – Positioned to fire just before TDC.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — Cut Section Identification

| Sl. No. | Component | Location in Cut Section | Material (Typical) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cylinder Block | Main body, lower half | Cast Iron / Al alloy | Houses cylinder liner and supports crankshaft |
| 2 | Cylinder Liner | Inner bore of block | Cast Iron (Ni-Cr) | Wear-resistant surface for piston |
| 3 | Cylinder Head | Top of block | Cast Iron / Al alloy | Closes cylinder, holds valves & plug |
| 4 | Piston | Inside liner | Al-Si alloy | Reciprocates to compress charge |
| 5 | Piston Rings | Grooves on piston | Cast Iron / steel | Seal gas, control oil film |
| 6 | Gudgeon Pin | Through piston boss | Hardened steel | Pivots con rod small end |
| 7 | Connecting Rod | Piston to crank | Forged steel | Converts linear to rotary motion |
| 8 | Crankshaft | In crankcase | Forged steel | Output rotating shaft |
| 9 | Flywheel | End of crankshaft | Cast iron | Stores inertia |
| 10 | Camshaft | Above/within block | Hardened steel | Operates valves (4-S only) |
| 11 | Inlet Valve | In head | Steel with hard-facing | Admits fresh charge |
| 12 | Exhaust Valve | In head | Heat-resistant steel | Releases burnt gases |
| 13 | Valve Spring | Around valve stem | Spring steel | Closes valve |
| 14 | Tappet | Between cam & push rod | Hardened steel | Transmits cam lift |
| 15 | Push Rod | In OHV only | Steel tube | Transfers tappet motion to rocker |
| 16 | Rocker Arm | On rocker shaft | Steel casting | Pushes valve open |
| 17 | Spark Plug | In cylinder head | Steel + ceramic insulator | Ignites compressed mixture |
| 18 | Inlet Port (2-S) | Cylinder wall | — | Admits charge in 2-S |
| 19 | Exhaust Port (2-S) | Cylinder wall | — | Releases burnt gases in 2-S |
| 20 | Transfer Port (2-S) | Cylinder wall | — | Directs fresh charge to cylinder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Cut Section Study:**
> 1. **Design verification** — Confirms that the assembled engine geometry matches the engineering drawing.
> 2. **Failure analysis** — Worn piston rings, scored liners, burnt valves, and cracked connecting rods are easily spotted.
> 3. **Maintenance training** — Mechanics-in-training dismantle and reassemble cut models to learn the **correct sequence and torque procedures** before touching a live engine.
> 4. **Sales and training tool** — Used in dealerships, technical institutes, and OEM training centres worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Comparing 4-Stroke and 2-Stroke Cut Sections

> Since this experiment is a **practical observation-based study** rather than a pure mathematical derivation, Section 3 is presented as a **multi-tier analytical matrix** that walks the student step-by-step through the comparison, observation, and reasoning required during the lab record writing and viva.

## 3.1 Tier-1: The Four Strokes of a 4-Stroke SI Engine (Observe on the Crank Handle)

Rotate the manual handle of the cut model slowly. Watch the piston and valves carefully. You will observe four distinct strokes occurring over **two full revolutions of the crankshaft (720°)**:

### Step 1 — Suction Stroke (0° to 180°)
- **Crank motion:** From TDC to BDC (first 180°).
- **Piston motion:** Moves downward from Top Dead Centre to Bottom Dead Centre.
- **Inlet valve:** **OPEN** (cam lobe has lifted it through tappet and rocker arm).
- **Exhaust valve:** Closed, held by spring.
- **Observed event:** Fresh air-fuel mixture is drawn through the open inlet valve into the cylinder, creating a partial vacuum.

### Step 2 — Compression Stroke (180° to 360°)
- **Crank motion:** From BDC back to TDC (second 180°).
- **Piston motion:** Moves upward from BDC to TDC.
- **Inlet valve:** Closed.
- **Exhaust valve:** Closed.
- **Observed event:** The mixture is compressed to a high pressure (typically 8–12 bar in SI engines) and a high temperature.

### Step 3 — Power (Expansion) Stroke (360° to 540°)
- **Crank motion:** From TDC to BDC (third 180°).
- **Piston motion:** Forced downward by expanding combustion gases.
- **Inlet valve:** Closed.
- **Exhaust valve:** Closed.
- **Observed event:** The spark plug fires just before TDC. Combustion raises the pressure to 30–50 bar, driving the piston down.

### Step 4 — Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°)
- **Crank motion:** From BDC to TDC (fourth 180°).
- **Piston motion:** Moves upward, pushing out burnt gases.
- **Inlet valve:** Closed.
- **Exhaust valve:** **OPEN.**
- **Observed event:** Exhaust gases are expelled through the open exhaust valve.

> [!NOTE]
> Note carefully in the cut section that the **camshaft rotates exactly once** while the **crankshaft rotates twice** — this 1:2 gearing is achieved by a **timing gear (or chain) with twice as many teeth on the crankshaft gear as on the camshaft gear**.

## 3.2 Tier-2: The Two Strokes of a 2-Stroke Engine (Observe on the Crank Handle)

In a 2-stroke cut model, you will see **no poppet valves in the head** and **no camshaft**. Instead, you will see **rectangular ports cut into the cylinder walls**. The complete cycle occurs in **one revolution (360°)** of the crankshaft.

### Step 1 — Compression + Crankcase Suction (0° to 180°)
- **Piston motion:** Moves upward from BDC to TDC.
- **Inlet port:** **UNCOVERED** by the descending piston skirt during the early part, then closed.
- **Transfer ports:** Closed (covered by piston).
- **Exhaust port:** Closed (covered by piston).
- **Reed valve:** Open (if fitted), drawing fresh charge into the crankcase beneath the piston.
- **Observed event:** Above the piston, the fresh charge is **compressed**. Below the piston in the crankcase, a **partial vacuum** is created, drawing in fresh charge through the reed valve.

### Step 2 — Power + Scavenging (180° to 360°)
- **Piston motion:** Moves downward from TDC to BDC.
- **Spark plug fires** just before TDC, igniting the compressed charge.
- **Combustion** drives the piston down — the **power stroke**.
- As the piston descends, it first **uncovers the exhaust port** (allowing burnt gases to escape), then **uncovers the transfer ports** (allowing fresh charge from the crankcase to enter the cylinder and push out the remaining exhaust gas — this is called **scavenging**).
- **Reed valve:** Closes (positive crankcase pressure pushes it shut), preventing backflow.
- **Observed event:** Three actions happen simultaneously: **(a) power delivery to the crank, (b) exhaust blow-down, (c) transfer/scavenging of fresh charge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation on the Cut Model:** Notice the **port timing** — the exhaust port top edge is positioned **slightly higher** than the transfer port top edge on the cylinder wall. This ensures the exhaust port opens **first** (around 25–30° before BDC) and the transfer ports open later, giving a **scavenging delay** that prevents fresh charge from escaping directly out the exhaust.

## 3.3 Tier-3: Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 4-Stroke SI Engine | 2-Stroke SI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of strokes per cycle** | 4 (Suction, Compression, Power, Exhaust) | 2 (Compression+Suction, Power+Scavenging) |
| **Crankshaft revolutions per cycle** | 2 (720°) | 1 (360°) |
| **Power strokes per cycle** | 1 | 1 |
| **Power strokes per minute (at N rpm)** | N / 2 | N |
| **Theoretical power for same displacement** | P | **2P** (in practice only 1.3P–1.7P) |
| **Camshaft present?** | Yes, geared 1:2 to crankshaft | No |
| **Poppet valves** | Yes (in head or block) | No (port-based) |
| **Port arrangement** | None (valves only) | Inlet, Transfer, Exhaust ports on cylinder wall |
| **Crankcase function** | Houses oil sump only | Acts as **scavenging pump** |
| **Reed/rotary valve in crankcase** | Not required | Yes (to prevent backflow) |
| **Valve overlap concept** | Exists (inlet opens before exhaust closes) | Not applicable (port-based) |
| **Lubrication system** | Wet sump / dry sump forced feed | Often **total-loss oil injection** (petrol 2-S) |
| **Cooling** | Water jacket or air-cooled fins | Predominantly air-cooled |
| **Thermal efficiency (ideal Otto)** | Same theoretical value | Same theoretical value |
| **Actual thermal efficiency** | Higher (~25–30%) | Lower (~20–25%) due to scavenging losses |
| **Specific fuel consumption** | Lower | Higher |
| **Weight-to-power ratio** | Heavier | Lighter (simpler construction) |
| **Typical applications** | Cars, LMVs, gensets, tractors | Two-wheelers, marine outboards, chainsaws, mopeds |
| **Exhaust emissions** | Cleaner (dedicated exhaust stroke) | Higher HC due to scavenging losses |
| **Cost** | Higher (more parts, camshaft, valves) | Lower (fewer parts) |
| **Noise & vibration** | Smoother | Higher (uneven firing pulses) |
| **Visible components in cut section** | Block, head, valves, camshaft, tappets, push rods, rockers | Block, head, ports (no valves/camshaft), reed valve, transfer passages |

## 3.4 Tier-4: Component-by-Component Identification Procedure (for Lab Record)

When the examiner hands you a cut section model in the lab, follow this **systematic identification sequence** to avoid missing parts:

### Step 1 — Start from the Cylinder Head and Work Downward
1. Spark plug
2. Inlet valve and spring
3. Exhaust valve and spring
4. Rocker arm assembly
5. Push rod (if OHV)
6. Cylinder head bolts
7. Head gasket joint face

### Step 2 — Move to the Cylinder Block Region
1. Cylinder liner (if separate)
2. Piston
3. Piston rings (count them: usually 2 compression + 1 oil control)
4. Gudgeon pin
5. Connecting rod (small end, shank, big end, big-end cap, big-end bolts)
6. Water jacket passages

### Step 3 — Examine the Crankcase Region
1. Crankshaft (main journals, crank pin, crank webs, counterweights)
2. Main bearings
3. Camshaft (4-S only) — note number of lobes equals number of valves
4. Timing gear / chain
5. Oil pump
6. Oil sump / pan
7. Flywheel (and ring gear teeth around its periphery)

### Step 4 — Identify External Accessories
1. Inlet manifold and carburettor
2. Exhaust manifold
3. Distributor / ignition coil (older engines)
4. Starter motor mounting flange
5. Alternator bracket
6. Fuel pump

### Step 5 — For 2-Stroke Models, Specifically Look For
1. Reed valve inside crankcase inlet
2. Three port windows on cylinder wall
3. Spark plug position relative to TDC
4. Absence of camshaft and rocker assembly
5. Transfer passage routing inside the block

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Always **state the function of every component as you identify it** — examiners award marks for the *understanding* behind the name, not merely the label. A student who says *"This is the connecting rod — it converts the linear motion of the piston into the rotary motion of the crankshaft"* scores higher than one who merely says *"Connecting rod"*.

## 3.5 Tier-5: Calculation of Theoretical Power Ratio (4-Stroke vs 2-Stroke)

The cut section model itself does not run, but the *conceptual exercise* of why a 2-stroke should produce twice the power for the same displacement is often asked in the viva. Here is the analytical derivation:

Let:
- $P_{4S}$ = Power developed by a 4-stroke engine at $N$ rpm
- $P_{2S}$ = Power developed by a 2-stroke engine at the same $N$ rpm
- $W$ = Work done per power stroke (assumed same for both, same swept volume, same cycle)

$$
n_{4S} = \frac{N}{2} \quad \text{(power strokes per minute in a 4-stroke)}
$$

$$
n_{2S} = N \quad \text{(power strokes per minute in a 2-stroke)}
$$

$$
P_{4S} = \frac{n_{4S} \cdot W}{60} = \frac{N \cdot W}{120}
$$

$$
P_{2S} = \frac{n_{2S} \cdot W}{60} = \frac{N \cdot W}{60}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{2S}}{P_{4S}} = \frac{\frac{N \cdot W}{60}}{\frac{N \cdot W}{120}} = 2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{2S,\text{theoretical}} = 2 \times P_{4S}\,}
$$

However, in practice, the 2-stroke engine only delivers about **1.3 to 1.7 times** the power because of:
- **Scavenging losses** — fresh charge escapes with exhaust gases.
- **Incomplete combustion** — short time available.
- **Reduced effective compression** — port opening reduces the effective compression ratio.
- **Higher friction and heat losses** — higher specific output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **KTU viva favourite** and is also a 3-mark short-answer question in the university exam for the *Theory of Machines* and *Thermal Engineering* theory courses linked to this lab.

## 4.3 Schematic — 4-Stroke Cycle Visual Mapping to Cut Section Events

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ST1[STROKE 1 SUCTION 0 TO 180]
        S1A[PISTON MOVES DOWN]
        S1B[INLET VALVE OPEN]
        S1C[EXHAUST VALVE CLOSED]
        S1D[FRESH MIXTURE ENTERS]
    end
    subgraph ST2[STROKE 2 COMPRESSION 180 TO 360]
        S2A[PISTON MOVES UP]
        S2B[INLET VALVE CLOSED]
        S2C[EXHAUST VALVE CLOSED]
        S2D[MIXTURE COMPRESSED]
    end
    subgraph ST3[STROKE 3 POWER 360 TO 540]
        S3A[PISTON FORCED DOWN]
        S3B[SPARK FIRES]
        S3C[COMBUSTION DRIVES PISTON]
        S3D[CRANK RECEIVES TORQUE]
    end
    subgraph ST4[STROKE 4 EXHAUST 540 TO 720]
        S4A[PISTON MOVES UP]
        S4B[EXHAUST VALVE OPEN]
        S4C[BURNT GASES PUSHED OUT]
    end

    ST1 --> ST2
    ST2 --> ST3
    ST3 --> ST4
    ST4 --> ST1
```

## 4.4 Schematic — 2-Stroke Cycle Visual Mapping to Cut Section Events

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[STROKE 1 COMPRESSION PLUS SUCTION 0 TO 180]
        T1A[PISTON MOVES UP]
        T1B[CRANKCASE DRAWS FRESH CHARGE VIA REED VALVE]
        T1C[CHARGE ABOVE PISTON COMPRESSED]
    end
    subgraph T2[STROKE 2 POWER PLUS SCAVENGING 180 TO 360]
        T2A[SPARK IGNITES CHARGE]
        T2B[PISTON FORCED DOWN BY COMBUSTION]
        T2C[PISTON UNCOVERS EXHAUST PORT BLOW DOWN]
        T2D[PISTON UNCOVERS TRANSFER PORTS FRESH CHARGE ENTERS]
        T2E[SCAVENGING PUSHES OUT BURNT GASES]
    end

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above are **functional architecture maps** rather than photorealistic engine drawings. In the actual lab, refer to the colour-coded laminated reference diagram provided alongside the cut section model for true-to-life component appearance.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1.** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the function of the **connecting rod** in an IC engine. Why is it made in **I-section**?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The connecting rod is a forged steel member that connects the **piston (via the gudgeon pin at its small end)** to the **crankshaft (via the crank pin at its big end)**. Its primary function is to **convert the reciprocating (linear) motion of the piston into the rotary motion of the crankshaft**, and to transmit the thrust of combustion from the piston to the crank.
> It is made in **I-section** because this shape provides a **high second moment of area about the bending axis** with the **minimum amount of material**, giving an excellent **strength-to-weight ratio**. The I-section resists buckling under the compressive load during the compression stroke and bending under the side thrust during the power stroke. **(3 marks — 1 for diagram, 1 for function, 1 for I-section reason.)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **4-stroke and a 2-stroke IC engine** based on **(a)** number of power strokes per cycle, **(b)** presence of camshaft, and **(c)** typical application.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Parameter | 4-Stroke Engine | 2-Stroke Engine |
> |---|---|---|
> | (a) Power strokes per cycle | 1 power stroke in **2 revolutions (720°)** | 1 power stroke in **1 revolution (360°)** |
> | (b) Camshaft | Present, geared **1:2** to crankshaft | **Absent** — port timing by piston |
> | (c) Typical application | Cars, tractors, gensets, LMVs | Two-wheelers, mopeds, chainsaws, marine outboards |
>
> **(3 marks — 1 mark for each correct row.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Group
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch of a **4-stroke SI engine cut section model**, identify and list the function of any **ten (10) major components**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **complete working of a 4-stroke SI engine** with reference to the four strokes, clearly stating the **crank angle, valve positions, and piston motion** for each stroke. Also state why the **camshaft rotates at half the speed of the crankshaft**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Component Identification (7 marks)**

*[The student should draw a neat labelled cut section diagram of a 4-stroke SI engine and mark the following ten components — 0.5 mark for correct label and 0.2 mark for function, totalling 7 marks.]*

| Sl. | Component | Function (to be written in the answer) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cylinder Block** | Houses the cylinder liner and supports the crankshaft and camshaft bearings. |
| 2 | **Cylinder Head** | Closes the top of the cylinder; carries valves, spark plug, and manifolds. |
| 3 | **Piston** | Reciprocates inside the cylinder to compress the charge and receive combustion pressure. |
| 4 | **Connecting Rod** | Transmits force from piston to crankshaft, converting linear to rotary motion. |
| 5 | **Crankshaft** | Output shaft that delivers rotary power; converts piston reciprocation into rotation. |
| 6 | **Camshaft** | Operates the inlet and exhaust valves through a 1:2 gear reduction from the crankshaft. |
| 7 | **Flywheel** | Stores rotational inertia to smooth out the single power stroke per two revolutions. |
| 8 | **Inlet Valve** | Allows fresh air-fuel mixture into the cylinder during the suction stroke. |
| 9 | **Exhaust Valve** | Releases burnt combustion gases during the exhaust stroke. |
| 10 | **Spark Plug** | Provides the high-voltage spark to ignite the compressed mixture. |

**Part (b) — Working of 4-Stroke SI Engine (7 marks)**

**Stroke 1 — Suction Stroke (0° to 180°):**
The piston moves from **TDC to BDC**. The **inlet valve is open** (lifted by the cam lobe through tappet, push rod, and rocker arm). The **exhaust valve is closed**. A partial vacuum is created below the descending piston, drawing the air-fuel mixture from the carburettor through the inlet manifold and into the cylinder. *(1.5 marks)*

**Stroke 2 — Compression Stroke (180° to 360°):**
Both valves are **closed** (held by valve springs). The piston moves from **BDC back to TDC**, compressing the trapped charge to a high pressure (typically 8–12 bar) and temperature (around 400–500°C). *(1.5 marks)*

**Stroke 3 — Power (Expansion) Stroke (360° to 540°):**
Just before TDC (about 10–20° before), the **spark plug fires**, igniting the compressed mixture. Combustion raises the pressure to 30–50 bar, forcing the piston down from **TDC to BDC**. Both valves remain closed. The kinetic energy of the descending piston is transmitted through the connecting rod to the crankshaft, producing useful **rotary work**. *(1.5 marks)*

**Stroke 4 — Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°):**
The **exhaust valve opens** (lifted by its cam lobe) and the piston moves from **BDC back to TDC**, pushing the burnt combustion gases out through the exhaust port and exhaust manifold. The inlet valve is closed. *(1.5 marks)*

**Why Camshaft Rotates at Half the Crankshaft Speed (1 mark):**
In a 4-stroke engine, the inlet and exhaust valves must each open **only once per two full revolutions** of the crankshaft. The inlet valve opens during the suction stroke (one revolution) and remains closed for the next revolution. Since the camshaft carries one lobe per valve, and each lobe must operate its valve once per 720°, the camshaft must rotate at exactly **N/2 rpm** where N is the crankshaft rpm. This is achieved by a **timing gear with twice as many teeth on the crankshaft gear as on the camshaft gear** (a 2:1 ratio).

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch of a **2-stroke SI engine cut section model**, identify the **inlet, transfer, and exhaust ports** and explain their function. State the role of the **reed valve**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **complete working of a 2-stroke SI engine** highlighting how the **crankcase acts as a scavenging pump**. Also prove analytically that a 2-stroke engine theoretically produces **twice the power** of a 4-stroke engine of the same swept volume and speed. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Port Identification and Reed Valve (7 marks)**

The cut section of a 2-stroke SI engine shows **three rectangular windows (ports) cut into the cylinder wall**, which replace the poppet valves of a 4-stroke engine. The piston skirt acts as the sliding valve that covers and uncovers these ports as it reciprocates. *(0.5 mark for diagram)*

**Inlet Port (1.5 marks):**
Located on the side of the cylinder, just above the crankcase. When the piston is at TDC, its skirt **uncovers the inlet port**, allowing fresh charge from the crankcase (already pressurized by the descending piston) to enter the cylinder. The reed valve (a one-way flap made of thin spring steel) in the crankcase inlet prevents the fresh charge from flowing back into the carburettor during the compression stroke.

**Transfer Ports (2 marks):**
Usually 2 or 3 ports positioned symmetrically around the cylinder wall, slightly lower than the exhaust port. They are connected through **cast passages inside the block** to the crankcase volume. When the piston descends past the transfer port level, the pressurized fresh charge in the crankcase is **routed upward through these passages** into the cylinder above the piston. The transfer ports are angled to direct the incoming charge **upward and toward the cylinder head**, creating a swirling loop that pushes the residual exhaust gas out through the exhaust port. This is the **scavenging** process.

**Exhaust Port (1.5 marks):**
Positioned on the opposite side of the cylinder from the inlet port, with its top edge **slightly higher** than the transfer port top edge. The exhaust port opens first (around 25–30° before BDC) — this is the **blow-down phase** — releasing the high-pressure burnt gases. The slight height difference (called **port timing offset**) ensures that scavenging is completed cleanly without losing fresh charge.

**Reed Valve (1.5 marks):**
A thin, flexible **petal-shaped or wedge-shaped flap** of spring steel mounted in the crankcase inlet tract. It opens when the crankcase pressure is below atmospheric (during the upward stroke) and **snaps shut** when the crankcase pressure rises (during the downward stroke), preventing the pressurized fresh charge from being pushed back into the carburettor. Without the reed valve, the 2-stroke engine would lose its charge through the carburettor and would not be able to build up crankcase pressure for scavenging.

**Part (b) — Working of 2-Stroke Engine and Power Ratio Proof (7 marks)**

**Working — Stroke 1 (0° to 180°) — Compression + Crankcase Suction (3 marks):**
The piston moves **upward from BDC to TDC**. During this upward motion:
- Above the piston: The fresh charge (drawn in during the previous cycle) is **compressed** to a high pressure.
- Below the piston: The rising piston **expands the crankcase volume**, lowering the pressure below atmospheric. The reed valve opens and **fresh air-fuel mixture is drawn in** from the carburettor into the crankcase.
- Just before TDC, the **spark plug fires**, igniting the compressed charge above the piston.
- All ports are covered by the piston skirt at TDC.

**Working — Stroke 2 (180° to 360°) — Power + Scavenging (2.5 marks):**
Combustion drives the piston **downward from TDC to BDC**. As the piston descends:
- The high-pressure burnt gases above the piston push the piston down — this is the **power stroke**.
- The descending piston **compresses the fresh charge** in the crankcase, closing the reed valve and building up crankcase pressure.
- As the piston skirt passes the **exhaust port top edge**, the exhaust port opens — burnt gases rush out (blow-down).
- A few degrees later, the piston skirt uncovers the **transfer ports** — the pressurized fresh charge in the crankcase **rises through the transfer passages** into the cylinder, sweeping out the remaining exhaust gas in a process called **scavenging**.
- The cycle then repeats.

**Analytical Proof of Power Ratio (1.5 marks):**

Let $N$ = crankshaft rpm (same for both engines), $W$ = work done per power stroke (assumed identical for same swept volume and cycle).

For the 4-stroke engine, the number of power strokes per minute is:
$$
n_{4S} = \frac{N}{2}
$$
$$
P_{4S} = \frac{n_{4S} \cdot W}{60} = \frac{N \cdot W}{120}
$$

For the 2-stroke engine, the number of power strokes per minute is:
$$
n_{2S} = N
$$
$$
P_{2S} = \frac{n_{2S} \cdot W}{60} = \frac{N \cdot W}{60}
$$

$$
\frac{P_{2S}}{P_{4S}} = \frac{\frac{N \cdot W}{60}}{\frac{N \cdot W}{120}} = 2
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{2S} = 2 \cdot P_{4S}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **No diagram = at least 2 marks lost.** Always draw a neat labelled cut section sketch, even if the question only asks for explanation. The diagram is worth 30–50% of the marks for component-identification questions.
> 2. **Confusing the camshaft rotation direction.** Students often write that the camshaft rotates at *twice* the crankshaft speed. The correct statement is the opposite: the camshaft rotates at **half** the crankshaft speed.
> 3. **Forgetting the camshaft timing ratio.** It is not enough to say "camshaft rotates at half speed" — you must mention the **2:1 tooth ratio on the timing gears** for full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up 4-stroke terminology.** Writing "compression stroke" when you mean "power stroke" is a very common slip during viva. Memorize the four strokes in order: **S-C-P-E** (Suction, Compression, Power, Exhaust).
> 5. **In 2-stroke port timing, students often draw the exhaust port below the transfer port.** This is wrong — the exhaust port must be **higher** than the transfer port to open first. Drawing it wrong loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the reed valve function.** A surprising number of students list the reed valve as just a "valve" without explaining its **one-way** action in preventing backflow. Be specific: *it opens on suction, closes on compression*.
> 7. **Ignoring the practical power ratio.** The theoretical $P_{2S} = 2 P_{4S}$ is only true under ideal conditions. Examiners often award bonus marks if you state that **in practice, 2-stroke engines produce only 1.3–1.7 times** the power due to scavenging and combustion losses.
> 8. **Labelling errors in the cut section.** Make sure the **big end** of the connecting rod is connected to the **crank pin** (not the main journal), and the **small end** is connected to the **gudgeon pin** (not the piston boss directly). Mixing these up loses identification marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ A **cut section model** is a static, sliced-apart engine used purely for component identification and motion study — it does **not run**.
- ☐ A **4-stroke engine** completes its working cycle in **2 crank revolutions (720°)** with **1 power stroke**.
- ☐ A **2-stroke engine** completes its working cycle in **1 crank revolution (360°)** with **1 power stroke**.
- ☐ The **4 strokes of an SI engine** in order are: **Suction → Compression → Power → Exhaust** (mnemonic: **S-C-P-E**).
- ☐ The **camshaft in a 4-stroke** rotates at **half** the crankshaft speed via a **2:1 timing gear/chain ratio**.
- ☐ The **2 strokes of a 2-stroke engine** combine events: **Stroke 1 = Compression above piston + Suction in crankcase; Stroke 2 = Power above piston + Scavenging into cylinder**.
- ☐ The **crankcase of a 2-stroke** acts as a **scavenging pump**; a **reed valve** prevents backflow.
- ☐ The **exhaust port** in a 2-stroke is positioned **higher** than the **transfer ports** to ensure exhaust blow-down happens before scavenging.
- ☐ The **connecting rod** is made in **I-section** for high strength-to-weight ratio and resistance to buckling/bending.
- ☐ The **theoretical power ratio** is $P_{2S} = 2 \times P_{4S}$; **practical ratio** is only **1.3 to 1.7** due to losses.
- ☐ **Poppet valves** (with springs) are characteristic of **4-stroke** engines; **ports** (without valves) are characteristic of **2-stroke** engines.
- ☐ **Cylinder head** carries the spark plug (in SI) or injector (in CI) and the valves in overhead-valve 4-stroke engines.
- ☐ **Camshaft lobes** = **number of valves** (one lobe per valve in a 4-stroke SI engine).
- ☐ **Piston rings** are typically **3** — **2 compression rings** (top) and **1 oil control ring** (bottom).
- ☐ **Spark plug fires** just before TDC (around **10–20° BTDC**) during the compression stroke.
- ☐ **4-stroke applications** — cars, tractors, gensets; **2-stroke applications** — mopeds, chainsaws, marine outboards.
- ☐ **Always draw a labelled diagram** in the KTU exam — it carries 30–50% of the marks.
- ☐ For the lab record, **state the function of every identified component** — never give a bare label.
- ☐ **Specific fuel consumption** of a 4-stroke is **lower**; **2-stroke has higher HC emissions** due to scavenging losses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Timing Diagram of a 4-Stroke Petrol/Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Valve Timing Diagram of a 4-Stroke Petrol/Diesel Engine ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valve Timing Diagram**: It is a graphical representation of the precise angular positions (in degrees of crank rotation) at which the **intake and exhaust valves** of a four-stroke Internal Combustion (IC) engine open and close, plotted with respect to the **Top Dead Center (TDC)** and **Bottom Dead Center (BDC)** reference points of the piston. The diagram spans **720° of crank rotation** (two complete revolutions) covering the four strokes: **Suction (Intake), Compression, Power (Expansion/Combustion), and Exhaust**. The valve timing diagram dictates the **breathing efficiency** of an engine — i.e., how much air (or air-fuel mixture) is inducted, how completely exhaust gases are scavenged, and the degree of overlap between intake and exhaust events that promotes volumetric efficiency and residual gas expulsion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Technical Term — Dead Center**: The extreme positions of the piston at the topmost and bottommost points of its stroke. > - **TDC (Top Dead Center)**: Piston is at the highest point in the cylinder. Crank angle = 0° (or 360°, 720°). > - **BDC (Bottom Dead Center)**: Piston is at the lowest point in the cylinder. Crank angle = 180° (or 540°). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Breathing Person" Intuition Think of the engine cylinder as a **human mouth and lungs**: - The **mouth** = the poppet valves (intake and exhaust) - The **lungs** = the cylinder volume - The **throat (trachea)** = the intake and exhaust manifolds When you **inhale**, your mouth opens *slightly before* your chest fully expands (so air starts flowing in early), and your mouth closes *slightly after* your chest fully contracts (to trap the last bit of air). Similarly, when you **exhale**, you open your mouth *slightly before* fully compressing your lungs, and close it *slightly after* completing the exhale. In an engine, valves open **early** and close **late** relative to dead centers: - **IVO (Intake Valve Opens)** ~ 10–20° **before TDC** of intake stroke → analogous to opening mouth slightly before inhaling. - **IVC (Intake Valve Closes)** ~ 40–50° **after BDC** of intake stroke → analogous to closing mouth slightly after full inhalation (ram effect). - **EVO (Exhaust Valve Opens)** ~ 40–50° **before BDC** of exhaust stroke → analogous to opening mouth slightly before exhaling. - **EVC (Exhaust Valve Closes)** ~ 10–20° **after TDC** of exhaust stroke → analogous to closing mouth slightly after completing the exhale. > [!TIP] > The small angular intervals during which **both valves remain open simultaneously** (just after exhaust ends and intake begins) is called **Valve Overlap** — a critical design parameter for scavenging residual gases. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Industrial Defaults) | Parameter | Symbol | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | |-----------|--------|--------------------|--------------------| | Intake Valve Opens | **IVO** | **5°–20° BTDC** | **5°–15° BTDC** | | Intake Valve Closes | **IVC** | **35°–55° ABDC** | **25°–45° ABDC** | | Exhaust Valve Opens | **EVO** | **40°–60° BBDC** | **30°–50° BBDC** | | Exhaust Valve Closes | **EVC** | **5°–15° ATDC** | **5°–15° ATDC** | | Valve Overlap | **θ_o** | **10°–35°** | **10°–30°** | | Injection Advance (CI) | — | N/A | **10°–25° BTDC** | | Ignition Advance (SI) | — | **20°–40° BTDC** | N/A | > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently test whether students can **distinguish timing differences between SI and CI engines**. CI engines have **later intake valve closure** (more reliance on ram air charging in turbocharged applications) and **earlier exhaust valve opening** (to manage high combustion pressures safely). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Operating Events of a 4-Stroke Cycle A four-stroke engine completes its working cycle in **two crank revolutions (720°)**. Each event occupies 180° nominally, but valve events are extended to exploit inertia of gases. **1. Suction Stroke (0° → 180° nominal)** - Starts at TDC. Piston moves down toward BDC. - Intake valve **opens just before TDC** (early opening for less restriction). - As piston descends, fresh charge is drawn in due to the partial vacuum created. - Intake valve **closes just after BDC** (late closing for ram effect at high RPM). **2. Compression Stroke (180° → 360° nominal)** - Piston moves up from BDC to TDC. - **Both valves are fully closed** — the charge is compressed. - In SI engines: compression ratio **8:1 to 12:1**. - In CI engines: compression ratio **14:1 to 22:1**. - Combustion/spark (SI) or fuel injection (CI) occurs **slightly before TDC** to maximize work output. **3. Power (Expansion) Stroke (360° → 540° nominal)** - Combustion gases expand, pushing piston from TDC to BDC. - Near the end of this stroke, **exhaust valve opens before BDC** (early exhaust blowdown to reduce cylinder pressure before piston reverses direction). - This is called **Blowdown** — high-pressure gas rushes out, doing no useful work but reducing pumping losses on the exhaust stroke. **4. Exhaust Stroke (540° → 720° nominal)** - Piston moves from BDC to TDC, pushing out remaining exhaust gases. - Exhaust valve **closes just after TDC** (late closing to allow scavenging inertia of exhaust gases to push out residual mass). ### 2.2 Why the Lead and Lag Angles Exist — The Core "Why" > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Valve Timing Deviations:** - **Early IVO (Lead before TDC)**: At TDC, piston velocity is theoretically zero, so any valve opening at exactly TDC would suffer a sudden pressure drop and create back-flow. Opening the intake valve a few degrees *before* TDC of the suction stroke ensures that by the time the piston actually begins descending, the valve is **fully open and stabilized** — minimizing throttling loss. - **Late IVC (Lag after BDC)**: At BDC, piston velocity momentarily goes to zero. By the time the piston is at BDC, the column of moving intake gas possesses **kinetic energy (ram effect)**. Closing the valve *after* BDC traps this extra mass into the cylinder, increasing **volumetric efficiency** at higher engine speeds. - **Early EVO (Lead before BDC)**: Combustion gases at BDC are still at high pressure (especially in CI engines). Opening the exhaust valve *before* BDC releases this pressure in a **blowdown event**, reducing the work required from the piston on the exhaust stroke. This improves **mechanical efficiency**. - **Late EVC (Lag after TDC)**: Exhaust gases leaving the cylinder have **momentum** in the exhaust manifold. Closing the valve *after* TDC allows this momentum to continue evacuating residual gases, providing **scavenging action**. ### 2.3 The Critical Concept: Valve Overlap **Valve Overlap (θ_o)** is the angular interval during the **TDC transition from exhaust to intake stroke** when *both* the intake valve and exhaust valve are open simultaneously. $$ \theta_{overlap} = (EVC \text{ angle}) + (IVO \text{ angle}) $$ Where: - EVC is measured in degrees **ATDC** (After Top Dead Center of exhaust stroke). - IVO is measured in degrees **BTDC** (Before Top Dead Center of intake stroke). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why overlap matters**: > - **Low overlap (0°–10°)**: Suited to low-speed, low-rpm engines. Minimizes reversion of exhaust gases into intake. > - **High overlap (30°–50°)**: Suited to high-rpm racing engines. Promotes aggressive scavenging using exhaust pulse energy to draw in fresh charge. > - **Excessive overlap**: Causes rough idle, loss of low-end torque, and increased HC/CO emissions in SI engines. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Time per crank degree | $t = \frac{60}{360 \times N} = \frac{1}{6N}$ | seconds | For a 4-stroke, two revs per cycle | | 2 | Crank degrees per second | $\omega_{deg} = 6N$ | deg/s | N = engine RPM | | 3 | Total cycle duration (4-stroke) | $T_{cycle} = \frac{120}{N}$ | seconds | Two revolutions | | 4 | Valve overlap | $\theta_{o} = \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC} + \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC}$ | degrees | Both measured from TDC | | 5 | Effective compression stroke angle | $\theta_{comp} = 180° + \theta_{IVC}^{ABDC} - \theta_{ign}^{BTDC}$ | degrees | Only if spark occurs mid-stroke | | 6 | Mean piston speed | $V_{p} = 2LN \times 10^{-3}$ | m/s | L = stroke length in mm | | 7 | Volumetric efficiency correction | $\eta_{v,eff} = \eta_{v,0} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta_{overlap}}{360}\right)$ | dimensionless | Approximate empirical relation | | 8 | Volumetric efficiency due to late IVC | $\eta_{v,l-IVC} = 1 + \frac{V_{ram}}{V_{disp}}$ | dimensionless | Ram effect gain | | 9 | Ram air velocity | $V_{ram} = L \cdot \omega \cdot \sin\phi_{IVC}$ | m/s | $\phi_{IVC}$ is crank angle at IVC | | 10 | Power lost to blowdown | $W_{blow} = \int P_{cyl} \, dV$ | Joules | Integral over blowdown period | | 11 | Scavenging efficiency | $\eta_{sc} = \frac{m_{air,trapped}}{m_{air,supplied}}$ | dimensionless | Mass-based | | 12 | Ignition advance (SI) | $\theta_{ig} = K \cdot N \cdot P$ | degrees | Empirical K, N, P = RPM, manifold pressure | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Assumption**: All crank angle references in exam problems are taken with **IVC** as degrees **ABDC (After Bottom Dead Center)** and **EVC** as degrees **ATDC (After Top Dead Center)**, unless explicitly stated otherwise. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Performance Tuning**: Race engine builders adjust camshaft profiles to alter valve overlap, achieving peak torque at specific RPM ranges. - **Emissions Control**: Modern engines use **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** systems (e.g., Toyota VVT-i, Honda i-VTEC) to dynamically alter overlap and lift based on operating conditions — a direct evolution of the static timing diagram. - **Turbocharging Compatibility**: CI engines with longer overlap benefit from **pulse turbocharging**, where exhaust pulse energy aids compressor operation. - **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**: Optimal valve timing minimizes pumping losses, directly reducing BSFC at part-load conditions. - **Cold Start Performance**: Excessive overlap at cold start leads to fuel washdown of cylinder walls — a calibration consideration for ECU mapping. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion of Crank Angle to Time of Valve Event **Given**: A 4-stroke petrol engine runs at $N = 3000$ RPM. The exhaust valve remains open from $40°$ BBDC to $10°$ ATDC. Compute the **total duration the exhaust valve is open**, in seconds. #### Step 1: Identify the angular span of the exhaust event The exhaust valve opens at $40°$ **Before BDC** of the power stroke (which is at $540°$ in the 720° cycle). It closes at $10°$ **After TDC** of the exhaust stroke (which is at $720°$). $$ \theta_{open,E} = 40° + 180° + 10° $$ $$ \theta_{open,E} = 230° $$ > **Textual Note**: We add the $180°$ nominal exhaust stroke (BDC to TDC) plus the lead ($40°$ before BDC) plus the lag ($10°$ after TDC) to get the total angular span. #### Step 2: Convert engine speed to degrees per second For a 4-stroke engine, the working cycle spans $720°$ in two crank revolutions. Therefore, the crank rotates at: $$ \theta_{per\,sec} = \frac{N \times 360°}{60} = 6N $$ $$ \theta_{per\,sec} = 6 \times 3000 = 18000 \; \frac{deg}{s} $$ > **Textual Note**: This means the crank sweeps 18000 degrees every second. Each degree therefore takes $1/18000$ seconds. #### Step 3: Calculate the time the exhaust valve is open $$ t_{E,open} = \frac{\theta_{open,E}}{\theta_{per\,sec}} = \frac{230}{18000} $$ $$ t_{E,open} = 0.01278 \; \text{seconds} = 12.78 \; \text{ms} $$ > **Textual Note**: This very brief interval allows the high-pressure combustion gases to evacuate, but the duration is sufficient for inertia-based scavenging. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculation of Valve Overlap **Given**: In a 4-stroke SI engine, the intake valve opens at $12°$ **BTDC** of the suction stroke, and the exhaust valve closes at $8°$ **ATDC** of the exhaust stroke. #### Step 1: Identify reference point Both events are referenced to the **same TDC** (the TDC that separates the exhaust stroke from the suction stroke). The exhaust valve is closing *after* this TDC, and the intake valve is opening *before* this TDC. #### Step 2: Apply the overlap formula $$ \theta_{o} = \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC} + \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} $$ $$ \theta_{o} = 8° + 12° $$ $$ \theta_{o} = 20° $$ > **Textual Note**: This 20° overlap means that for 20° of crank rotation centered on the TDC, both valves are simultaneously open, allowing a brief period of cross-flow that aids scavenging. #### Step 3: Compute the equivalent time at 2500 RPM $$ \theta_{per\,sec} = 6 \times 2500 = 15000 \; \frac{deg}{s} $$ $$ t_{overlap} = \frac{20}{15000} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} $$ $$ t_{overlap} = 1.33 \; \text{ms} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Stroke Length with Late Valve Closure (Ram Effect) **Given**: A 4-stroke petrol engine has bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 90$ mm. IVC occurs at $45°$ ABDC. Calculate the **theoretical additional volume of air trapped** due to the late IVC at 3500 RPM. #### Step 1: Crank angular position of IVC in radians $$ \phi_{IVC} = 180° + 45° = 225° \;\text{(measured from TDC of suction stroke)} $$ $$ \phi_{IVC,rad} = \frac{225 \times \pi}{180} = 3.927 \; \text{rad} $$ > **Textual Note**: The IVC is $180° + 45°$ because BDC is at $180°$ and we add $45°$ past it. #### Step 2: Mean piston velocity and ram air velocity The mean piston speed is: $$ V_{p} = \frac{2L N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 0.090 \times 3500}{60} $$ $$ V_{p} = 10.5 \; \text{m/s} $$ > **Textual Note**: Piston mean speed is the average of its to-and-fro motion, equal to twice stroke length times RPM divided by 60. The ram velocity component at IVC instant: $$ V_{ram} = V_{p} \cdot \sin(\phi_{IVC,rad} - \pi) = 10.5 \cdot \sin(45°) $$ $$ V_{ram} = 10.5 \times 0.7071 = 7.425 \; \text{m/s} $$ > **Textual Note**: At $45°$ past BDC, the piston is moving **upward** in the compression stroke, so any ram velocity from the previous intake must be considered to flow into the cylinder through the still-open valve. #### Step 3: Displaced volume gain due to late IVC The additional volumetric efficiency gain: $$ \Delta\eta_{v} = \frac{V_{ram}}{V_{p}} = \sin(45°) \approx 0.707 $$ > **Textual Note**: This indicates a ~70% theoretical gain in mass of air trapped due to ram effect — in practice, gains are smaller due to frictional and throttling losses. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SI vs CI Timing | Parameter | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) | Engineering Reasoning | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------------| | IVO | 5°–20° BTDC | 5°–15° BTDC | Similar; CI slightly less because diesel cycle uses pure air induction | | IVC | 40°–55° ABDC | 25°–45° ABDC | SI relies more on ram effect for power density | | EVO | 40°–60° BBDC | 30°–50° BBDC | CI opens earlier — higher cylinder pressures at BDC demand faster blowdown | | EVC | 5°–15° ATDC | 5°–15° ATDC | Similar scavenging requirement | | Overlap | 10°–35° | 10°–30° | SI often uses wider overlap for better high-RPM breathing | | Spark/Fuel Timing | Spark 20°–40° BTDC | Injection 10°–25° BTDC | CI uses higher compression auto-ignition; SI relies on controlled spark | | Compression Ratio | 8:1 to 12:1 | 14:1 to 22:1 | Diesel needs high CR for auto-ignition of fuel | | Throttle | Yes (pumping loss) | No (load via fuel) | CI valve timing optimized for full-load operation | | Combustion Initiation | Spark plug | Fuel injection + auto-ignition | Affects valve event scheduling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight**: In a typical exam, students are asked to **draw a timing diagram** and **justify the lead/lag** of each event. Marks are awarded for: (1) accurate angular positions, (2) correct labeling of TDC/BDC, (3) justification of why each valve is early/late, and (4) identifying the overlap region. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem**: A 4-stroke petrol engine has the following valve timing specifications: - IVO = 10° BTDC - IVC = 45° ABDC - EVO = 50° BBDC - EVC = 12° ATDC If the engine runs at $N = 3600$ RPM, determine: **(a)** The valve overlap angle. **(b)** The total angular duration the intake valve is open. **(c)** The total angular duration the exhaust valve is open. **(d)** The time duration (in ms) of the valve overlap. **Solution**: #### Part (a): Valve Overlap $$ \theta_{o} = \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC} + \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} = 12° + 10° = 22° $$ #### Part (b): Intake Valve Open Duration The intake valve opens $10°$ BTDC and closes $45°$ ABDC. Total angular span: $$ \theta_{I,open} = 10° + 180° + 45° = 235° $$ #### Part (c): Exhaust Valve Open Duration The exhaust valve opens $50°$ BBDC and closes $12°$ ATDC. Total angular span: $$ \theta_{E,open} = 50° + 180° + 12° = 242° $$ #### Part (d): Time Duration of Overlap at 3600 RPM $$ \theta_{per\,sec} = 6N = 6 \times 3600 = 21600 \; \frac{deg}{s} $$ $$ t_{overlap} = \frac{22}{21600} = 1.0185 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} $$ $$ t_{overlap} \approx 1.02 \; \text{ms} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Sequential Valve Event Flow (SI Engine) ```mermaid graph TD A[START OF CYCLE - TDC SUCTION]:::startNode B[IVO: Intake Valve Opens 10-20 deg BTDC]:::valveEvent C[PISTON DESCENDS: Suction Stroke 0 to 180 deg]:::piston D[BDC SUCTION REACHED at 180 deg]:::deadCenter E[IVC: Intake Valve Closes 40-55 deg ABDC]:::valveEvent F[BOTH VALVES CLOSED: Compression Stroke 180 to 360 deg]:::compression G[TDC COMPRESSION: Spark fires 20-40 deg BTDC at 360 deg]:::spark H[PISTON DESCENDS: Power Stroke 360 to 540 deg]:::piston I[BDC POWER: EVO at 40-60 deg BBDC Blowdown occurs]:::valveEvent J[PISTON ASCENDS: Exhaust Stroke 540 to 720 deg]:::piston K[TDC EXHAUST: EVC at 5-15 deg ATDC Valve overlap starts]:::valveEvent L[VALVE OVERLAP: Both valves open briefly 10-35 deg]:::overlap M[CYCLE RESTART: New Suction Stroke Begins]:::endNode A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L L --> M M --> A classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef valveEvent fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef deadCenter fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef piston fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef compression fill:#F38181,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef spark fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef overlap fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Crank-Angle Timeline (Horizontal Visualization) ```mermaid graph LR P1["0 deg TDC SUCTION
IVO 10 deg BTDC
Suction begins"]:::tNode P2["90 deg Mid-Suction"]:::tNode P3["180 deg BDC
Suction nominal end"]:::tNode P4["225 deg IVC 45 deg ABDC"]:::valveNode P5["360 deg TDC COMPRESSION
Spark 25 deg BTDC"]:::tNode P6["540 deg BDC POWER
EVO 50 deg BBDC"]:::tNode P7["700 deg EVC 10 deg ATDC"]:::valveNode P8["720 deg TDC EXHAUST
VALVE OVERLAP"]:::overlapNode P9["710 deg New IVO
Overlap continues"]:::valveNode P10["730 deg New cycle continues"]:::tNode P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10 classDef tNode fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef valveNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef overlapNode fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — SI vs CI Engine Timing Comparison (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SI[SI ENGINE PETROL - VALVE TIMING PARAMETERS] SIVO[IVO: 5-20 deg BTDC]:::siParam SIVC[IVC: 40-55 deg ABDC]:::siParam SEVO[EVO: 40-60 deg BBDC]:::siParam SEVC[EVC: 5-15 deg ATDC]:::siParam SSPRK[SPARK: 20-40 deg BTDC]:::siParam SOVER[OVERLAP: 10-35 deg]:::siOverlap end subgraph CI[CI ENGINE DIESEL - VALVE TIMING PARAMETERS] CIVO[IVO: 5-15 deg BTDC]:::ciParam CIVC[IVC: 25-45 deg ABDC]:::ciParam CEVO[EVO: 30-50 deg BBDC]:::ciParam CEVC[EVC: 5-15 deg ATDC]:::ciParam CINJ[INJECTION: 10-25 deg BTDC]:::ciParam COVER[OVERLAP: 10-30 deg]:::ciOverlap end SIVO --- SOVER SEVC --- SOVER CIVO --- COVER CEVC --- COVER classDef siParam fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ciParam fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef siOverlap fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef ciOverlap fill:#A1887F,stroke:#3E2723,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Description of the Actual Circular Timing Diagram > [!NOTE] > **How to draw a valve timing diagram on paper (KTU practical exam):** > > **Construction Steps:** > 1. Draw a **horizontal line** of length 720° representing one complete 4-stroke cycle. > 2. Mark **0°** (TDC suction), **180°** (BDC suction), **360°** (TDC compression), **540°** (BDC power), and **720°** (TDC exhaust). > 3. Draw **two parallel horizontal circles** (or use a circular polar plot) — one for the **intake valve** profile and one for the **exhaust valve** profile. > 4. The intake valve profile is **HIGH (open)** from $10°$ BTDC of suction to $45°$ ABDC. > 5. The exhaust valve profile is **HIGH (open)** from $50°$ BBDC of power stroke to $12°$ ATDC of exhaust. > 6. Mark the **overlap region** at the top of the diagram where both profiles are HIGH simultaneously. > 7. Mark the **ignition/injection point** in the compression stroke (a star symbol). > 8. Indicate the direction of rotation with an **arrow**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Q1. Define valve timing diagram. Why are valves opened early and closed late in a four-stroke IC engine?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A valve timing diagram is a graphical plot showing the exact crank angles at which the intake and exhaust valves of a four-stroke IC engine open and close, referenced to TDC and BDC of the piston. > > Valves are **opened early (lead)** so that they are fully open by the time the piston begins the induction/exhaust stroke, minimizing throttling loss. Valves are **closed late (lag)** to take advantage of the **ram effect** (inertia of incoming air) during intake and **scavenging momentum** of exhaust gases during exhaust, improving volumetric efficiency and scavenging. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for early/late justification)** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **Q2. What is valve overlap? Why is it desirable in a high-speed engine?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Valve overlap is the small angular interval (typically 10°–35° of crank rotation) around the TDC between the exhaust stroke end and the suction stroke start, during which **both the intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously**. > > In a high-speed engine, the **exhaust pulse energy** (kinetic energy of rapidly moving exhaust gases) creates a momentary low pressure in the cylinder. This low pressure actively **draws in fresh charge** through the open intake valve, promoting **scavenging** of residual exhaust gases. This results in higher volumetric efficiency, better breathing, and improved high-RPM power output. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for high-speed advantage)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Module 2]** > ### Question A (14 Marks) > > **(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled valve timing diagram, explain the events occurring in a **4-stroke petrol (SI) engine**. Mention typical angular values for IVO, IVC, EVO, and EVC. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Define valve overlap. A 4-stroke petrol engine runs at 3000 RPM with IVO = 10° BTDC and EVC = 8° ATDC. Calculate (i) valve overlap angle, (ii) the time duration of overlap in milliseconds, and (iii) the crank degrees swept per second. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) — Valve Timing Diagram of SI Engine (7 marks):** > > The four-stroke petrol engine cycle consists of: > > **Suction Stroke (0°–180°):** Piston moves from TDC to BDC. Intake valve opens at **10°–20° BTDC** (early opening to ensure full lift by the time piston descends). Fresh air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder. Intake valve closes at **40°–55° ABDC** (late closure to trap ram-effect air mass). > > **Compression Stroke (180°–360°):** Both valves closed. Piston compresses the mixture. Compression ratio 8:1 to 12:1. **Spark is delivered 20°–40° BTDC** (at ~330°–340° crank angle). > > **Power Stroke (360°–540°):** Combustion pushes piston to BDC. Combustion pressure peaks at ~15–25° ATDC. Exhaust valve opens at **40°–60° BBDC** (blowdown event, ~520°–530°). > > **Exhaust Stroke (540°–720°):** Piston moves up, expelling exhaust. Exhaust valve closes at **5°–15° ATDC** (after TDC at 720°). **(4 marks for diagram + events)** > > At the TDC of exhaust/suction transition (around 720°/0°), the overlap region exists. **(1 mark)** > > Justification of lead/lag: **(2 marks)** > - Early IVO: minimize throttling. > - Late IVC: ram effect for higher mass of charge. > - Early EVO: blowdown reduces pumping loss. > - Late EVC: scavenging inertia empties residual gases. > > **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** > > **(i) Valve Overlap:** > > $$ > \theta_{o} = \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC} + \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} = 8° + 10° = 18° > $$ > > **(1 mark)** > > **(ii) Time Duration of Overlap:** > > Crank degrees per second: > > $$ > \theta_{per\,sec} = 6N = 6 \times 3000 = 18000 \; \frac{deg}{s} > $$ > > **(1 mark)** > > Time of overlap: > > $$ > t_{o} = \frac{18}{18000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} = 1.0 \; \text{ms} > $$ > > **(2 marks)** > > **(iii) Crank Degrees Swept per Second:** > > Already computed: $18000 \; deg/s$ **(1 mark)** > > **Conclusion:** With 1 ms of overlap, the engine achieves effective scavenging. **(2 marks for unit conversion, conclusion, and clarity)** > > --- > > ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > > **(a)** Draw the valve timing diagram of a **4-stroke diesel (CI) engine** and compare it with a 4-stroke petrol engine. List at least three key differences. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 4-stroke diesel engine operates at 2400 RPM. The exhaust valve opens at 50° BBDC and closes at 10° ATDC. Calculate (i) the total angular duration the exhaust valve is open, and (ii) the time in milliseconds. **(7 marks)** > > **Model Solution:** > > **Part (a) — CI Engine Diagram and Comparison (7 marks):** > > **Drawing (4 marks):** Same as SI engine but with these typical CI values: > - IVO = 5°–15° BTDC > - IVC = 25°–45° ABDC > - EVO = 30°–50° BBDC > - EVC = 5°–15° ATDC > - **Fuel Injection Point** marked 10°–25° BTDC > - **No spark plug** — auto-ignition by compression. > > **Three Key Differences from SI Engine (3 marks):** > > | # | Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine | Implication | > |---|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------| > | 1 | Combustion Initiation | Spark plug at 20°–40° BTDC | Fuel injection at 10°–25° BTDC + auto-ignition | Different timing reference | > | 2 | Intake Charge | Air-fuel mixture (homogeneous) | Pure air only | Slightly different IVC optimization | > | 3 | EVO Timing | 40°–60° BBDC | 30°–50° BBDC | CI opens earlier to manage higher cylinder pressure | > | 4 | Compression Ratio | 8:1 to 12:1 | 14:1 to 22:1 | Higher CR affects mechanical stresses and timing | > | 5 | Throttle | Present (load control) | Absent (load by fuel) | CI valve timing tuned for full-load operation | > > **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** > > **(i) Total Angular Duration of EV Open:** > > The exhaust valve is open from $50°$ BBDC of power stroke through the entire $180°$ exhaust stroke to $10°$ ATDC: > > $$ > \theta_{E,open} = 50° + 180° + 10° = 240° > $$ > > **(3 marks)** > > **(ii) Time in Milliseconds:** > > Crank angular speed: > > $$ > \theta_{per\,sec} = 6N = 6 \times 2400 = 14400 \; \frac{deg}{s} > $$ > > **(1 mark)** > > Time: > > $$ > t_{E,open} = \frac{240}{14400} = 0.01667 \; \text{s} = 16.67 \; \text{ms} > $$ > > **(3 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors**: Many students confuse BTDC/ABDC and ATDC/BBDC. KTU specifically deducts **0.5–1 mark** for incorrect sign conventions in overlap calculation. > 2. **Forgetting the 180° nominal stroke**: When calculating total valve open duration, students often forget to add the 180° nominal stroke and only add the lead and lag. **Deduction: 2 marks.** > 3. **Using wrong RPM-to-degree conversion**: For 4-stroke engines, the relation is $\theta_{per\,sec} = 6N$, NOT $360N$. Using $360N$ is a common error worth **1.5 marks** deduction. > 4. **Missing the diagram labels**: A valve timing diagram without clear TDC/BDC markings, valve labels, and direction-of-rotation arrow loses **2 marks** out of the diagram component. > 5. **Ignoring why values differ between SI and CI**: Simply listing values without justification is incomplete. KTU specifically awards marks for the "engineering reasoning" component. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Valve Timing Diagram** - **Definition**: A plot of valve open/close events (in crank degrees) referenced to TDC and BDC of a 4-stroke cycle (720° total). - **Four Major Valve Events**: **IVO**, **IVC**, **EVO**, **EVC** — with leads and lags relative to dead centers. - **Typical SI (Petrol) Values**: IVO = 5°–20° BTDC, IVC = 40°–55° ABDC, EVO = 40°–60° BBDC, EVC = 5°–15° ATDC, Spark = 20°–40° BTDC. - **Typical CI (Diesel) Values**: IVO = 5°–15° BTDC, IVC = 25°–45° ABDC, EVO = 30°–50° BBDC, EVC = 5°–15° ATDC, Injection = 10°–25° BTDC. - **Valve Overlap Formula**: $$ \theta_{o} = \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC} + \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} $$ - **Crank Angular Speed (4-stroke)**: $$ \theta_{per\,sec} = 6N $$ - **Time for Valve Event**: $$ t = \frac{\theta_{event}}{6N} $$ - **Justification Mantras**: - Early IVO → less throttling. - Late IVC → **ram effect**, more air mass. - Early EVO → **blowdown**, less pumping loss. - Late EVC → **scavenging inertia**, cleaner exhaust. - Overlap → residual gas expulsion, better volumetric efficiency at high RPM. - **SI vs CI**: CI opens exhaust earlier (higher pressure blowdown), closes intake earlier (less reliance on ram), uses higher compression ratio and fuel injection instead of spark. - **Key Conversion Reminder**: 4-stroke cycle = $720°$ per cycle = 2 crank revolutions. RPM × 360° gives crank degrees per **minute**; divide by 60 (or multiply RPM by 6) to get degrees per **second**. - **Overlap Sign Convention**: Both events are on the same side of the TDC — EVC is *after* TDC and IVO is *before* TDC — so we **add** them to get the total overlap angle. - **Modern Extension**: Static timing diagrams have evolved into **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** and **Cam Phasing** systems in modern engines — but the fundamental concepts remain identical for KTU exam purposes. - **One-Line Summary**: *"Open early, close late, overlap briefly at TDC, scavenge aggressively, and breathe efficiently."* ---

topics.title: Port Timing Diagram of a 2-Stroke Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Port Timing Diagram of a 2-Stroke Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Port Timing Diagram (PTD)** of a 2-stroke Internal Combustion (IC) engine is a polar/sector diagram that graphically depicts the angular positions of the engine crank at which the various ports — namely the **Intake Port (IP)**, **Exhaust Port (EP)**, and **Transfer Port (TP)** — open and close. The diagram is plotted with the **crank angle (measured in degrees)** along the angular axis and the **port lift (in mm)** along the radial axis, referenced against the Top Dead Center (TDC) of the piston.

In simpler academic phrasing adopted by the KTU board:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port Timing Diagram Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *"It is a graphical representation of the opening and closing instants of the inlet, exhaust, and transfer ports of a 2-stroke spark ignition or compression ignition engine, expressed as a function of crank angle measured from the Top Dead Center (TDC) position of the piston."*

The diagram is constructed from experimental observations taken using a **degree disc (protractor plate)** mounted on the crankshaft and a **stationary pointer** fixed to the engine body. As the crank rotates, the instants when each port just begins to uncover and just fully closes are recorded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy shopping mall with three sets of automatic sliding doors — **Customer Entry Doors**, **Staff Exit Doors**, and **Service Corridor Doors**. Each door opens and closes at a specific time relative to the mall's central clock. If you plotted the open/closed status of all three doors against time, you would get a "Door Timing Diagram" showing overlapping open intervals, brief simultaneous openings, and small delays.

Now replace the mall with the engine cylinder, the doors with ports, and the clock with the **crank angle (rotating continuously)**. The Port Timing Diagram tells us *when each port opens, how long it stays open, and how these intervals overlap to admit fresh charge and expel burnt gases — all in one revolution of the crankshaft*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it drawn as a polar diagram (rather than a strip chart)?**
> Because the crank rotates continuously and the ports are uncovered by the piston skirt as it moves up and down, the most intuitive representation is to **unroll the circular motion into a linear crank-angle axis (0° → 360°)**, with the port-lift plotted vertically. This is the universally adopted KTU laboratory convention.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Markers

| Marker | Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Top Dead Center | **TDC** | Piston at highest position; crank at 0° (reference) |
| Bottom Dead Center | **BDC** | Piston at lowest position; crank at 180° |
| Stroke Length | **L** | Distance between TDC and BDC |
| Port Lift | **h** | Vertical distance the piston skirt has moved past the port crown |

The **standard ambient pressure** for port timing experiments is taken as **P₀ = 1.01325 bar**, and the **standard atmospheric temperature** is **T₀ = 288.15 K (15 °C)**, although these are not used directly in the timing diagram itself.

---

## 1.4 Brief Introduction to the Experiment

In the KTU 2024 scheme lab curriculum (PCMUL508, Module Experiment 2), the student is expected to:

1. Identify the engine (2-stroke SI or CI) and its specifications.
2. Mount the degree disc on the crankshaft pulley.
3. Mount the stationary pointer on a non-rotating part of the engine.
4. Slowly rotate the crank by hand (or use a slow-speed drive) and mark:
   - The crank angle where each port **opens** (piston skirt just uncovers the port).
   - The crank angle where each port **closes** (piston skirt just covers the port).
5. Measure port lift at intermediate crank angles (typically every 15°).
6. Plot the polar/sector diagram on graph sheet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Tip:** When asked *"What does the Port Timing Diagram indicate?"* always answer with reference to the **degree crank angles** at which the ports open and close. Never answer in seconds — the diagram is **angular**, not temporal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How the 2-Stroke Engine Cycle Works

A 2-stroke engine completes one **working cycle** in **one revolution of the crankshaft (360°)**. This is achieved by using ports in the cylinder wall (instead of valves as in 4-stroke engines), which are uncovered and covered by the piston as it reciprocates.

The complete cycle, broken into 6 sequential phases along the crank-angle axis:

1. **Compression Stroke (TDC → ~70° before BDC):** Piston moves up from BDC. All ports are covered. The air-fuel mixture (in SI) or air (in CI) is compressed.
2. **Ignition / Combustion:** Just before TDC, combustion begins. Peak pressure is reached shortly after TDC.
3. **Power / Expansion Stroke (TDC → BDC):** High-pressure gases push the piston down.
4. **Exhaust Port Opening (EPO):** At a few degrees before BDC (~70° to 80° before BDC), the descending piston uncovers the **exhaust port**. Burnt gases rush out due to high cylinder pressure — this is called **blowdown**.
5. **Transfer Port Opening (TPO):** A few degrees later (~10° to 20° before BDC), the **transfer port** opens. Fresh charge from the crankcase is pushed in by the rising piston on the underside.
6. **Scavenging Phase (EPO + TPO overlap):** The simultaneous opening of EP and TP causes fresh charge to push out remaining exhaust gases — this is called **scavenging** (cross, loop, or uniflow type).

At BDC (180°), the piston reverses direction. As it moves up, it first **closes the transfer port** and then **closes the exhaust port**, ending the scavenging phase.

## 2.2 Why "Port Timing" Matters

The efficiency of scavenging (the percentage of fresh charge retained in the cylinder) depends critically on:

- **When** the exhaust port opens (too early = loss of expansion work; too late = poor blowdown).
- **When** the transfer port opens (must overlap EP for effective scavenging).
- **Duration** of port overlap (in crank degrees).
- **Symmetry** of port opening about BDC (ensures uniform flow).

This is why the **Port Timing Diagram is the most fundamental performance indicator** for any 2-stroke engine.

## 2.3 Typical KTU Reference Values (2-Stroke Petrol Engine — SI)

| Event | Symbol | Typical Angle (BTDC or ATDC, ref. TDC) |
|-------|--------|-----------------------------------------|
| Exhaust Port Opens | **EPO** | 70° – 80° **BTDC** (i.e., 100° – 110° ATDC before reaching BDC) |
| Transfer Port Opens | **TPO** | 50° – 60° BTDC of BDC stroke (i.e., 120° – 130° ATDC) |
| Transfer Port Closes | **TPC** | 50° – 60° ABDC (after BDC, piston moving up) |
| Exhaust Port Closes | **EPC** | 60° – 70° ABDC |
| Scavenging Period | **EPO → TPC** | ~130° – 140° of crank rotation |

> [!NOTE]
> **BTDC = Before Top Dead Center**, **ATDC = After Top Dead Center**, **BBDC = Before Bottom Dead Center**, **ABDC = After Bottom Dead Center**. Examiners expect students to use these abbreviations correctly.

## 2.4 Typical KTU Reference Values (2-Stroke Diesel / CI Engine)

| Event | Symbol | Typical Angle |
|-------|--------|---------------|
| Exhaust Port Opens | EPO | 60° – 70° BTDC of BDC |
| Scavenging Air Port Opens | TPO | 40° – 50° BTDC of BDC |
| Scavenging Air Port Closes | TPC | 40° – 50° ABDC |
| Exhaust Port Closes | EPC | 50° – 60° ABDC |

CI engines have smaller port overlap because they use a separate scavenging blower (Roots or piston-type).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **only formulas** required by KTU 2024 for solving port-timing problems in the lab exam.

### Table 2.1 — Core Equations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Crank Angle (linear to angular) | $\theta = \dfrac{s}{r} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | where $s$ is piston displacement from TDC, $r$ is crank radius |
| 2 | Piston displacement | $s = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2L}\sin^{2}\theta$ | Approximate: $s \approx r(1 - \cos\theta)$ for long strokes |
| 3 | Port Lift | $h = (r + L/2) - (r\cos\theta + \sqrt{L^{2} - r^{2}\sin^{2}\theta})$ | Exact kinematic relation |
| 4 | Effective Stroke | $L_{eff} = L - h_{max}$ | Stroke available for compression / expansion |
| 5 | Scavenging Period (degrees) | $\theta_{scav} = (EPO_{BTDC}) + (EPC_{ABDC})$ | Total crank degrees of EP open |
| 6 | Port Overlap | $\theta_{overlap} = EPO_{angle} - TPC_{angle}$ | When both EP and TP are open |
| 7 | Crank speed to time | $t = \dfrac{\theta}{6N}$ | Time (s) for crank angle $\theta$ at $N$ rpm |
| 8 | Mean Piston Speed | $\bar{U}_{p} = \dfrac{2LN}{60}$ | m/s; useful for port velocity checks |
