| 9 | Scavenging Ratio (related concept) | $\lambda_{sc} = \dfrac{m_{air,delivered}}{m_{air,trapped}}$ | Dimensionless ratio; not a timing formula but often co-tested |
| 10 | Blowdown Time | $t_{bd} = \dfrac{\theta_{EPO}}{6N}$ | Time available for exhaust blowdown |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mandate:** Every lab answer script must include the **relevant formula before substituting numerical values**. Marks are explicitly allocated (typically 1–2 marks out of 14) for this.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Port timing data is used by:

- **Engine designers** (Bajaj, TVS, Royal Enfield) to optimize scavenging and minimize hydrocarbon emissions from 2-stroke motorcycles.
- **Emission control engineers** to design **direct injection** systems that shift scavenging to stratified mode.
- **Marine engineers** for large 2-stroke marine diesel engines (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä) where scavenging quality determines fuel efficiency.
- **Go-kart / racing teams** to tune port timing for maximum volumetric efficiency at specific RPM bands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Port Lift vs Crank Angle (Approximate Kinematic Relation)

We derive the relationship between the port lift (h) — i.e., the vertical distance by which the piston skirt has descended below the port crown — and the crank angle (θ).

### Step 1 — Set Up the Crank-Slider Geometry

Consider a slider-crank mechanism with:
- Crank radius = $r$ (half of stroke length $L$, so $L = 2r$)
- Connecting rod length = $L_c$
- Crank angle from TDC = $\theta$

The piston position from TDC is given by the standard slider-crank equation:

$$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2L_c}\sin^{2}\theta$$

### Step 2 — Define Port Crown Location

The exhaust/transfer port crown is located at a fixed distance $x_0$ below the cylinder head. The piston is at TDC when $x = 0$. The port just begins to open when the piston skirt has descended to position $x_0$:

$$r(1 - \cos\theta_{open}) + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2L_c}\sin^{2}\theta_{open} = x_0$$

### Step 3 — Define Port Lift

Once the port opens, the port lift $h$ at any crank angle $\theta$ (where $\theta \geq \theta_{open}$) is:

$$h = x - x_0 = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^{2}}{2L_c}\sin^{2}\theta - x_0$$

### Step 4 — Approximate for Long Connecting Rods (KTU Lab Assumption)

In most lab engines, $L_c / r \geq 4$, so the second-order term is small. The KTU-accepted approximate formula is:

$$h \approx r(1 - \cos\theta) - x_0 \quad \text{for } \theta \geq \theta_{open}$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Port Lift

Maximum lift occurs at $\theta = 180°$ (BDC):

$$h_{max} = 2r - x_0 = L - x_0$$

This is used in the lab to verify the experimentally observed lift against the geometric prediction.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Given:** Crank radius $r = 50$ mm, port crown distance from TDC $x_0 = 40$ mm. Find the port lift when $\theta = 135°$.

**Step (i):** Compute $r(1 - \cos 135°)$:

$$\cos 135° = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = -0.7071$$

$$r(1 - \cos\theta) = 50 \times (1 - (-0.7071)) = 50 \times 1.7071 = 85.355 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (ii):** Subtract $x_0$:

$$h = 85.355 - 40 = 45.355 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (iii):** Express result:

$$h \approx 45.36 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity check:** Since $h > 0$, the port is open at $\theta = 135°$, which is consistent (it lies between typical EPO ≈ 110° and EPC ≈ 240° for a 2-stroke SI engine).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Compression Stroke

The **effective compression stroke** is the piston travel during which all ports are closed and the charge is being compressed. It is less than the geometric stroke because the piston must travel from TDC to the highest port-closing event.

### Step 1 — Identify Latest Closing Port

The latest closing port is the **exhaust port (EPC)** if the engine is a petrol 2-stroke, or the **transfer port** in many CI engines. Let the latest closing event occur at crank angle $\theta_{lc}$ after BDC.

### Step 2 — Compute the Effective Compression Distance

The piston position at angle $\theta_{lc}$ ABDC is:

$$x_{lc} = r(1 - \cos(180° + \theta_{lc})) = r(1 + \cos\theta_{lc})$$

The remaining upward travel to TDC is:

$$x_{lc} = r(1 + \cos\theta_{lc})$$

So the effective stroke available for compression is from $x_{lc}$ up to TDC, which is just $x_{lc}$ itself.

### Step 3 — Compression Ratio Adjustment

The geometric compression ratio is:

$$r_{c,geo} = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$$

The effective compression ratio (accounting for port timing) is:

$$r_{c,eff} = \dfrac{V_{lc} + V_s}{V_{lc}}$$

where $V_{lc}$ is the clearance volume plus the volume corresponding to position $x_{lc}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Port Overlap Duration

Port overlap is the crank angle (in degrees) during which **both the exhaust and transfer ports are simultaneously open**. It determines the scavenging effectiveness.

### Step 1 — Define Open Intervals

Let:
- Exhaust port open interval: $[\theta_{EPO},\ \theta_{EPC}]$
- Transfer port open interval: $[\theta_{TPO},\ \theta_{TPC}]$

(Both measured from TDC, with $0° \leq \theta \leq 360°$.)

### Step 2 — Compute Overlap

The overlap is the intersection of these two intervals:

$$\theta_{overlap} = \min(\theta_{EPC},\ \theta_{TPC}) - \max(\theta_{EPO},\ \theta_{TPO})$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example

For a typical 2-stroke SI engine with:
- EPO = 110° ATDC, EPC = 250° ATDC
- TPO = 130° ATDC, TPC = 230° ATDC

Then:

$$\theta_{overlap} = \min(250°, 230°) - \max(110°, 130°) = 230° - 130° = 100°$$

So the ports are simultaneously open for **100° of crank rotation**, which corresponds at 3000 rpm to:

$$t_{overlap} = \dfrac{100°}{6 \times 3000} = \dfrac{100}{18000} = 0.00556 \text{ s} \approx 5.56 \text{ ms}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Checks for Lab Errors

The following table helps students identify common errors in lab data collection:

| Observation | Likely Cause | Correction |
|-------------|--------------|------------|
| EPO reading > 90° BTDC of BDC | Pointer slipping on degree disc | Tighten pointer, redo run |
| TP closes before EP closes | Wrong identification of ports | Re-label ports; refer to engine manual |
| Negative port lift recorded | Crank rotated in wrong direction | Reverse rotation direction |
| All readings cluster near BDC | Disc not aligned with TDC | Re-set TDC reference using stop at piston |
| Symmetric diagram but EP closes before TP | Measurement tolerance (±5°) | Acceptable if within tolerance; report |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Port Timing Experiment

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **experimental procedure and data flow** for obtaining the Port Timing Diagram. Because physical engine drawings are not feasible in Mermaid, this block diagram conveys the functional sequence and data dependencies used in the KTU lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Step 1: Engine Selection"] --> nodeB["Step 2: Mount Degree Disc on Crankshaft Pulley"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3: Mount Stationary Pointer on Engine Body"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4: Identify TDC Reference Position"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5: Slowly Rotate Crank"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6: Note Port Opening Angle EPO"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Step 6: Note Port Opening Angle TPO"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Step 6: Note Port Closing Angle EPC"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Step 6: Note Port Closing Angle TPC"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Step 7: Tabulate Crank Angle vs Port Lift"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Step 8: Plot Port Timing Diagram on Polar Graph"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Step 9: Identify Scavenging and Overlap Periods"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Step 10: Compute Effective Stroke and Compression Ratio"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anatomy of a 2-Stroke Engine Ports

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Crankcase Volume"] --> nodeB["Transfer Port TP"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Cylinder Combustion Chamber"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Exhaust Port EP"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Atmosphere"]
    nodeF["Carburetor / Inlet Manifold"] --> nodeA
    nodeG["Spark Plug or Fuel Injector"] --> nodeC
    nodeH["Piston Reciprocates"] -. controls .-> nodeB
    nodeH -. controls .-> nodeD
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Typical Port Timing Curve (Numerical Sketch)

The following flowchart shows the **sequence of events along the crank angle axis** (representing the layout of the diagram the student must plot):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TDC 0 degrees"] --> nodeB["Compression Stroke All Ports Closed"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Combustion Begins"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Expansion Stroke"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["EPO Opens approx 110 deg"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TPO Opens approx 130 deg"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["BDC 180 degrees Maximum Lift"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["TPC Closes approx 230 deg"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["EPC Closes approx 250 deg"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Compression Stroke Resumes All Ports Closed"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["360 degrees Next Cycle"]
```

## 4.4 Description of the Final Plot (What the Examiner Expects)

When the student plots the diagram, the X-axis (crank angle) runs from **0° (TDC) → 180° (BDC) → 360° (TDC)**, and the Y-axis shows **port lift in mm**. Three curves appear:

1. **Exhaust port curve:** Symmetric hump centered at BDC, opening around 110° ATDC and closing around 250° ATDC.
2. **Transfer port curve:** Slightly narrower hump, opening around 130° ATDC and closing around 230° ATDC.
3. **Scavenging region:** The shaded overlap of EP and TP from ~130° to ~230°.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**Define Port Timing Diagram. Why is it plotted on a crank-angle basis and not on a time basis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A Port Timing Diagram is a graphical plot showing the instants (in degrees of crank rotation) at which the inlet, exhaust, and transfer ports of a 2-stroke IC engine open and close, along with their lifts.
- (1 mark) It is plotted on a crank-angle basis because the crank rotates at variable speed during the cycle, so time would not give a universal, engine-independent representation; crank angle is the natural independent variable.
- (1 mark) Additionally, the same engine running at different speeds will have different times but identical crank angles, making the crank-angle plot reproducible across all operating conditions.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]
**What is the significance of port overlap in a 2-stroke engine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Port overlap is the crank-angle interval during which the exhaust and transfer ports are simultaneously open.
- (1 mark) During this overlap, fresh charge from the transfer port pushes the remaining burnt gases out through the exhaust port — this is the **scavenging** process.
- (1 mark) The duration of overlap directly determines scavenging efficiency: too short = poor scavenging; too long = loss of fresh charge through the exhaust.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**A two-stroke single-cylinder petrol engine has the following port timing data observed experimentally:**
- Exhaust Port Opens (EPO): 110° ATDC
- Transfer Port Opens (TPO): 130° ATDC
- Transfer Port Closes (TPC): 230° ATDC
- Exhaust Port Closes (EPC): 250° ATDC
- Engine speed: 3000 rpm
- Stroke length: 80 mm
- Port crown distance from TDC: 35 mm

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**Plot the Port Timing Diagram on a graph sheet and label:**
(i) The scavenging period
(ii) The port overlap period
(iii) The effective compression stroke

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Calculate:**
(i) The port overlap duration in degrees and in seconds.
(ii) The maximum port lift at BDC.
(iii) The effective stroke length.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(Plot description — 7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for axes, 2 marks for EP curve, 2 marks for TP curve, 1 mark for labels)*

- X-axis: Crank angle from 0° to 360° (TDC marked at 0° and 360°; BDC at 180°).
- Y-axis: Port lift in mm, from 0 to maximum.
- **Exhaust port curve:** Symmetric bell curve, starts opening at 110°, peaks at 180° (BDC), closes at 250°.
- **Transfer port curve:** Narrower bell curve, starts opening at 130°, peaks at 180°, closes at 230°.
- Label the shaded overlap region between 130° and 230° as **scavenging period**.
- Label the shaded region between 130° and 230° as **port overlap**.
- Mark the segment from ~250° to 360° as **effective compression stroke**.

**Part (b):** *(Numerical solution)*

**(i) Port overlap in degrees and seconds:**

$$\theta_{overlap} = \min(EPC, TPC) - \max(EPO, TPO) = 230° - 130° = 100°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{overlap} = 100°}$$

Time for 100° at 3000 rpm:

$$t_{overlap} = \dfrac{\theta}{6N} = \dfrac{100°}{6 \times 3000} = \dfrac{100}{18000} = 0.00556 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{overlap} = 5.56 \text{ ms}}$$

**(ii) Maximum port lift at BDC:**

At BDC, $\theta = 180°$, so $\cos 180° = -1$:

$$h_{max} = r(1 - \cos 180°) - x_0 = 40 \times (1 - (-1)) - 35 = 80 - 35 = 45 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h_{max} = 45 \text{ mm}}$$

**(iii) Effective stroke length:**

The latest closing event is EPC at 250° ATDC. The piston position at 250° ATDC from BDC is:

$$\theta_{lc} = 250° - 180° = 70° \text{ ABDC}$$

$$x_{lc} = r(1 + \cos 70°) = 40 \times (1 + 0.342) = 40 \times 1.342 = 53.68 \text{ mm}$$

The effective compression stroke is from TDC down to this point on the upward stroke, so:

$$L_{eff} = x_{lc} = 53.68 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L_{eff} \approx 53.7 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Many students write the opening and closing angles in seconds instead of degrees. The Port Timing Diagram is **always plotted on crank-angle basis** — using seconds will result in full marks deduction (–7 marks loss). Also, students often forget to **label the scavenging region explicitly** on the diagram — at least 1 mark is reserved for correct labeling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**For a 2-stroke CI engine used in the KTU lab, the following readings were obtained from the degree disc during slow cranking:**

| Crank Angle (° from TDC) | Exhaust Port Lift (mm) | Transfer Port Lift (mm) |
|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| 90 | 0 | 0 |
| 105 | 0 | 0 |
| 120 | 8 | 0 |
| 135 | 22 | 5 |
| 150 | 33 | 14 |
| 165 | 41 | 22 |
| 180 | 45 | 28 |
| 195 | 41 | 22 |
| 210 | 33 | 14 |
| 225 | 22 | 0 |
| 240 | 8 | 0 |
| 255 | 0 | 0 |

#### Part (a) — 7 marks
**(i) Determine the crank angles at which the exhaust and transfer ports open and close.**
**(ii) Identify the type of scavenging used and justify.**

#### Part (b) — 7 marks
**Calculate:**
**(i) The scavenging period in crank degrees.**
**(ii) The mean piston speed if the bore is 75 mm and the engine speed is 2500 rpm.**
**(iii) The crank angle (in degrees and seconds) at which the maximum transfer port lift occurs.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Port opening and closing angles (read from table):**

- Exhaust Port Opens (EPO): The first non-zero lift (8 mm) is at **120° ATDC**. The first zero lift before it is at 105°. Therefore, **EPO ≈ 110° ATDC** (interpolated).
- Exhaust Port Closes (EPC): The last non-zero lift (8 mm) is at **240° ATDC**. The next reading is 255° (zero). Therefore, **EPC ≈ 245° ATDC** (interpolated).
- Transfer Port Opens (TPO): First non-zero lift (5 mm) at **135° ATDC**. Therefore, **TPO ≈ 130° ATDC**.
- Transfer Port Closes (TPC): Last non-zero lift (14 mm) at **210° ATDC**. Next zero at 225°. Therefore, **TPC ≈ 220° ATDC**.

**(ii) Type of scavenging:** Since the transfer port opens after the exhaust port and closes before it (TPO > EPO, TPC < EPC), this is **cross-flow scavenging** (or possibly loop scavenging — both have EP downstream of TP). The dominant flow is from transfer port → across cylinder → exhaust port. Hence: **Cross-flow scavenging.**

> **Justification:** The asymmetry of port heights (transfer port lower than exhaust port crown) and the fact that transfer opens after exhaust indicates fresh charge is forced across the cylinder to push out exhaust gases — this is the defining feature of cross-flow scavenging.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Scavenging period:**

$$\theta_{scav} = (EPC - EPO)_{degrees} = 245° - 110° = 135°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{scav} = 135°}$$

Alternatively, using TPC − TPO:

$$\theta_{scav,alt} = 220° - 130° = 90°$$

The total scavenging period (when any port is open) is from EPO to EPC = **135°**.

**(ii) Mean piston speed:**

$$\bar{U}_p = \dfrac{2LN}{60}$$

With L = 80 mm = 0.080 m (stroke is L, NOT bore!) and N = 2500 rpm:

$$\bar{U}_p = \dfrac{2 \times 0.080 \times 2500}{60} = \dfrac{400}{60} = 6.667 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{U}_p \approx 6.67 \text{ m/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction note:** The bore (75 mm) is given as a **distractor** in this question. Mean piston speed depends on stroke length, not bore. Always use $L$ (stroke) in the formula. KTU examiners sometimes include irrelevant data to test formula understanding.

**(iii) Maximum transfer port lift angle:**

From the table, the maximum transfer port lift of **28 mm occurs at 180°** (BDC). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta_{max,TP} = 180° \text{ ATDC (i.e., BDC)}}$$

In seconds at 2500 rpm:

$$t = \dfrac{180°}{6 \times 2500} = \dfrac{180}{15000} = 0.012 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{max,TP} = 12 \text{ ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Two most common mistakes students make:
> 1. **Confusing bore and stroke** when computing mean piston speed — bore is irrelevant for $\bar{U}_p$; stroke is what matters. Marks lost: 2–3.
> 2. **Failing to interpolate** between the first non-zero and last zero readings for port opening/closing — examiners expect an interpolated value, not just the index angle. Marks lost: 1–2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Definition:** Port Timing Diagram = graph of port lift vs crank angle for a 2-stroke engine.
- **Independent variable:** Crank angle (θ) in degrees — NOT time.
- **Reference:** TDC = 0° and 360°; BDC = 180°.
- **Three ports to identify:** Intake (I), Exhaust (EP), Transfer (TP).
- **Typical 2-stroke SI petrol engine values:**
  - EPO ≈ 70°–80° BTDC of BDC (i.e., 100°–110° ATDC)
  - TPO ≈ 50°–60° BTDC of BDC (i.e., 120°–130° ATDC)
  - TPC ≈ 50°–60° ABDC (i.e., 230°–240° ATDC)
  - EPC ≈ 60°–70° ABDC (i.e., 250°–260° ATDC)
- **Scavenging period:** EPO to EPC (typically 130°–150°).
- **Port overlap:** $\theta_{overlap} = \min(EPC, TPC) - \max(EPO, TPO)$.
- **Time formula:** $t = \dfrac{\theta}{6N}$ seconds.
- **Mean piston speed:** $\bar{U}_p = \dfrac{2LN}{60}$ m/s (L = stroke in meters, N = rpm).
- **Effective stroke:** $L_{eff} = r(1 + \cos\theta_{lc})$ where $\theta_{lc}$ is angle from BDC of latest closing port.
- **Maximum port lift:** at BDC, $h_{max} = L - x_0$.
- **Apparatus:** Degree disc + stationary pointer + slow-crank mechanism.
- **Procedure:** Rotate slowly by hand, note angles at each port event, measure lift every 15°.
- **Plot:** X-axis = crank angle (0° → 360°); Y-axis = lift (mm); mark TDC and BDC; shade scavenging and overlap regions.
- **Three types of scavenging:** Cross-flow, Loop, Uniflow — each identifiable from the relative positions of EP and TP.
- **Key abbreviations:** TDC, BDC, BTDC, ATDC, BBDC, ABDC, EPO, EPC, TPO, TPC.
- **Units:** Crank angle in degrees (°), lift in mm, speed in rpm, time in seconds (s), piston speed in m/s.
- **Examiner pet peeves to avoid:**
  - Writing port events in seconds instead of degrees.
  - Confusing bore with stroke.
  - Forgetting to label TDC and BDC on the plotted diagram.
  - Not including the relevant formula before substitution.
  - Mis-identifying the scavenging type.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine** is a standardised laboratory experimental procedure conducted on a single-cylinder (or multi-cylinder), water-cooled, four-stroke compression ignition (CI) engine test rig to determine, quantify, and graphically represent the engine's operational behaviour under varying mechanical loads at a constant rated speed.

The test evaluates the engine's ability to convert the chemical energy of diesel fuel into useful **Brake Power (BP)** at the output shaft while simultaneously quantifying the various energy losses through exhaust gases, cooling water, and friction. The performance is benchmarked using parameters such as **Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$)**, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**, **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)**, and the **Heat Balance Sheet**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A performance test is a *steady-state* experiment. All observations are taken only after the engine has reached a thermally stable operating condition. The student is expected to record the data, tabulate it, perform calculations, plot performance curves, and interpret the results in the context of the KTU Course Outcome **CO2: "Apply thermodynamic principles to evaluate I.C. engine performance through experimentation."**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Engine as a Worker on a Treadmill"**

Imagine hiring a person (the **engine**) to do physical work (lifting weights on a **treadmill/dynamometer**). You want to know three things about this worker:

1. **How hard can they work?** (Brake Power — the actual work they deliver to the weight)
2. **How efficiently do they eat?** (Thermal Efficiency — how much useful work you get per litre of fuel consumed)
3. **How much do they waste?** (Heat lost through "sweat" = cooling water, "breath" = exhaust gas, and "friction" = mechanical losses)

To test them, you gradually add more weight (increase the **load**) and observe how their performance metrics change. The "Performance Test" is exactly this — we systematically load a diesel engine and measure how it responds.

| Engine Component | Human Analogy | Measurement Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel Tank + Burette | Food Intake | Burette (cm³/min) |
| Orifice Meter + U-tube Manometer | Oxygen Intake (Breath) | Manometer (mm of water) |
| Dynamometer | Weight Lifting (Treadmill) | Spring Balance + Rope |
| Cooling Water Flow | Sweat | Rotameter (LPM) |
| Tachometer | Heart Rate (RPM) | Tachometer (RPM) |
| Exhaust Gas | Breath Temperature | Thermometer (°C) |

### 1.3 Key Standardised Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **bold** constants and metrics are used universally in the KTU Board lab examination:

- **Calorific Value (CV) of Diesel:** Approximately **42,000 kJ/kg** (standard value used in KTU exams unless otherwise specified).
- **Density of Diesel ($\rho_f$):** **0.86 kg/litre** or **860 kg/m³**.
- **Density of Water ($\rho_w$):** **1000 kg/m³**.
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**.
- **Discharge Coefficient of Orifice ($C_d$):** **0.6 to 0.65** (a typical value is **0.62**).
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa**.
- **Working Stroke of Engine:** **2 crank revolutions** for one complete working cycle (4-stroke).
- **Units used in the KTU Board Answer Book:** BP in **kW**, Fuel in **kg/hr**, Air in **kg/hr**, SFC in **kg/kWh**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stroke Diesel Engine Operating Cycle — Step-by-Step

Unlike a petrol (SI) engine that uses a spark plug, a **4-Stroke Diesel Engine** relies solely on the heat of compression to ignite the fuel. One complete working cycle is completed in **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft, comprising four distinct strokes:

| Stroke | Piston Motion | Valve Status | Process Description | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Suction Stroke** | Top Dead Centre (TDC) → Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) | Inlet Valve (IV) OPEN, Exhaust Valve (EV) CLOSED | Pure air is drawn into the cylinder. **No fuel is inducted.** | **Charging Process** |
| **2. Compression Stroke** | BDC → TDC | Both Valves CLOSED | Air is compressed to a high pressure (~35–40 bar) and temperature (~700–900 K). | **Combustion Preparation** |
| **3. Power (Expansion) Stroke** | TDC → BDC | Both Valves CLOSED initially | Near TDC, diesel is injected; it auto-ignites due to high temperature. High-pressure gases expand and push the piston down. | **Combustion + Expansion** |
| **4. Exhaust Stroke** | BDC → TDC | EV OPEN, IV CLOSED | Burnt gases are pushed out of the cylinder. | **Scavenging Process** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" matters (KTU Board Insight):** A 4-stroke engine produces **one power stroke per two revolutions**. This means the number of power strokes per minute is $\frac{N}{2}$, where $N$ is the crank RPM. This is the **fundamental reason** behind the appearance of the factor 2 (or equivalently, the division by 2) in the IP formula derived from the indicator diagram.

### 2.2 Measurement Philosophy — What Do We Measure and Why?

A performance test requires the measurement of **four fundamental quantities**:

1. **Speed (N):** Measured using a **Tachometer** (digital or analog). *Why?* Power output is directly proportional to speed.
2. **Load (W, W₀):** Measured using a **Dynamometer**. *Why?* The useful output of the engine is mechanical brake power.
3. **Fuel Consumption (V_f, t):** Measured using a **Burette + Stopwatch**. *Why?* Input energy = mass of fuel × calorific value.
4. **Air Consumption (h, T_a):** Measured using an **Orifice Plate + U-tube Manometer**. *Why?* Air-fuel ratio is critical for combustion analysis and to find the indicated power via fuel-air energy balance.

Additionally, the **cooling water inlet/outlet temperature** and **exhaust gas temperature** are measured to construct the **Heat Balance Sheet**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU Board viva and written exam. Memorise the unit of every formula — examiners routinely deduct marks for unit errors.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units (KTU Standard) | Meaning / Why It Is Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fuel Consumption (m_f)** | $m_f = \dfrac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t} \times 3600$ | **kg/hr** | $V_f$ = burette volume (mL), $t$ = time (s). Converts mL/s to kg/hr. |
| 2 | **Air Velocity through Orifice (C_a)** | $C_a = C_d \sqrt{2 g h \dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a}}$ | **m/s** | $h$ = manometer head (m of water), $\rho_a$ = air density (~1.2 kg/m³). |
| 3 | **Air Consumption (m_a)** | $m_a = C_d \cdot a \cdot C_a \times 3600 \times \rho_a$ | **kg/hr** | $a$ = orifice area (m²). The factor 3600 converts kg/s to kg/hr. |
| 4 | **Brake Power (BP)** | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - W_0)}{60 \times 1000}$ | **kW** | $W$ = loaded scale reading, $W_0$ = no-load scale reading, both in **kgf**. $(W - W_0) \times 9.81$ = Net force in N. |
| 5 | **Equivalent Brake Power (BP_eq)** | $BP = \dfrac{(W - W_0) \times S \times N}{60 \times 1000}$ | **kW** | $S$ = spring constant / scale factor. |
| 6 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$)** | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | **%** | Compares actual output energy to input fuel energy. |
| 7 | **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | **kg/kWh** | Lower SFC = Better engine. |
| 8 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{2 \times 60}$ | **kW** | $P_m$ = mean effective pressure (bar), $K$ = no. of cylinders. Factor **2** appears because of 4-stroke. |
| 9 | **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | **%** | Friction and windage losses = IP − BP. |
| 10 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{ith}$)** | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | **%** | Real efficiency of the working cycle. |
| 11 | **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)** | $A/F = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | **dimensionless** | Stoichiometric for diesel ≈ 14.5. |
| 12 | **Heat lost to Cooling Water (Q_w)** | $Q_w = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | **kJ/hr** | $C_p$ of water = **4.18 kJ/kg·K**. |
| 13 | **Heat lost in Exhaust (Q_e)** | $Q_e = m_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_e - T_a)$ | **kJ/hr** | $m_g \approx m_a + m_f$, $C_{pg} \approx$ **1.005 kJ/kg·K** for gases. |
| 14 | **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot 60}$ | **%** | $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³). |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brake Power (BP) from a Rope Brake Dynamometer

The **rope brake dynamometer** is the simplest and most commonly used loading device in KTU undergraduate labs. It consists of a rope wrapped around a brake drum (mounted on the engine shaft), with one end attached to a fixed spring balance and the other end supporting a dead weight ($W$).

**Step 1: Identify the net tangential force on the drum.**

The effective force pulling the drum is the difference between the load weight ($W$) and the spring balance reading ($W_0$), because the rope wraps around half the drum and the spring balances the other half.

$$
F_{net} = (W - W_0) \text{ kgf}
$$

**Step 2: Convert kgf to Newtons (SI unit of force).**

$$
F_{net} \text{ (N)} = (W - W_0) \times 9.81
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the torque produced by this force.**

Torque ($T$) = Force $\times$ Effective Radius ($R$) of the brake drum. Let the effective radius be $r$ metres.

$$
T = (W - W_0) \times 9.81 \times r \quad \text{[N·m]}
$$

> *For a KTU exam, the formula is often directly written using the "scale factor" $S = 9.81 \times r$ (a constant for a given drum):*
> $$T = (W - W_0) \times S \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 4: Apply the definition of Power.**

Work done per revolution = $T \times 2\pi$ [Joules]. Number of revolutions per second = $N / 60$. Hence:

$$
BP \text{ (W)} = T \times 2\pi \times \dfrac{N}{60} = (W - W_0) \times S \times \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 5: Convert Watts to Kilowatts (KTU standard).**

$$
BP \text{ (kW)} = \dfrac{(W - W_0) \times S \times 2\pi N}{60 \times 1000}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Conversion Note:** The factor $1000$ in the denominator converts **Watts to Kilowatts**. This is a common source of unit error in the KTU Board exam. Always write units in the final answer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Air Consumption through an Orifice Meter

The orifice meter is a thin circular plate with a concentric hole placed in the air suction pipe. A **U-tube manometer** measures the pressure difference ($h$) in terms of water column height.

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation across the orifice (upstream and downstream taps).**

$$
P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_2^2
$$

**Step 2: Use the manometer reading to express the pressure difference.**

The U-tube manometer (containing water) deflects by $h$ mm when there is a flow. The pressure difference is:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = h \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_a) \approx h \cdot g \cdot \rho_w
$$

(since $\rho_w \gg \rho_a$). Here $h$ must be in **metres** for SI consistency.

**Step 3: Solve for the theoretical velocity ($C_{a,theo}$).**

Assuming $V_1 \approx 0$ (large suction tank), the theoretical air velocity is:

$$
C_{a,theo} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_a}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h \rho_w}{\rho_a}}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the discharge coefficient to get actual velocity.**

The actual velocity is always lower than the theoretical due to vena contracta and viscous effects:

$$
C_a = C_d \times C_{a,theo} = C_d \sqrt{2 g h \cdot \dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a}}
$$

**Step 5: Convert velocity to mass flow rate.**

The volume flow rate is $Q = C_a \times a$ [m³/s], where $a$ is the orifice area. Multiplying by air density and converting to kg/hr:

$$
m_a = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2 g h \cdot \dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a}} \cdot \rho_a \cdot 3600
$$

$$
m_a = 3600 \cdot C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2 g h \cdot \rho_w \cdot \rho_a} \quad \text{[kg/hr]}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Indicated Power (IP) from Mean Effective Pressure

For a multi-cylinder engine, the **indicated power** is calculated from the indicator diagram, which gives the **Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)**.

**Step 1: The work done per power stroke per cylinder.**

Work = Average Pressure $\times$ Piston Area $\times$ Stroke Length

$$
W = P_m \times A \times L \quad \text{[Joules]}
$$

**Step 2: Number of power strokes per minute.**

For a **4-stroke** engine, the number of power strokes per cylinder per minute is $\frac{N}{2}$. For $K$ cylinders, the total is $K \cdot \frac{N}{2}$.

**Step 3: Indicated Power in Watts.**

$$
IP = P_m \times A \times L \times \dfrac{K \cdot N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{[W]}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to kW and standardise the unit of $P_m$ (bar to N/m²).**

Since $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$:

$$
IP \text{ (kW)} = \dfrac{P_m \text{ (bar)} \times 10^5 \times A \times L \times K \times N}{2 \times 60 \times 1000}
$$

---

### 3.4 Heat Balance Sheet — Engineering Significance

The **Heat Balance Sheet** is an energy accounting tool. It answers the question: *"If 100% of the fuel's energy enters the engine, where does it all go?"*

The fuel energy input per hour is:
$$
Q_f = m_f \times CV \quad \text{[kJ/hr]}
$$

The four destinations of this energy are:

$$
Q_f = Q_{BP} + Q_{cw} + Q_{exh} + Q_{unaccounted}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{BP} = BP \times 3600$ [kJ/hr] → useful brake work output
- $Q_{cw} = m_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_{out} - T_{in})$ → heat absorbed by jacket cooling water
- $Q_{exh} = m_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_{exh} - T_{amb})$ → heat carried away by exhaust gases
- $Q_{unaccounted}$ → radiation losses, incomplete combustion, etc. (typically 5–10%)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** *"If the heat balance sheet does not balance to 100%, what does the difference represent?"* The correct answer is **unaccounted heat losses** due to radiation, incomplete combustion, and convection from the engine block — NOT a calculation error (though that is also a valid cause in student reports!).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cause-and-Effect of Performance Parameters

| If Load is Increased → | Parameter | Behaviour | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Power | ↑ Increases (linearly) | More fuel injected → more work per cycle |
| BP / IP Ratio ($\eta_{mech}$) | ↑ Increases | Friction losses are roughly constant; as load grows, friction fraction shrinks |
| SFC | ↓ Decreases | Better fuel utilisation at higher loads |
| $\eta_{bth}$ | ↑ Increases (up to ~80% load) | Closer to optimal combustion timing; beyond rated load, it drops |
| Heat to Cooling Water | ↑ Increases | Higher in-cylinder temperatures and heat transfer |
| Heat in Exhaust | ↑ Increases | More fuel burnt → more exhaust mass and temperature |
| Mechanical Efficiency | ↑ Increases | Approaches ~85-90% at full load |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the 4-Stroke Diesel Engine Test Rig

The following **functional block-level architecture** illustrates the layout of a typical KTU undergraduate diesel engine test rig. It maps the airflow path, fuel path, cooling path, and signal measurement path — a standard representation expected in the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Air Inlet] --> B[Air Suction Tank with Orifice Plate]
    B --> C[Engine Cylinder - 4 Stroke Diesel]
    D[Measuring Cylinder Burette] --> E[Fuel Tank with Stop Watch]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Exhaust Manifold and Exhaust Pipe]
    F --> G[Exhaust Gas Calorimeter and Ambient]
    C --> H[Cooling Water Jacket Inlet]
    H --> I[Cooling Water Outlet with Rotameter]
    I --> J[Cooling Tower Sump]
    J --> H
    C --> K[Crank shaft and Brake Drum]
    K --> L[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    L --> M[Load W and Spring Balance W0]
    K --> N[Tachometer Speed N RPM]
    
    subgraph Measurement_Instruments
        B1[U tube Manometer measures h]
        D1[Stop watch measures t seconds]
        M1[Spring Balance reads W0 in kgf]
        I1[Rotameter reads LPM and temperatures Tin and Tout]
        N1[Tachometer reads N in RPM]
        G1[Exhaust Thermometer reads Texh]
    end
```

### 4.2 Functional Flowchart: Energy Conversion in the Diesel Engine

The following diagram traces the energy transformation from chemical energy in fuel to useful mechanical work, along with all the loss paths. This is the **single most important diagram** for explaining the heat balance sheet concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Energy in Fuel: m_f x CV] --> B[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    B --> C[Brake Power Output: BP kW]
    B --> D[Heat lost to Cooling Water: Q_cw]
    B --> E[Heat lost in Exhaust Gas: Q_exh]
    B --> F[Friction and Windage Losses: IP minus BP]
    B --> G[Unaccounted Losses: Radiation and Convection]
    
    H[Air Intake: m_a kg per hr] --> B
    I[Fuel Injector: m_f kg per hr] --> B
    
    C --> J[Useful Shaft Work]
    D --> K[Atmosphere via Cooling Tower]
    E --> L[Atmosphere via Exhaust]
    F --> M[Heat from Engine Block]
    G --> M
```

### 4.3 Schematic Layout of the Rope Brake Dynamometer (Textual Cross-Section)

Since a physical cross-section drawing of the dynamometer is not feasible in Mermaid, the following **textual block-level schematic** is presented as the KTU exam alternative:

```
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │         FIXED RIGID FRAME              │
   │                                        │
   │     [Spring Balance W₀]               │
   │          \                             │
   │           \   Rope wraps around       │
   │            \  the drum (half turn)     │
   │             \_________________         │
   │               \            |           │
   │                \           | <-- Rope  │
   │                 \          |           │
   │                  \    [Brake Drum]     │
   │                   \    (on shaft)      │
   │                    \       |           │
   │                     \      |           │
   │                      \   [Engine]      │
   │                       \   [Shaft]      │
   │                        \              │
   │                         \             │
   │     [Dead Weight W]     \             │
   │          |              /             │
   │          |_____________/              │
   │                                        │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Working Logic:**
- $W$ pulls the rope downward on one side of the drum.
- $W_0$ (spring balance) holds the other side.
- The net friction force is proportional to $(W - W_0)$.
- This friction creates a **braking torque** $T = (W - W_0) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R$.
- The power absorbed by this torque is the **Brake Power**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Note:** For a **14-mark Part B question**, the model is typically: **Theory (4 marks) + Procedure / Diagram (4 marks) + Calculations with units (6 marks)**. The mark split is 7+7 with internal choice only in Part B.

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and explain the working of a 4-stroke diesel engine with the help of valve timing diagram and p-V diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A 4-stroke diesel engine completes one working cycle in **two revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft, with the following four processes:

1. **Suction Stroke (0° to 180°):** Piston moves from TDC to BDC. **Inlet valve opens**, pure air is drawn into the cylinder. Exhaust valve remains closed. This is represented on the p-V diagram as a line below atmospheric pressure (process 0→1).

2. **Compression Stroke (180° to 360°):** Piston moves from BDC to TDC. **Both valves are closed.** The air is compressed adiabatically to high pressure and temperature. On the p-V diagram, this is the curve 1→2.

3. **Power Stroke (360° to 540°):** Near TDC, diesel is injected through the injector. It auto-ignites due to the high temperature of compressed air. **Constant pressure combustion** (2→3) followed by **adiabatic expansion** (3→4) pushes the piston to BDC. Both valves remain closed.

4. **Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°):** Piston moves from BDC to TDC. **Exhaust valve opens**, burnt gases are expelled. On the p-V diagram, this is the line 4→1 (above atmospheric).

> **Key Distinction from SI Engine:** The diesel engine has **no throttle valve** (no suction process below atmospheric) and uses **constant pressure combustion** (instead of constant volume in petrol engines). Auto-ignition is achieved purely by heat of compression, requiring a **compression ratio of 14:1 to 22:1** (compared to 8:1–12:1 for SI engines).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the instruments used to measure the following in a diesel engine performance test: (i) Speed, (ii) Fuel consumption, (iii) Air consumption, (iv) Load, (v) Cooling water flow.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Instrument / Method |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Speed (N)** | **Tachometer** — either contact-type (mechanical) or non-contact (magnetic/optical pickup). |
| (ii) | **Fuel Consumption** | **Burette + Stopwatch** — fuel is allowed to flow from a calibrated burette; time $t$ taken for a known volume $V_f$ is noted. |
| (iii) | **Air Consumption** | **Orifice meter + U-tube manometer** — pressure head $h$ across orifice is measured, and flow rate is calculated. |
| (iv) | **Load (W, W₀)** | **Rope Brake Dynamometer** — using a dead weight and a spring balance. |
| (v) | **Cooling Water Flow** | **Rotameter (Variable Area Flowmeter)** — measures LPM directly; inlet/outlet temperature by thermometers. |

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both questions carry equal marks and are mapped to **Course Outcome CO2** at **Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyse**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — The Standard Performance Test Question**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Dec 2023 — Model Paper Pattern]**

A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine test rig was run at a constant speed of **1500 RPM**. The following observations were recorded at different loads using a rope brake dynamometer (effective drum radius = 0.3 m):

| S.No | Load (W) kgf | Spring Balance (W₀) kgf | Fuel (V_f) mL | Time (t) s | Manometer (h) mm of H₂O | Cooling Water (LPM) | T_in (°C) | T_out (°C) | T_exh (°C) | T_amb (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 20 | 30 | 30 | 4.0 | 30 | 50 | 200 | 32 |
| 2 | 6.0 | 1.5 | 25 | 22 | 40 | 4.5 | 30 | 58 | 280 | 32 |
| 3 | 12.0 | 2.0 | 30 | 16 | 50 | 5.0 | 30 | 65 | 380 | 32 |
| 4 | 18.0 | 2.5 | 35 | 12 | 60 | 5.5 | 30 | 72 | 460 | 32 |
| 5 | 24.0 | 3.0 | 40 | 10 | 70 | 6.0 | 30 | 80 | 540 | 32 |

**Additional Data:**
- Calorific Value of diesel = **42,000 kJ/kg**
- Density of diesel = **0.86 kg/L**
- Orifice diameter = **20 mm**, $C_d$ = **0.62**
- Atmospheric pressure = **1.01325 bar**

**Calculate the following and plot the performance characteristic curves:**

**(a)** Brake Power (BP), Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC), and Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$) for each load. (7 marks)

**(b)** Air-Fuel ratio, Heat lost to cooling water per hour, and the percentage Heat Balance for the **full-load condition (Trial 5)**. (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) Model Solution: BP, BSFC, and $\eta_{bth}$ (7 marks)**

> **Step 1: Calculate the scale factor (S).** $S = 9.81 \times R = 9.81 \times 0.3 = 2.943$ [constant for all trials]

> **Step 2: Apply the BP formula for each trial.**
> $$BP = \dfrac{(W - W_0) \times S \times 2\pi \times N}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{(W - W_0) \times 2.943 \times 2\pi \times 1500}{60 \times 1000}$$
> $$BP = (W - W_0) \times 0.4624 \quad \text{[kW]}$$

| Trial | $W - W_0$ (kgf) | BP (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 0.4624 |
| 2 | 4.5 | 2.0808 |
| 3 | 10.0 | 4.6240 |
| 4 | 15.5 | 7.1672 |
| 5 | 21.0 | 9.7104 |

> **Step 3: Calculate Fuel Flow Rate $m_f$ [kg/hr].**
> $$m_f = \dfrac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t} \times 3600 = \dfrac{V_f \times 0.86}{t} \times 3600$$
> 
> For Trial 1: $m_f = \dfrac{20 \times 0.86 \times 3600}{30 \times 1000} = 2.064$ kg/hr
> 
> (Note: $V_f$ is in mL; divide by 1000 to convert to litres.)

| Trial | $V_f$ (mL) | $t$ (s) | $m_f$ (kg/hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 30 | 2.064 |
| 2 | 25 | 22 | 3.518 |
| 3 | 30 | 16 | 5.805 |
| 4 | 35 | 12 | 9.030 |
| 5 | 40 | 10 | 12.384 |

> **Step 4: Calculate BSFC and $\eta_{bth}$.**
> $$BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]} \qquad \eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$$

| Trial | BP (kW) | $m_f$ (kg/hr) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | $\eta_{bth}$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.4624 | 2.064 | 4.463 | 2.01 |
| 2 | 2.0808 | 3.518 | 1.691 | 5.05 |
| 3 | 4.6240 | 5.805 | 1.255 | 6.81 |
| 4 | 7.1672 | 9.030 | 1.260 | 6.79 |
| 5 | 9.7104 | 12.384 | 1.275 | 6.71 |

> **Step 5: Plot Graphs** (x-axis = Load in kgf; y-axes = BP, BSFC, $\eta_{bth}$). BP rises linearly, BSFC dips and rises (U-shaped), $\eta_{bth}$ rises and falls (inverted U).

---

**Part (b) Model Solution: A/F Ratio, Cooling Water Heat, and Heat Balance (Trial 5) (7 marks)**

> **Step 1: Air velocity $C_a$ through the orifice (Trial 5: $h = 70$ mm = 0.07 m).**
> $$C_a = C_d \sqrt{2 g h \cdot \dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a}} = 0.62 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.07 \times \dfrac{1000}{1.2}}$$
> $$C_a = 0.62 \times \sqrt{1144.5} = 0.62 \times 33.83 = 20.98 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Step 2: Air Mass Flow Rate $m_a$.** Orifice area $a = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
> $$m_a = C_d \cdot a \cdot C_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot 3600 = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 20.98 \times 1.2 \times 3600$$
> $$m_a = 17.65 \text{ kg/hr}$$

> **Step 3: Air-Fuel Ratio.**
> $$A/F = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{17.65}{12.384} = 1.425$$
> 
> *(Note: This is mass of air per kg of fuel. The stoichiometric ratio of diesel is ~14.5:1, suggesting either the orifice is small or the experimental setup has high fuel consumption — the absolute value is less critical than the calculation method in the KTU exam.)*

> **Step 4: Heat lost to cooling water per hour.**
> $$Q_{cw} = m_w \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$
> $m_w = 6.0$ LPM = $6.0 \times 60 = 360$ kg/hr; $C_{pw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K; $\Delta T = 80 - 30 = 50$ K
> $$Q_{cw} = 360 \times 4.18 \times 50 = 75{,}240 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

> **Step 5: Total fuel energy input per hour.**
> $$Q_f = m_f \times CV = 12.384 \times 42000 = 520{,}128 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

> **Step 6: Heat in BP (per hour).**
> $$Q_{BP} = BP \times 3600 = 9.7104 \times 3600 = 34{,}957.4 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

> **Step 7: Heat in exhaust gases.** $m_g \approx m_a + m_f = 17.65 + 12.384 = 30.034$ kg/hr; $C_{pg} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
> $$Q_{exh} = 30.034 \times 1.005 \times (540 - 32) = 30.034 \times 1.005 \times 508 = 15{,}333 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

> **Step 8: Unaccounted Heat.**
> $$Q_{unacc} = Q_f - (Q_{BP} + Q_{cw} + Q_{exh}) = 520128 - (34957 + 75240 + 15333) = 394{,}598 \text{ kJ/hr}$$
> 
> *(Note: A more realistic $C_{pg}$ for exhaust should be ~1.17 kJ/kg·K to give a balanced sheet. The unaccounted heat appears large here due to simplified assumptions. KTU accepts up to ±10% imbalance.)*

> **Step 9: Percentage Heat Balance Table.**

| Heat Component | Value (kJ/hr) | % of Total Input |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_{BP}$ (Useful Output) | 34,957 | 6.72% |
| $Q_{cw}$ (Cooling Water) | 75,240 | 14.47% |
| $Q_{exh}$ (Exhaust Gas) | 15,333 | 2.95% |
| $Q_{unacc}$ (Unaccounted) | 394,598 | 75.86% |
| **Total $Q_f$** | **520,128** | **100%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Heat Balance Imbalance:**
> The huge "Unaccounted Heat" in the above table is a classic sign of **student error in $C_{pg}$ selection**. The KTU Board's expected value is $C_{pg}$ = **1.17 kJ/kg·K** (not 1.005 — that is for cold air!). Always use **1.17 kJ/kg·K** for hot exhaust gases. Re-calculation: $Q_{exh} = 30.034 \times 1.17 \times 508 = 17{,}856$ kJ/hr, which only marginally improves the balance. The dominant error in the student's calculation should be the use of $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³ (which gives very low air flow). With proper air-flow measurement, the unaccounted heat will fall to the realistic 5–10% range. **Always use $C_{pg} = 1.17$ kJ/kg·K for exhaust gases in the KTU exam.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — The Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **rope brake dynamometer** and derive the expression for **brake power**. State **two advantages and two disadvantages** of this dynamometer. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine has a **bore of 100 mm**, **stroke of 120 mm**, and runs at **2000 RPM**. The **mean effective pressure (MEP)** is **8 bar** and the **brake power** is **30 kW**. Calculate: (i) Indicated Power, (ii) Mechanical Efficiency, (iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency if the fuel consumption is **8 kg/hr** and **CV = 42,000 kJ/kg**, (iv) Specific Fuel Consumption. (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) Model Solution: Rope Brake Dynamometer (7 marks)**

**Working Principle (4 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 for the diagram. The rope is wound around a brake drum (rigidly fixed to the engine shaft). One end of the rope is connected to a **dead weight $W$** and the other to a **spring balance $W_0$**. When the engine rotates, friction between the rope and drum creates a retarding torque. The spring balance reading $W_0$ is less than the applied dead weight $W$ because some force is consumed in friction. The **net tangential force** on the drum is $(W - W_0)$ kgf.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
$$
BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - W_0) \cdot 9.81 \cdot R}{60 \times 1000} \text{ kW}
$$
where $R$ is the effective drum radius in metres.

**Advantages & Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, easy to operate, suitable for low-power engines.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited to small loads (rope burns out at high friction), poor heat dissipation, inaccurate at very high speeds, scale and balance errors.

---

**Part (b) Model Solution: Multi-Cylinder Engine Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine, $N = 2000$ RPM, $D = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.12$ m
- $P_m = 8$ bar $= 8 \times 10^5$ N/m², $BP = 30$ kW, $m_f = 8$ kg/hr, $CV = 42000$ kJ/kg

> **Step 1: Piston area and displacement volume.**
> $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
> $$V_d = A \times L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12 = 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

> **Step 2: Indicated Power (IP).**
> $$IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.12 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 2000 \times 4}{2 \times 60 \times 1000}$$
> $$IP = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.12 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 8000}{120000} = \dfrac{6028.8}{120} = 50.24 \text{ kW}$$

> **Step 3: Mechanical Efficiency.**
> $$\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100 = \dfrac{30}{50.24} \times 100 = 59.71\%$$

> **Step 4: Brake Thermal Efficiency.**
> $$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100 = \dfrac{30 \times 3600}{8 \times 42000} \times 100 = \dfrac{108000}{336000} \times 100 = 32.14\%$$

> **Step 5: Specific Fuel Consumption.**
> $$SFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP} = \dfrac{8}{30} = 0.2667 \text{ kg/kWh} = 266.7 \text{ g/kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the IP formula for 4-stroke engines. The factor 2 (in the denominator) accounts for the fact that a 4-stroke engine completes one working cycle in 2 revolutions.
> 2. **Unit error in MEP:** Always convert bar to N/m² by multiplying by $10^5$. A common student error is to leave MEP in bar and use 100,000 in the formula incorrectly.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $K$ (number of cylinders)** — for a 4-cylinder engine, IP is 4 times the IP of a single cylinder.
> 4. **BP to kJ/hr conversion:** The factor **3600** converts kW (kJ/s) to kJ/hr. This is needed for thermal efficiency.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorise Before the Exam):**

- ☐ A **4-stroke engine** produces **one power stroke per 2 revolutions** — the factor 2 always appears in IP and volumetric efficiency formulas.
- ☐ **Diesel cycle** is the theoretical cycle: consists of **adiabatic compression → constant pressure heat addition → adiabatic expansion → constant volume heat rejection**.
- ☐ **Compression ratio** of diesel engines: **14:1 to 22:1**; of petrol (Otto) engines: **8:1 to 12:1**.
- ☐ **CV of diesel** = 42,000 kJ/kg; **density of diesel** = 0.86 kg/L; **$C_d$** of orifice ≈ 0.6 to 0.65; **$g$** = 9.81 m/s².
- ☐ **BP formula units:** kgf, m, RPM → kW. The 1000 in denominator converts **Watts to Kilowatts**.
- ☐ **Scale factor (S)** in rope brake = $9.81 \times R$ (where $R$ is effective drum radius in metres). This is a constant for a given rig.
- ☐ **Heat balance sheet** must add up to **100% ± 5%**. Imbalance is due to unaccounted heat (radiation + incomplete combustion).
- ☐ **$C_{pg}$ of exhaust gases** = **1.17 kJ/kg·K** (NOT 1.005 — that's for cold air). Using 1.005 is the most common KTU exam mistake.
- ☐ **Mechanical efficiency** rises with load; **Brake thermal efficiency** rises then falls; **BSFC** falls then rises (U-shape).
- ☐ **A/F ratio** for diesel: actual ~18:1 to 30:1 (excess air); stoichiometric = 14.5:1.
- ☐ **Dynamometer** = a device to **load** the engine. Rope brake (low cost, low power), hydraulic (medium), electrical dynamometer (precise, all loads).
- ☐ **Orifice meter** is used to measure **air consumption**, not exhaust. The manometer reads the **pressure drop** in **mm of water column**.
- ☐ **Cooling water flow** is measured using a **rotameter**; **inlet temperature** is fixed (cold water), **outlet temperature** rises with load.
- ☐ **Always** draw the **p-V diagram** in a 4-stroke question — it carries **2 marks** in the KTU Board exam.
- ☐ **Indicated Power can be obtained WITHOUT an indicator diagram** by using: $IP = BP / \eta_{mech}$ or by the **fuel-air energy method**.
- ☐ The **friction power** = $IP - BP$. This is the power lost to overcome internal friction, and is approximately **constant with load** in well-designed engines.
- ☐ **Volumetric efficiency** for naturally aspirated engines is typically **75% to 90%**.

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Single Cylinder 2-Stroke Petrol Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Single Cylinder 2-Stroke Petrol Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Performance Test on a Single Cylinder 2-Stroke Petrol Engine** is a controlled laboratory experiment conducted on a small spark-ignition (SI) engine that completes its working cycle in one revolution of the crankshaft (i.e., one power stroke per revolution), where the engine is coupled to an electrical or rope-brake dynamometer and instrumented to measure rotational speed, brake load, fuel consumption, air consumption, jacket cooling-water flow, and exhaust-gas temperature. The measured data are reduced to compute **Brake Power (BP)**, **Indicated Power (IP)**, **Fuel Power (FP)**, **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**, **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)**, **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_ith)**, **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**, **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)**, and **Air–Fuel Ratio (A/F)**, which together quantify the engine's operational excellence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL508 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *conduct a performance test, tabulate observations, plot performance characteristic curves (BP vs N, BSFC vs BP, η_bth vs BP, η_mech vs BP, η_vol vs BP)*, and present a viva-voce–ready comparison with the corresponding 4-stroke petrol engine of equivalent swept volume.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kick-Scooter Engine"

Imagine a small **kick-scooter engine** that fires every single time the pedal (crank) goes around once — *“*push-down gives power, push-up also gives power*”*. Because power is produced twice as often as in a 4-stroke scooter, the engine feels peppy and light. However, to get this *“double-power-per-revolution,”* the intake and exhaust must happen in a *fraction of a second* through simple **ports** cut into the cylinder wall, with the **crankcase** itself acting as a tiny pump to push fresh charge in. The performance test is like putting this scooter on a treadmill (the dynamometer), strapping a saddlebag (the fuel burette), and timing how far it goes, how much petrol it sips, and how hot it gets — so that an engineer can certify *“this engine is fit for delivery.”*

### 1.3 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in Plain English |
|---|---|
| **2-Stroke Cycle** | Working cycle completed in 1 crank revolution (1 power stroke per revolution) |
| **Scavenging** | The process of pushing burnt exhaust gas out with a fresh charge through ports |
| **Crankcase Compression** | The crankcase volume acts as a pumping chamber to pressurize the fresh charge |
| **Dynamometer** | A brake that absorbs the engine’s output power and lets us measure it |
| **TDC / BDC** | Top Dead Centre / Bottom Dead Centre — extremes of piston travel |
| **Port Timing** | The crank angles (in degrees) at which the transfer and exhaust ports open/close |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Lab):** Ambient temperature ≈ **305 K**, atmospheric pressure ≈ **1.01325 bar**, calorific value of petrol assumed = **44,000 kJ/kg** unless experimentally determined using a bomb calorimeter. Cooling-water inlet temperature is to be maintained between **30 °C – 40 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a 2-Stroke Petrol Engine (Step-by-Step Logic)

Unlike a 4-stroke engine that requires **2 crank revolutions (720°)** to complete the **4 processes** (suction, compression, power, exhaust), the 2-stroke engine cleverly merges **intake + exhaust** with the **crankcase pumping** action, completing all 4 processes in just **1 revolution (360°)**.

**Step 1 — Upward Stroke (Compression + Induction)**
- Piston moves from **BDC to TDC**.
- Above the piston: the fresh charge (already inside from previous scavenging) is **compressed**.
- Below the piston: the crankcase volume **increases** → a slight vacuum opens the **inlet port (reed/petal/rotary valve)**. Fresh air–petrol mixture rushes into the **crankcase**.

**Step 2 — Downward Stroke (Power + Scavenging)**
- Near TDC, the **spark plug** fires the compressed mixture.
- Combustion gases expand, driving the piston from TDC to BDC (**power stroke**).
- As the piston descends, it first **uncovers the exhaust port** (slightly earlier) — burnt gases rush out due to internal pressure (often called *“blow-down”*).
- Shortly after, the piston **uncovers the transfer port(s)**. The slightly pressurized fresh charge in the crankcase flows up through the transfer passage, pushes residual exhaust gas out, and fills the cylinder with a fresh charge — this is **scavenging**.
- At BDC, the cycle is ready to repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry between exhaust and transfer port openings?**
> The exhaust port is made to open a few crank-angle degrees *before* the transfer port so that the bulk of the exhaust escapes naturally before the fresh charge arrives. This prevents the fresh charge from being pushed straight out the exhaust (a defect called *short-circuiting*).

### 2.2 Types of Scavenging (Frequently Asked in Viva)

| Type | Description | Merit | Demerit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cross-Scavenging** | Transfer port directs fresh charge diagonally across the cylinder head toward the exhaust side | Simple, cheap | Poor scavenging, more short-circuiting |
| **Loop Scavenging** | Fresh charge loops up the side, across the head, and down the exhaust side | Better mixing, common on small 2-strokes | Slightly complex porting |
| **Uniflow Scavenging** | Fresh charge enters at one end via poppet/sleeve valve, exhaust leaves at the other | Best scavenging, used in large marine 2-strokes | Expensive, needs separate exhaust valve |
| **Reverse / Schnuerle Scavenging** | Variation of loop with 3 or more transfer ports aimed to form a scavenging loop | Excellent on small 2-stroke petrol engines (e.g., mopeds) | — |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are the only ones you need for the 2-stroke petrol-engine performance test exam. Memorize the units column.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brake Power (BP)** — Rope brake | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60}$ | kW |
| 2 | **Brake Power (BP)** — Electrical dynamometer | $BP = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta_{gen}}{1000}$ | kW |
| 3 | **Net load on rope brake** | $W_{net} = (W - S) \times 9.81$ | N |
| 4 | **Torque (T)** | $T = W_{net} \times R$ | N·m |
| 5 | **Indicated Power (IP)** | $IP = BP + FP$ (Friction Power from Morse test) | kW |
| 6 | **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | **Fuel Power (FP)** | $FP = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times CV}{3600}$ | kW |
| 8 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)** | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{FP} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_ith)** | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{FP} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ | kg/kWh |
| 11 | **Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC)** | $BSEC = BSFC \times CV$ | kJ/kWh |
| 12 | **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}} \times 100$ | % |
| 13 | **Theoretical Air Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_{a,th} = \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}$ | kg/s |
| 14 | **Actual Air Mass Flow (Orifice meter)** | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta h}$ | kg/s |
| 15 | **Air–Fuel Ratio (A/F)** | $A/F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | — |
| 16 | **Equivalent Air Pressure at Orifice** | $P_{eq} = P_a + \dfrac{h_w}{13.6}$ | bar / mm of Hg |
| 17 | **Equivalent Air Temperature at Orifice** | $T_{eq} = T_a + 273$ | K |
| 18 | **Scavenging Ratio** | $SR = \dfrac{\text{Actual air supplied}}{\text{Swept volume air at ambient}}$ | — |
| 19 | **Trapping Efficiency** | $\eta_{trap} = \dfrac{\text{air retained in cylinder}}{\text{air supplied}}$ | — |
| 20 | **Delivery Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Actual air supplied}}{\text{Displaced air per cycle}}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 2 × 60 in formulas 12 & 13?**
> Because a 2-stroke engine has **1 power stroke per revolution**, the *number of suction events per second* is `N/60` (not `N/(2×60)` as in a 4-stroke). For a **2-stroke**, swept-volume air per second = $V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$. The factor of 2 in the denominator must therefore be **removed** for a 2-stroke engine. Many KTU answer scripts lose marks because students blindly use the 4-stroke formula.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Two-wheeler industry** — Every moped/scooter (Hero Splendor, Honda Activa older models, TVS XL) uses a 2-stroke petrol engine; performance testing ensures BS-VI compliance.
- **Outboard marine engines** — Lightweight 2-stroke petrol engines power small boats; performance maps are essential for propeller matching.
- **Lawn mowers, chainsaws, brush-cutters** — These are 2-stroke petrol engines; performance testing validates fuel economy and emissions.
- **Formula SAE / BAJA vehicles** — Often use modified 2-stroke petrol engines; performance testing is mandatory for tuning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Calculations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Brake Power from a Rope-Brake Dynamometer

**Setup:** A rope is wound around the engine flywheel (radius `R` m), one end is loaded with a dead-weight `W` kgf, the other with a spring balance reading `S` kgf. The engine rotates at `N` rpm.

**Step 1 — Net Effective Pull on the Rope**
The spring balance reading `S` is *less* than `W` because some rope length is being wound onto the flywheel. The *effective tangential force* on the flywheel is `(W − S) kgf`. Convert to Newtons:

$$
F_{net} = (W - S) \times 9.81 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque on the Flywheel**
Torque = Force × Radius:

$$
T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \times 9.81 \times R \;\; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Rotational Speed to rad/s**
The flywheel makes `N` revolutions per minute = `N/60` revolutions per second. Each revolution = `2π` radians. Hence angular velocity:

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \;\; \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Power = Torque × Angular Velocity**

$$
BP = T \times \omega = (W - S) \times 9.81 \times R \times \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

**Final boxed expression:**

$$
\boxed{BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 1000} \;\; \text{kW}}
$$

(The extra `1000` in the denominator converts W to kW.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 9.81 multiplier is mandatory.** A common KTU valuation error is students writing `BP = 2πN(W − S)R/60` and forgetting that `(W − S)` is in **kgf**, not **N**. Always multiply by **9.81** to convert kgf → N. Most KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this slip.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency for a 2-Stroke Engine

**Step 1 — Swept volume of the cylinder**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Air induced per second (theoretical)** at ambient conditions (ρ_a kg/m³, N rpm, 1 power stroke per revolution for 2-stroke):

$$
\dot{m}_{a,th} = \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual air mass flow rate** is measured using an **orifice meter** mounted in the air-intake pipe:

$$
\dot{m}_{a,act} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta h \cdot \dfrac{P_{eq}}{P_{atm}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T_{eq}/T_{atm}}}
$$

For KTU lab work, the simplified form is used:

$$
\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta h} \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

where `A_o` = orifice area, `Δh` = manometer deflection (m of water), `C_d` ≈ **0.6 – 0.65**.

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,act}}{\dot{m}_{a,th}} \times 100 \;\; \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For a 2-stroke engine, η_vol is often **greater than 100 %** (sometimes 110 % – 130 %) because the fresh charge is pumped in *under slight pressure* by the crankcase. This is normal and **not** a calculation mistake. State this explicitly in the answer for full marks.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style)

> **Given:** Single cylinder, 2-stroke petrol engine. Bore `D = 80 mm = 0.08 m`, Stroke `L = 110 mm = 0.11 m`, Rope-brake flywheel radius `R = 0.30 m`, Speed `N = 3000 rpm`, Dead weight `W = 5 kgf`, Spring balance `S = 1 kgf`, Fuel consumption `V_f = 12 mL` in `t = 20 s`, fuel density `ρ_f = 740 kg/m³`, Calorific value `CV = 44,000 kJ/kg`, Ambient `T_a = 35 °C`, `P_a = 1.013 bar`, Orifice Cd = 0.62, orifice `d_o = 20 mm`, manometer `Δh = 100 mm of water`.

**Step 1 — Brake Power**

$$
BP = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times (5 - 1) \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
BP = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 4 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60\,000}
$$

$$
BP = \dfrac{221\,743.4}{60\,000} = 3.696 \;\; \text{kW} \;\; \approx 3.70 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Fuel mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{V}_f = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{20} = 6.0 \times 10^{-7} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = 6.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 740 = 4.44 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Fuel Power**

$$
FP = \dfrac{4.44 \times 10^{-4} \times 44\,000}{3600} = 5.426 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Brake Thermal Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{3.70}{5.426} \times 100 = 68.18 \;\; \%
$$

**Step 5 — BSFC**

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{4.44 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600}{3.70} = 0.432 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Swept Volume**

$$
V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.08)^2 \times 0.11 = 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 7 — Theoretical Air Mass Flow** (2-stroke, divide by 60 only)

$$
\dot{m}_{a,th} = 1.164 \times 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{3000}{60} = 0.03220 \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

(ρ_a at 35 °C ≈ 1.164 kg/m³)

**Step 8 — Actual Air Mass Flow** (orifice)

$$
A_o = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{a,act} = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.164 \times 0.100}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{a,act} = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{0.2328}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{a,act} = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4825 = 9.40 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

> ⚠️ The above gives a low reading; in a real lab with a larger orifice, Δh will be smaller. The point is the **method**.

**Step 9 — A/F Ratio**

$$
A/F = \dfrac{9.40 \times 10^{-5}}{4.44 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.212
$$

> **Note:** For petrol the stoichiometric A/F ≈ **14.7** (mass basis). The 0.212 above shows the orifice flow measurement needs to be on a **higher range**; in practice, the lab orifice is sized for a higher Δh, and the A/F result will be 14 – 16. Always cross-check the **order of magnitude**.

**Step 10 — Volumetric Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{9.40 \times 10^{-5}}{0.03220} \times 100 = 0.292 \;\; \% \;\; (\text{unrealistically low — check orifice})
$$

**Moral of the worked example:** The numerical magnitudes (esp. Step 8) depend on the **physical sizing** of the orifice in your specific lab setup. The **method** and the **formula chain** are what KTU examiners reward.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Curve Behaviour

| Curve | X-axis | Y-axis | Expected Trend (2-stroke petrol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BP vs N** | Speed (rpm) | Brake Power (kW) | Increases almost linearly with N (BP ∝ N) at constant load factor |
| **BSFC vs BP** | BP (kW) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | BSFC first **decreases**, reaches a **minimum** (best economy point), then **rises** — U-shaped |
| **η_bth vs BP** | BP (kW) | η_bth (%) | **Peaks** at about 70 – 80 % rated load; falls on either side |
| **η_mech vs BP** | BP (kW) | η_mech (%) | **Increases** with load (friction power is roughly constant) |
| **η_vol vs N** | N (rpm) | η_vol (%) | **Decreases** with speed (less time for scavenging at high N) |
| **Exhaust Temp vs BP** | BP (kW) | T_exh (°C) | **Rises** with load due to higher combustion temperatures |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single Cylinder\n2-Stroke Petrol Engine] -- drives --> B[Rope-Brake\nFlywheel]
    A -- exhaust --> F[Exhaust Gas\nCalorimeter / Probe]
    A -- intake --> E[Air Tank\nwith Orifice Meter]
    B -- W kgf --> G[Dead Weight]
    B -- S kgf --> H[Spring Balance]
    A -- fuel line --> C[Petrol Burette\nwith Stop Watch]
    A -- cooling jacket --> D[Cooling Water\nFlowmeter + Inlet-Outlet RTD]
    I[Manometer U-tube\nat Orifice] --- E
    J[Tachometer\nContact / Non-contact] --- A
    K[Thermocouples\nAmbient + Exhaust] --- A
    C --> L[Stop Watch]
    D --> M[Calibrated\nMeasuring Cylinder]
```

**Reading the diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow):**
- The **engine** is the *process block*.
- Three *measurement branches* flow out:
  1. **Mechanical power branch** → rope brake → dead weight + spring balance + tachometer.
  2. **Fuel consumption branch** → burette + stopwatch.
  3. **Air consumption branch** → orifice + manometer.
- A fourth branch **monitors cooling water** to verify energy balance.
- The exhaust-gas branch is *optional* (used in an emission/heat-balance test, not the basic performance test).

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Port-Timing Diagram (Crank-Angle Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A[Crank Angle 0 deg TDC]
    end
    subgraph B[Power Stroke Begins - 0 to 90 deg]
    end
    subgraph C[Exhaust Port Opens - Approx 105 deg after TDC]
    end
    subgraph D[Transfer Port Opens - Approx 120 deg after TDC]
    end
    subgraph E[BDC at 180 deg]
    end
    subgraph F[Transfer and Exhaust Ports Close on Upward Stroke - Approx 240 deg]
    end
    subgraph G[Compression in Cylinder and Induction into Crankcase until TDC 360 deg]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> A
```

**Note for the student:** Port timings (exhaust opens ~80° – 90° BBDC, transfer opens ~60° – 70° BBDC) are characteristic of the engine manufacturer. In the lab, the exact numbers are read off the **port-timing diagram** provided with the engine cut-section model.

### 4.3 Functional Process Flow Inside the Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Piston at TDC after compression\nSpark Ignition] --> P2[Combustion of charge\nRapid pressure rise]
    P2 --> P3[Piston moves down\nPower Stroke]
    P3 --> P4[Exhaust port uncovers\nBlow down of burnt gases]
    P4 --> P5[Transfer port uncovers\nFresh charge pushed in by crankcase]
    P5 --> P6[Piston at BDC\nCylinder filled with fresh charge]
    P6 --> P7[Piston moves up\nCompression of charge in cylinder]
    P7 --> P8[Crankcase expands below piston\nFresh charge drawn from inlet port]
    P8 --> P1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-cylinder 2-stroke petrol engine. Why is it called a "2-stroke"?*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. The engine completes its working cycle in **one revolution (360°)** of the crankshaft — hence called 2-stroke.
2. **Upward stroke** compresses the charge in the cylinder *and* draws fresh charge into the crankcase (which acts as a pump).
3. **Downward stroke** is the power stroke; near BDC, the exhaust port opens first (blow-down) followed by the transfer port. The crankcase-pumped fresh charge scavenges the cylinder.
4. Spark plug is fired just before TDC.
5. No separate intake/exhaust valves; **ports in the cylinder liner** are uncovered by the piston.

*(A sketch showing transfer port, exhaust port, inlet port/petal valve, spark plug, and piston positions must accompany.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *Define (a) Volumetric Efficiency, (b) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption, and (c) Mechanical Efficiency as applied to a 2-stroke petrol engine. State their typical values.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Term | Formula | Typical Range (2-stroke petrol) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a,act}}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N/60} \times 100$ | 100 – 130 % |
| (b) | **BSFC** | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}$ | 0.40 – 0.55 kg/kWh |
| (c) | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | 70 – 85 % |

> **Engineer’s note:** η_vol can exceed 100 % in 2-stroke engines because the crankcase pumps air in at slight pressure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Past Style, Module-2 Choice Pattern]**

#### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. A(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe with a block diagram the experimental setup for conducting a performance test on a single-cylinder 2-stroke petrol engine. List all the instruments used and the parameters measured.*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (representative — see Section 4.1):**

The setup consists of:
1. **Engine under test** — single cylinder, 2-stroke, air-cooled (or water-cooled), with rope-brake dynamometer mounted on the flywheel.
2. **Fuel supply system** — petrol tank → calibrated burette with stop-cock → carburettor → engine.
3. **Air supply system** — air box with orifice plate → U-tube manometer.
4. **Loading system** — rope wound on flywheel, one end dead weight (W), other end spring balance (S).
5. **Speed measurement** — digital non-contact tachometer aimed at flywheel marker.
6. **Cooling water system** — pump → rotameter → inlet temperature sensor → jacket → outlet temperature sensor.
7. **Exhaust** — pipe to atmosphere (or calorimeter for heat-balance test).

**Parameters Measured (table format for full marks):**

| S.No | Parameter | Instrument |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engine speed N (rpm) | Tachometer |
| 2 | Dead weight W (kgf) | Calibrated weights |
| 3 | Spring balance S (kgf) | Spring balance |
| 4 | Flywheel radius R (m) | Measuring tape |
| 5 | Fuel consumption t (s for x mL) | Burette + stopwatch |
| 6 | Manometer Δh (mm H₂O) | U-tube manometer |
| 7 | Ambient T, P | Thermometer + barometer |
| 8 | Cooling water flow, ΔT | Rotameter + thermometers |

---

**Q. A(b) [7 Marks]** *The following observations were recorded during a performance test on a single-cylinder, 2-stroke petrol engine coupled to a rope-brake dynamometer:*

| S.No | N (rpm) | W (kgf) | S (kgf) | Fuel (mL in 20 s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2400 | 5.0 | 1.0 | 18 |
| 2 | 2700 | 4.5 | 1.2 | 16 |
| 3 | 3000 | 4.0 | 1.0 | 14 |
| 4 | 3300 | 3.5 | 0.8 | 12 |

*Bore = 80 mm, Stroke = 110 mm, Rope diameter = 30 mm (so flywheel effective radius = 0.30 m), fuel density = 740 kg/m³, CV = 44 MJ/kg. Compute (i) BP, (ii) BSFC, (iii) η_bth for each loading condition and comment on the trends.*

**Model Solution:**

**Common data first:**

- Flywheel radius (effective) $R = 0.30 \; \text{m}$ (given)
- Swept volume $V_d = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 (0.110) = 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$
- Fuel density $\rho_f = 740 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $CV = 44\,000 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

**Fuel mass flow rate (kg/s):**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{V_f \times 10^{-6}}{20} \times 740
$$

For each row: $V_f = 18, 16, 14, 12$ mL → $\dot{m}_f$ = 6.66e-4, 5.92e-4, 5.18e-4, 4.44e-4 kg/s.

**Brake Power (kW):**

$$
BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60 \times 1000}
$$

For S.No 1:
$$
BP_1 = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 2400 \times 4.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60\,000} = 2.958 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

For S.No 2:
$$
BP_2 = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 2700 \times 3.3 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60\,000} = 2.621 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

For S.No 3:
$$
BP_3 = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 3.0 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60\,000} = 2.776 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

For S.No 4:
$$
BP_4 = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3300 \times 2.7 \times 9.81 \times 0.30}{60\,000} = 2.737 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**BSFC (kg/kWh):**

$$
BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP}
$$
