# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Morse test. Mention its significance in engine performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Morse test is an experimental procedure, devised by **Professor H.W. Morse**, used to determine the **indicated power developed by each individual cylinder** of a multi-cylinder I.C. engine without dismantling it. The test is performed by running the engine at a constant speed, then sequentially disabling one cylinder at a time by cutting off the fuel/spark supply, and recording the drop in total indicated power. **(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for significance.)** Its significance lies in identifying **weak cylinders**, computing **cylinder-wise IP and mechanical efficiency**, and diagnosing engine health issues non-invasively.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the assumptions made in the Morse test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Frictional power of the engine remains constant during the test (i.e., before and after a cylinder is cut off). **(1 mark)**
2. The engine speed is maintained constant throughout the test. **(0.5 marks)**
3. The frictional power is uniformly distributed among all cylinders (used for computing per-cylinder FP). **(0.5 marks)**
4. The calorific value and air-fuel ratio in firing cylinders remain steady. **(0.5 marks)**
5. Ambient conditions do not change appreciably. **(0.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**Part (a)** Explain the Morse test procedure in detail with a neat sketch. State all the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

**1. Apparatus Used (1 mark):**
A multi-cylinder petrol engine test rig fitted with a rope-brake dynamometer or hydraulic dynamometer, a tachometer, a fuel measuring unit (burette), and a spark-plug cut-off switch for each cylinder.

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Start the engine and allow it to warm up to steady operating temperature.
2. Run the engine at a **constant rated speed** (e.g., 3000 rpm) and apply a standard dead-weight load on the brake drum.
3. Measure the **total indicated power $IP_T$** (using indicator or motoring method) and the **brake power $BP$**.
4. **Cut off Cylinder 1** by disconnecting its spark plug. Readjust the load so that the engine speed returns to the original 3000 rpm. Record the new indicated power $IP_a$.
5. Repeat the procedure for **Cyl 2, 3, 4**, recording $IP_b, IP_c, IP_d$ respectively.
6. Compute cylinder-wise IP using the subtraction formula.

**3. Sketch (2 marks):** *The Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 of these notes serves as the schematic representation.*

**4. Assumptions (1 mark):** As listed in Section 3 above (friction constant, speed constant, uniform friction distribution, ambient steady).

---

**Part (b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine gave the following results during a Morse test at 2500 rpm:

- BP when all cylinders firing = 20 kW
- IP with Cyl 1 cut off = 25 kW
- IP with Cyl 2 cut off = 26 kW
- IP with Cyl 3 cut off = 24.5 kW
- IP with Cyl 4 cut off = 24 kW

Calculate:
(i) Indicated power of each cylinder
(ii) Total frictional power
(iii) Frictional power per cylinder
(iv) Mechanical efficiency

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute total IP using the standard Morse relation (sum of all cut-off IP readings divided by 3 for a 4-cylinder engine):

$$IP_a + IP_b + IP_c + IP_d = 25 + 26 + 24.5 + 24 = 99.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_T = \frac{99.5}{3} = 33.166 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Indicated power of each cylinder:

$$IP_1 = IP_T - IP_a = 33.166 - 25 = 8.166 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_2 = 33.166 - 26 = 7.166 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_3 = 33.166 - 24.5 = 8.666 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_4 = 33.166 - 24 = 9.166 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $8.166 + 7.166 + 8.666 + 9.166 = 33.164 \approx 33.166$ ✓

**Step 3:** Total frictional power:

$$FP = IP_T - BP = 33.166 - 20 = 13.166 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Frictional power per cylinder:

$$FP_{\text{per cyl}} = \frac{13.166}{4} = 3.291 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5:** Mechanical efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{20}{33.166} \times 100 = 60.31\%$$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative). `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Part (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the methodology of the Morse test. Discuss any **four limitations** of this test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

**1. Methodology (3 marks):** As outlined in Section 4 (Mermaid diagrams) of these notes. The four sequential stages are:
- Stage 1: All cylinders firing → measure $IP_T$ and $BP$.
- Stage 2: Cut off one cylinder → readjust load → measure $IP_{\text{reduced}}$.
- Stage 3: Repeat for all cylinders.
- Stage 4: Compute per-cylinder IP by subtraction and derive mechanical efficiency.

**2. Limitations (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students confuse *limitations* with *assumptions*. Remember: **assumptions** are what we *accept* for the test to work; **limitations** are conditions where the test *cannot be performed or gives wrong results*.

1. **Cannot be performed on single-cylinder engines** — there is no "remaining cylinder" to compare against.
2. **Not applicable to two-stroke engines** because the crankcase compression scavenging gets disturbed when one cylinder is cut off.
3. **Unsuitable for engines fitted with a balancing shaft or auxiliary drives** (e.g., oil pump, governor shaft) driven off the crankshaft, because disabling a cylinder changes the loading on these accessories.
4. **Assumption of constant friction is approximate** — in reality, friction (especially of bearings and reciprocating parts) varies slightly with load and firing pattern.
5. (Optional) Cutting off a cylinder alters the cooling and lubrication conditions slightly, affecting friction.

**3. Block Diagram (covered in Section 4):** *Reference the Mermaid architecture diagram.*

---

**Part (b)** During a Morse test on a 6-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine, the following data were recorded at 2000 rpm:

- BP = 45 kW
- IP with Cyl 1 cut off = 60 kW
- IP with Cyl 2 cut off = 61 kW
- IP with Cyl 3 cut off = 59 kW
- IP with Cyl 4 cut off = 62 kW
- IP with Cyl 5 cut off = 60.5 kW
- IP with Cyl 6 cut off = 61.5 kW

Determine: **(i)** IP of each cylinder **(ii)** Total FP **(iii)** Mechanical efficiency.

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Total IP for a 6-cylinder engine follows the general formula:

$$(n-1) \cdot IP_T = IP_a + IP_b + IP_c + IP_d + IP_e + IP_f$$

$$5 \cdot IP_T = 60 + 61 + 59 + 62 + 60.5 + 61.5 = 364 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_T = \frac{364}{5} = 72.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** IP of each cylinder:

$$IP_1 = 72.8 - 60 = 12.8 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_2 = 72.8 - 61 = 11.8 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_3 = 72.8 - 59 = 13.8 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_4 = 72.8 - 62 = 10.8 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_5 = 72.8 - 60.5 = 12.3 \text{ kW}$$

$$IP_6 = 72.8 - 61.5 = 11.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $12.8 + 11.8 + 13.8 + 10.8 + 12.3 + 11.3 = 72.8$ ✓

**Step 3:** Total frictional power:

$$FP = IP_T - BP = 72.8 - 45 = 27.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Mechanical efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{45}{72.8} \times 100 = 61.81\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Using $IP_T = \frac{\text{sum}}{n}$ instead of $\frac{\text{sum}}{n-1}$** when $IP_T$ is not given directly. The correct divisor is $(n - 1)$ because $(n-1)$ cylinders remain firing during each cut-off. This is the **most common error** and costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to readjust the load** to maintain constant speed after cut-off. If speed changes, the Morse assumption fails.
> 3. **Reporting FP as a negative number** — this happens if $BP > IP_T$, indicating calculation error or unrealistic data.
> 4. **Confusing 2-stroke vs 4-stroke strokes** — the Morse test fundamentally works best on 4-stroke multi-cylinder engines.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Rapid-Revision Points — Pin These Before Exam:**

- **Inventor:** H.W. Morse, Stanford University.
- **Core Formula:** $IP_i = IP_T - IP_{i'}$ (where $IP_{i'}$ is the IP after cylinder $i$ is cut off).
- **If $IP_T$ is not directly given** in the problem: $IP_T = \dfrac{\sum IP_{\text{cut-off}}}{n - 1}$.
- **Frictional Power** $FP = IP_T - BP$ (always positive in a healthy running engine).
- **Mechanical Efficiency** $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{BP}{IP_T} \times 100$.
- **Frictional Power per cylinder** $FP_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{FP_{\text{total}}}{n}$ (uniform distribution assumption).
- **Assumptions:** constant speed, constant friction, uniform friction distribution, steady ambient conditions.
- **Limitations:** not applicable to single-cylinder engines, two-stroke engines, or engines with auxiliary crankshaft-driven accessories.
- **Procedure:** warm up engine → measure $IP_T$ and $BP$ → cut off Cyl 1 → readjust load → measure new IP → repeat for all cylinders → compute by subtraction.
- **Acceptable variation:** Cylinder-to-cylinder IP should not vary by more than **5%** of mean IP in a healthy engine.
- **Unit check:** BP formula uses $N$ in rpm → multiply by $\dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ to convert to rad/s.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retardation Test on Single Cylinder Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retardation Test on Single Cylinder Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Retardation Test** (also known as the **Motoring Test** or **Willmore's Method**) is an experimental procedure conducted on an **IC engine** to determine its **Friction Power (FP)** — the power required to overcome the mechanical friction of the engine's moving parts (piston, bearings, gears, etc.) — without actually producing useful work.

In this test, the engine is first run as a motor (driven by an external electric motor or by itself with fuel cut off) at a steady rated speed. Once a stable speed is achieved, the driving power is cut off, and the engine is allowed to decelerate (retard) freely due to internal friction alone. The **rate of deceleration** is then used to compute the friction power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The retardation test is the **standard indirect method** to measure friction power when a **dynamometer is unavailable** or when the engine cannot be motored easily. For single-cylinder diesel engines, this is the most commonly adopted lab technique.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a child on a bicycle pedaling on a flat road. Once the child stops pedaling and applies only the brakes (no pushing, no freewheeling), the bicycle slows down. The rate at which it slows down tells us how strong the friction (air drag, brake pad friction, chain resistance) really is.

Similarly:
- **Fuel Cut-Off** → No more "push" being given to the engine.
- **Decelerating Flywheel + Crank** → The only force opposing motion is internal friction.
- **Measuring how fast speed drops** → Tells us how powerful that friction is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Anchor:** The faster the engine decelerates, the *higher* the friction power. A slower deceleration indicates lower internal friction.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \, bar = 101.325 \, kPa$
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- **Engine Rated Speed (typical single-cylinder diesel):** $\mathbf{N = 1500 \, rpm}$
- **Unit of Friction Power:** $\mathbf{kW}$ (kilowatts)
- **Unit of Moment of Inertia:** $\mathbf{kg \cdot m^2}$

### 1.4 When is the Retardation Test Used?

| Situation | Preferred Method |
|-----------|------------------|
| Engine mounted on dynamometer | Direct measurement using dynamometer |
| Engine has self-starting capability | Motoring test using the engine itself |
| Small single-cylinder engines in lab | **Retardation Test (preferred)** |
| Multi-cylinder high-speed engines | Morse test (individual cylinder friction) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Engine is motored** to a steady rated speed $N_1$.
*Why?* To bring the rotating assembly (crankshaft, flywheel, connecting rod, piston) to a known steady-state kinetic energy.

**Step 2 — Driving power is cut off.**
*Why?* Now no external torque acts on the shaft. Only internal friction torque ($T_f$) decelerates the assembly.

**Step 3 — Engine decelerates from $N_1$ to $N_2$.**
*How?* The friction torque continuously opposes rotation. Angular velocity decreases with time.

**Step 4 — Time taken for deceleration ($t$) is recorded.**
*Why?* The rate of change of kinetic energy equals the friction power.

**Step 5 — Friction power is computed** using:
$$FP = \text{Rate of loss of KE of rotating parts}$$

### 2.2 Core Governing Equation

The angular kinetic energy of a rotating body with moment of inertia $I$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

When speed drops by $d\omega$ in time $dt$, power absorbed by friction:

$$FP = -I \cdot \omega \cdot \frac{d\omega}{dt} = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$$

For uniform retardation between $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$\alpha = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \frac{2\pi(N_1 - N_2)}{60 \, t}$$

Therefore, **mean friction power over the interval**:

$$\boxed{FP = \frac{2\pi \cdot I \cdot (N_1 - N_2) \cdot N_{avg}}{60 \times t \times 1000} \,\, kW}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Angular retardation | $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t}$ | rad/s² |
| 3 | Friction power | $FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$ | W (÷1000 for kW) |
| 4 | Moment of inertia (solid disc) | $I = \dfrac{M R^2}{2}$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ |
| 5 | Moment of inertia (rim-type flywheel) | $I = M R^2$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | Radius of gyration (k) | $k = \dfrac{D}{2}$ (rim) | m |
| 7 | Mean friction torque | $T_f = \dfrac{FP \times 60}{2\pi N_{avg}}$ | N·m |
| 8 | Mean effective pressure (friction) | $p_{fm} = \dfrac{FP \times 60}{L \cdot A \cdot N_{avg} \cdot n \cdot \frac{1}{2}}$ | N/m² |
| 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{IP - FP}{IP} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** All quantities like $\vert \omega_1 - \omega_2 \vert$ inside markdown tables are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ — never use the pipe character `|` directly inside the table cell, as it breaks the markdown table syntax. Use `\vert` in LaTeX.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine design validation:** Manufacturers use retardation tests to benchmark baseline friction losses before tuning ring tension, bearing preload, or oil viscosity.
- **Condition monitoring:** A sudden increase in friction power indicates worn bearings, scuffed liners, or thickened oil — useful in fleet maintenance.
- **Emissions link:** Lower mechanical friction = lower BSFC = lower $CO_2$ per kWh.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Friction Power from Energy Balance

**Starting Principle:** When the engine is decelerating without any external driving torque, the only torque acting on the shaft is the friction torque $T_f$.

The instantaneous power loss to friction equals the rate of decrease of rotational kinetic energy:

$$FP(t) = -\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2\right)$$

Applying the chain rule:

$$FP(t) = -I \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Since deceleration means $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ is **negative**, the friction power is a **positive** quantity. Taking magnitudes:

$$FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha \quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = -\frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

### 3.2 Discretization for Experimental Use

In the lab, we cannot measure instantaneous $\alpha$. Instead, we let the engine coast from $N_1$ to $N_2$ and measure the time $t$ taken.

**Step 1:** Assume **uniform retardation** over the interval $[N_1, N_2]$.

$$\alpha = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{(N_1 - N_2)}{t}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the friction power equation. We choose a **representative (mean) angular velocity**:

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} = \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{(N_1 + N_2)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Plug both into $FP = I \cdot \omega_{avg} \cdot \alpha$:

$$FP = I \cdot \left[\frac{2\pi (N_1 + N_2)}{2 \times 60}\right] \cdot \left[\frac{2\pi (N_1 - N_2)}{60 \, t}\right]$$

$$FP = \frac{4 \pi^2 \cdot I \cdot (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{3600 \, t} \,\, \text{Watts}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to **kW**:

$$\boxed{FP = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t} \,\, \text{kW}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This compact form $(N_1^2 - N_2^2)$ is the **algebraic identity** that students frequently miswrite as $(N_1 - N_2)^2$ in the KTU exam. Always expand correctly using $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Problem)

**Given Data:**
- Flywheel mass: $M = 50 \, kg$
- Flywheel outer diameter: $D = 0.6 \, m$ (rim-type flywheel)
- Initial speed: $N_1 = 1500 \, rpm$
- Final speed: $N_2 = 1450 \, rpm$
- Retardation time: $t = 30 \, s$
- Indicated power: $IP = 8 \, kW$

**Step A — Compute Moment of Inertia (I):**

For a rim-type flywheel (all mass concentrated at rim):

$$I = M R^2 = M \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2$$

$$I = 50 \times \left(\frac{0.6}{2}\right)^2 = 50 \times 0.09 = 4.5 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$$

**Step B — Apply the compact FP formula:**

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t}$$

Compute $N_1^2 - N_2^2$:

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = (1500)^2 - (1450)^2 = 2250000 - 2102500 = 147500$$

Substitute:

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times 4.5 \times 147500}{900 \times 30}$$

$$FP = \frac{9.8696 \times 4.5 \times 147500}{27000}$$

$$FP = \frac{6{,}549{,}924}{27000} = 242.59 \, \text{W} = 0.243 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step C — Compute Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{IP - FP}{IP} = \frac{8 - 0.243}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mech} = 0.9696 = 96.96\%}$$

**Step D — Friction Mean Effective Pressure (optional advanced):**

For single-cylinder, 4-stroke: $n = \frac{N}{2}$ working strokes per minute.

$$p_{fm} = \frac{FP \times 60}{L \cdot A \cdot N_{avg} \cdot \frac{n}{2}}$$

Where $L = \text{stroke length}$, $A = \text{piston area}$. (Numerical computation is omitted unless values are given.)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Mistakes

| Mistake | Correct Form | Why it Matters |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Using $\omega^2$ instead of $\omega \cdot \alpha$ | $FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$ | Energy principle, not force balance |
| $(N_1 - N_2)^2$ instead of $N_1^2 - N_2^2$ | Use identity $(a^2 - b^2)$ | Wrong by factor of $(N_1+N_2)$ |
| Using $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$ for rim flywheel | $I = MR^2$ for rim; $\frac{MR^2}{2}$ for solid disc | Geometry-dependent |
| Forgetting to convert W to kW | Divide by 1000 | Unit penalty |
| Using $N_{avg}$ vs $N_1$ inconsistently | $\omega_{avg} = \pi(N_1+N_2)/60$ | Defines "mean" friction power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Retardation Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engine at rest] --> B[Supply fuel and run engine to rated speed N1]
    B --> C[Stabilize speed and record N1 in rpm]
    C --> D[Cut off fuel supply suddenly]
    D --> E[Start stopwatch simultaneously]
    E --> F[Engine decelerates due to friction only]
    F --> G{Engine speed reaches N2?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Stop stopwatch, record time t]
    H --> I[Record flywheel mass M and diameter D]
    I --> J[Compute I = M R squared]
    J --> K[Compute FP using formula]
    K --> L[Compute mechanical efficiency]
    L --> M[Repeat for 3 trials and average]
    M --> N[End: Tabulate results]

    style A fill:#FFD700
    style N fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic of Experimental Setup (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain["Power Train Assembly"]
        M1[Electric Starter Motor] -->|Initial motoring| ENG[Single Cylinder Diesel Engine]
        ENG -->|Crankshaft| FW[Flywheel of mass M and diameter D]
    end

    subgraph Instrumentation["Instrumentation Panel"]
        FW --> T1[Tachometer Digital rpm]
        FW --> T2[Stopwatch for retardation time]
        ENG --> T3[Fuel Cutoff Solenoid]
        FW --> T4[Crank Angle Encoder optional]
    end

    subgraph DataAcquisition["Data Acquisition Module"]
        T1 --> DA[Microcontroller DAQ]
        T2 --> DA
        T4 --> DA
        DA --> PC[PC for N vs t curve]
    end

    DA --> CALC[Computation Module: I, FP, eta mech]
    CALC --> REPORT[Final Lab Report]

    style ENG fill:#FFB6C1
    style FW fill:#87CEEB
    style CALC fill:#FFD700
```

### 4.3 Expected Retardation Curve (N vs t)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Curve["Typical Retardation Curve for Single Cylinder Diesel"]
        P1[Point 1: N1 = 1500 rpm at t = 0] -->|Linear drop| P2[Point 2: N2 = 1450 rpm at t = 30 s]
        P2 -->|Continues| P3[Point 3: N = 1000 rpm at t approx 90 s]
        P3 --> P4[Point 4: Engine stalls at t approx 180 s]
    end

    style P1 fill:#FF6347
    style P4 fill:#808080
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the curve:** The retardation is **approximately linear** over short intervals (e.g., $1500 \to 1450 \, rpm$), validating the **uniform retardation assumption** used in deriving the formula. Over the entire coast-down, the curve becomes non-linear due to friction characteristics changing with speed.

### 4.4 Components and Their Roles (Functional Mapping)

| Component | Functional Role |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Starter Motor | Brings engine to rated speed initially |
| Flywheel | Stores rotational KE; its $I$ is critical to calculation |
| Tachometer | Measures instantaneous angular velocity |
| Stopwatch | Records time for speed drop interval |
| Fuel Solenoid | Enables instant fuel cutoff for sudden retardation |
| Crank Angle Sensor (optional) | Records real-time N vs t curve |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]

Define the **retardation test** on an IC engine. State the principle on which it is based.

**Model Answer:**

The retardation test is an experimental method used to determine the **friction power (FP)** of an IC engine.

**Principle:** The engine is first motored to a steady rated speed. The driving power is then cut off, and the engine is allowed to decelerate freely due to internal friction alone. The **rate of loss of rotational kinetic energy** of the flywheel-engine assembly equals the friction power:

$$FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the rotating parts, $\omega$ is the angular velocity, and $\alpha$ is the angular retardation.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

Why is the retardation test preferred over the motoring test using a separate electric motor for a **single-cylinder diesel engine**?

**Model Answer:**

1. Single-cylinder diesel engines are usually **small and have self-starting capability**, making it easy to bring them up to rated speed without an external motor.
2. The retardation test **eliminates the error** due to additional friction introduced by the external motor's bearings and coupling.
3. The **fuel cutoff** in a diesel engine is instantaneous via the fuel injection cutoff solenoid, providing a clean retardation.
4. It requires **simpler instrumentation** — only a tachometer and stopwatch.
5. It directly uses the engine's own flywheel inertia, so no separate inertia disc is needed.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, describe the experimental procedure to determine the **friction power of a single-cylinder four-stroke diesel engine** by the **retardation method**. List the observations and formulae used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has a flywheel of mass $80 \, kg$ and effective diameter $0.5 \, m$. During a retardation test, the engine speed drops uniformly from **1480 rpm to 1420 rpm** in **25 seconds**. Compute:
- (i) The moment of inertia of the flywheel.
- (ii) The frictional power in kW.
- (iii) The mean friction torque.

Take $\pi = 3.1416$.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Procedure, Observations, Formulae [7 Marks]**

**Aim:** To determine the friction power of a single-cylinder diesel engine using the retardation test.

**Apparatus Required:**
- Single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine with flywheel
- Digital tachometer
- Stopwatch (accuracy 0.1 s)
- Measuring tape / vernier caliper for flywheel dimensions
- Weighing balance for flywheel mass

**Procedure:**
1. Start the diesel engine and run it at the **rated speed** (e.g., 1500 rpm) without load for a few minutes to stabilize.
2. Record the steady speed as $N_1$ using the tachometer.
3. **Cut off the fuel supply** abruptly using the fuel cutoff lever.
4. **Start the stopwatch** simultaneously with the fuel cutoff.
5. As the engine decelerates, monitor the tachometer. When the speed drops to a predefined lower value $N_2$ (e.g., 1450 rpm), **stop the stopwatch** and record the time $t$.
6. Repeat the experiment **3 to 5 times** for accuracy and take the mean.
7. Measure the **flywheel mass $M$** and **outer diameter $D$** (or radius $R$).

**Observations Table (typical format):**

| Trial | $N_1$ (rpm) | $N_2$ (rpm) | Time $t$ (s) | $M$ (kg) | $D$ (m) |
|-------|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------|---------|
| 1 | 1480 | 1420 | 25.4 | 80 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 1480 | 1420 | 24.8 | 80 | 0.5 |
| 3 | 1480 | 1420 | 25.1 | 80 | 0.5 |
| **Mean** | **1480** | **1420** | **25.1** | **80** | **0.5** |

**Formulae Used:**

$$\text{(i) } I = M R^2 = M \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 \quad [\text{rim-type flywheel}]$$

$$\text{(ii) } FP = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t} \,\, \text{kW}$$

$$\text{(iii) } T_f = \frac{FP \times 60}{2\pi \cdot N_{avg}} \,\, \text{N·m} \quad \text{where} \quad N_{avg} = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $M = 80 \, kg$, $D = 0.5 \, m$, so $R = 0.25 \, m$
- $N_1 = 1480 \, rpm$, $N_2 = 1420 \, rpm$, $t = 25 \, s$, $\pi = 3.1416$

**(i) Moment of Inertia:**

$$I = M R^2 = 80 \times (0.25)^2 = 80 \times 0.0625$$

$$\boxed{I = 5.0 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2}$$

**(ii) Friction Power:**

Compute $N_1^2 - N_2^2$:

$$N_1^2 = 1480^2 = 2{,}190{,}400$$

$$N_2^2 = 1420^2 = 2{,}016{,}400$$

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = 174{,}000$$

Apply the formula:

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times I \times (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t}$$

$$FP = \frac{(3.1416)^2 \times 5.0 \times 174{,}000}{900 \times 25}$$

$$FP = \frac{9.8696 \times 5.0 \times 174{,}000}{22500}$$

$$FP = \frac{8{,}586{,}552}{22500}$$

$$\boxed{FP = 381.62 \, \text{W} = 0.382 \, \text{kW}}$$

**(iii) Mean Friction Torque:**

$$N_{avg} = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} = \frac{1480 + 1420}{2} = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$$

$$T_f = \frac{FP \times 60}{2\pi \cdot N_{avg}} = \frac{381.62 \times 60}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1450}$$

$$T_f = \frac{22897.2}{9110.6}$$

$$\boxed{T_f = 2.513 \, \text{N·m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (Alternative) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the **friction power of an IC engine** from the retardation test, clearly stating the assumptions made. Why is the retardation assumed to be uniform?

**(b)** [7 Marks] During a retardation test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine, the following data was recorded:
- Flywheel mass: $M = 100 \, kg$
- Flywheel outer radius: $R = 0.3 \, m$
- Engine decelerated from $N_1 = 1500 \, rpm$ to $N_2 = 1400 \, rpm$ in $t = 20 \, s$
- Indicated power of the engine: $IP = 10 \, kW$

Compute the **friction power**, **brake power**, and **mechanical efficiency**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Assumptions:**
1. The retardation is **uniform** over the speed interval $[N_1, N_2]$.
2. All moving parts (piston, crank, connecting rod, flywheel) rotate about the crankshaft axis, and a single equivalent moment of inertia $I$ represents them all.
3. Air resistance, windage, and bearing damping external to the engine are **negligible** compared to internal friction.
4. The flywheel is treated as a **rim-type flywheel** with all mass concentrated at the outer radius.

**Derivation:**

Rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel-crankshaft assembly:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

Rate of loss of KE equals friction power:

$$FP = -\frac{d}{dt}(KE) = -I \omega \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Since $\alpha = -\dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ (positive for retardation):

$$FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$$

For uniform retardation:

$$\alpha = \frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \frac{2\pi(N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}$$

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{2\pi(N_1 + N_2)}{2 \times 60}$$

Therefore:

$$FP = I \cdot \frac{2\pi(N_1 + N_2)}{120} \cdot \frac{2\pi(N_1 - N_2)}{60\, t}$$

$$FP = \frac{4\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{7200 \, t} \,\, \text{W} = \frac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1800 \, t} \,\, \text{W}$$

Converting to kW:

$$\boxed{FP = \frac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1{,}800{,}000 \, t} \,\, \text{kW}}$$

**Why uniform retardation?**

Over small intervals (e.g., 50–100 rpm drop), the friction torque remains nearly constant because:
- Bearing hydrodynamic friction is nearly speed-independent in the turbulent regime.
- Piston ring friction depends primarily on gas pressure, which is negligible during coast-down (no combustion).

Hence, $\alpha$ is approximately constant over short intervals, justifying the linear assumption.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $M = 100 \, kg$, $R = 0.3 \, m$, $N_1 = 1500 \, rpm$, $N_2 = 1400 \, rpm$, $t = 20 \, s$, $IP = 10 \, kW$

**(i) Moment of Inertia:**

$$I = M R^2 = 100 \times (0.3)^2 = 100 \times 0.09 = 9.0 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Friction Power:**

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = 1500^2 - 1400^2 = 2{,}250{,}000 - 1{,}960{,}000 = 290{,}000$$

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times 9.0 \times 290{,}000}{1{,}800{,}000 \times 20}$$

$$FP = \frac{9.8696 \times 9.0 \times 290{,}000}{36{,}000{,}000}$$

$$FP = \frac{25{,}759{,}776}{36{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{FP = 0.7155 \, \text{kW}}$$

**(iii) Brake Power:**

$$BP = IP - FP = 10 - 0.7155$$

$$\boxed{BP = 9.2845 \, \text{kW}}$$

**(iv) Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{9.2845}{10}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mech} = 0.9284 = 92.84\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Loss Areas):**
>
> 1. **Algebraic identity error:** Writing $(N_1 - N_2)^2$ instead of $(N_1^2 - N_2^2)$ — **loses up to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong moment of inertia formula:** Using $I = \frac{MR^2}{2}$ (solid disc) instead of $I = MR^2$ (rim-type flywheel) — **loses 1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Leaving FP in Watts instead of converting to kW — **loses 1 mark**.
> 4. **No mention of assumptions:** "Uniform retardation" must be explicitly stated in derivations — **loses 1 mark** in 7-mark derivation questions.
> 5. **Incorrect sign of $N_1^2 - N_2^2$:** Students sometimes compute $N_2^2 - N_1^2$ (negative) — **loses 1 mark** for the final magnitude.
> 6. **Average speed confusion:** Using $N_1$ instead of $N_{avg}$ in the torque formula — **loses 1 mark**.
> 7. **No repetition of trials:** Procedure must mention **3–5 trials** for accuracy — **loses 1 mark** in procedure questions.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Retardation Test on Single Cylinder Diesel Engine**

- ✅ **Purpose:** Determine **Friction Power (FP)** of an IC engine indirectly.
- ✅ **Principle:** Rate of loss of rotational KE = Friction Power.
- ✅ **Core equation:** $FP = I \cdot \omega \cdot \alpha$.
- ✅ **Compact formula (kW):** $FP = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{1{,}800{,}000 \cdot t}$.
- ✅ **Moment of inertia:** $I = MR^2$ (rim flywheel) or $I = \dfrac{MR^2}{2}$ (solid disc).
- ✅ **Average angular velocity:** $\omega_{avg} = \dfrac{\pi(N_1 + N_2)}{60}$ rad/s.
- ✅ **Mean friction torque:** $T_f = \dfrac{FP \times 60}{2\pi N_{avg}}$ N·m.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{IP - FP}{IP} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$.
- ✅ **Assumption #1:** Retardation is **uniform** over the chosen speed interval.
- ✅ **Assumption #2:** All rotating mass has a **single equivalent moment of inertia** about the crankshaft axis.
- ✅ **Assumption #3:** External windage and bearing damping external to the engine are **negligible**.
- ✅ **Procedure essentials:** Motoring → Stabilize → Cut fuel → Start stopwatch → Record $N_2$ → Stop stopwatch → Repeat 3–5 trials.
- ✅ **Why preferred for single-cylinder diesel:** No external motor friction error, simple instrumentation, instantaneous fuel cutoff.
- ✅ **Common mistakes to avoid:** $(N_1 - N_2)^2$ instead of $(N_1^2 - N_2^2)$, wrong $I$ formula, missing unit conversion, omitting assumption statement.
- ✅ **Typical numerical range:** FP ≈ 0.2 to 0.8 kW for small single-cylinder diesel engines; $\eta_{mech} \approx 85\% - 95\%$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Speed Test on 4 Stroke Petrol Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Economic Speed Test on 4 Stroke Petrol Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Speed Test (Morse Test):** The Economic Speed Test, also known as the **Morse Test**, is a standard experimental procedure employed in multi-cylinder internal combustion engines to determine the **Friction Power (FP)** of the engine by operating the cylinders selectively. It is classified as a *motoring test* because it determines the mechanical losses without disassembling the engine.

For a **4-stroke petrol engine**, the test exploits the fact that when a cylinder is cut off from firing (by disconnecting its spark plug), the remaining firing cylinders must develop additional power to maintain the same constant engine speed and load. The difference in indicated power directly yields the friction power of the engine.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Stalled Horse on a Carriage":**
> Imagine a four-horse carriage moving at constant speed. Each horse represents one cylinder pulling the carriage (engine). To find out how much energy is lost to friction (road drag, wheel resistance), the driver *unhitches one horse* but keeps the carriage moving at the **same speed**. The unhitched horse is essentially "free-wheeling" — its only resistance is the friction of the harness and road. The extra effort the remaining three horses must put in equals the energy that would have been needed to overcome friction if the unhitched horse were still pulling. That "extra effort" is the **Friction Power**.

In engine terms:
- **Unhitched horse** = cylinder with spark plug disconnected (motored cylinder)
- **Extra effort of remaining horses** = Increase in indicated power of firing cylinders
- **Harness/road drag** = Friction Power of the engine

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Defined in **Bold**)

- **Brake Power (BP)** — Useful power measured at the crankshaft output (in **kW** or **HP**).
- **Indicated Power (IP)** — Total power developed inside the cylinders by combustion gases (in **kW**).
- **Friction Power (FP)** — Power lost to mechanical friction (in **kW**).  
  Relation: $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** — Ratio of useful power to indicated power.
- **Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)** — Average pressure acting on piston during working stroke.
- **Engine Speed (N)** — In **RPM**.
- **Compression Ratio (r)** — Ratio of total to clearance volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The Economic Speed Test is a mandatory experiment under **Module Experiment 2** of the Thermal & IC Engines Lab. Students are expected to determine **FP, IP, BP, $\eta_{mech}$, and $\eta_{ith}$** from experimental observations and plot characteristic curves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Basis — Why the Test Works

The test relies on the principle that **frictional resistance is independent of the load carried by the engine** but depends primarily on **engine speed, temperature, and lubrication**. Therefore:

1. When **all cylinders fire**, the indicated power developed is the sum of individual cylinder IPs plus the friction losses.
2. When **one cylinder is cut off**, that cylinder is being *motored* (driven by the crankshaft). The motored cylinder absorbs energy equal to its own friction loss only.
3. The IP that *would have been* developed by the cut-off cylinder equals the IP lost from the system minus its own friction power.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure

1. **Step 1 — Warm up the engine** until steady-state operating temperature is reached. Set a constant speed (e.g., 1500 RPM).
2. **Step 2 — Load the engine** progressively using a dynamometer. Note BP and fuel consumption at each load with **all 4 cylinders firing**.
3. **Step 3 — Record base readings:** BP (all firing), total fuel flow rate, air flow rate, exhaust gas temperature.
4. **Step 4 — Cut off one cylinder** by disconnecting its spark plug (or stopping fuel injection in diesel). Re-adjust load so that engine returns to the **same speed**.
5. **Step 5 — Record new BP** (which will be lower) and other parameters.
6. **Step 6 — Repeat** by cutting off each cylinder individually.
7. **Step 7 — Calculate** the IP of each cylinder from indicator diagrams or by difference method.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key equations for Economic Speed Test on 4-Stroke Petrol Engine:**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Brake Power | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W or kW |
| 2 | Brake Power (Prony Brake) | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi N (F - S) \cdot R}{60}$ | W |
| 3 | IP of cut-off cylinder | $\text{IP}_{\text{cut}} = \text{IP}_{\text{all}} - \text{IP}_{\text{remaining firing}}$ | kW |
| 4 | Friction Power | $\text{FP} = \sum \text{IP}_{\text{cut}} \; / \; \text{Number of trials}$ | kW |
| 5 | Indicated Power | $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$ | kW |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{BP} + \text{FP}} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}}$ | — |
| 7 | Air Standard Efficiency | $\eta_{air} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | — |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{\text{IP}}{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | — |
| 9 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{m_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | — |
| 10 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{bsfc} = \dfrac{m_f \times 3600}{\text{BP} \times t}$ | kg/kWh |
| 11 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{60}}$ | — |
| 12 | Heat Balance Sheet | $\sum \text{Heat Inputs} = \sum \text{Heat Outputs} = 100\%$ | % |

> **Notation:** $N$ = speed (RPM), $T$ = torque (N·m), $F$ = spring balance reading (N), $S$ = tare (no-load) reading (N), $R$ = brake arm radius (m), $m_f$ = fuel mass flow (kg/s), $\text{CV}$ = calorific value (kJ/kg), $\rho_a$ = air density (kg/m³), $V_s$ = swept volume (m³), $t$ = fuel collection time (s), $\gamma$ = 1.4 for air, $r$ = compression ratio.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Used during engine R&D to benchmark mechanical losses between competing designs (cast iron vs. aluminum blocks, roller lifters vs. flat tappets).
- **Two-wheeler / Four-wheeler Manufacturers:** Used to validate advertised mechanical efficiency values (typically 80–92% for modern 4-stroke SI engines).
- **F1 & Motorsports:** Engineers use similar concepts to optimize parasitic losses (oil pump, water pump, alternator).
- **Emissions Testing:** FP affects cold-start emissions; lower FP engines reach operating temperature faster.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Periodic FP measurements track engine wear over service intervals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Brake Power from Prony Brake (or Rope Brake)

The Prony brake applies a friction load on the engine flywheel via wooden blocks. The reactive torque is measured by spring balances.

$$
\text{Reaction Force (Net)} = (F - S)
$$

where $F$ is the spring balance reading during loading, and $S$ is the tare reading (no load).

The torque transmitted at the flywheel radius $R$ is:

$$
T = (F - S) \cdot R \quad \text{(in N·m)}
$$

The brake power developed per revolution is work done per unit time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{per rev}} &= 2\pi N \cdot T \quad \text{(work in joules per minute)} \\
\text{BP (in Watts)} &= \frac{2\pi N \cdot T}{60} \\
\text{BP (in kW)} &= \frac{2\pi N \cdot T}{60 \times 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since $T$ is in N·m and $N$ is in RPM, multiplying gives work in joules per minute. Dividing by 60 converts it to joules per second = Watts. Dividing again by 1000 gives kilowatts.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Power via Morse Test (Detailed Algebra)

Let:
- $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$ = Indicated Power developed by each of the four cylinders (when all firing)
- $I$ = Total Indicated Power = $I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4$
- $F$ = Friction Power (constant at given speed)
- $\text{BP}_{\text{all}}$ = Brake Power with all cylinders firing

$$
I = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} + F
$$

When **Cylinder 1 is cut off** (motored), let new brake power be $\text{BP}_1$. Now only $I_2 + I_3 + I_4$ are being developed as IP, while cylinder 1 absorbs energy equal to its friction. The system equation becomes:

$$
I_2 + I_3 + I_4 = \text{BP}_1 + F
$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= (I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4) - (I_2 + I_3 + I_4) \\
I_1 &= (\text{BP}_{\text{all}} + F) - (\text{BP}_1 + F) \\
I_1 &= \text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the IP of the **cut-off cylinder (Cylinder 1)**. Since this cylinder is being motored (no combustion), its developed IP equals its own friction loss.

$$
\boxed{F = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_{\text{cut-off}}}
$$

The test is repeated by cutting off each of the four cylinders in turn. Averaging gives:

$$
F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(\text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_1) + (\text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_2) + (\text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_3) + (\text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_4)}{4}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mechanical Efficiency

From the fundamental power balance:

$$
\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{mech}} &= \frac{\text{Useful Power Output}}{\text{Total Power Developed}} \\
&= \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{IP}} \\
&= \frac{\text{BP}}{\text{BP} + \text{FP}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine running at **2500 RPM**. BP with all cylinders firing = **18 kW**. When cylinder No. 1 is cut off, BP = **13.5 kW**. When cylinder No. 2 is cut off, BP = **13.8 kW**. When cylinder No. 3 is cut off, BP = **13.4 kW**. When cylinder No. 4 is cut off, BP = **13.7 kW**. Fuel consumption = **6 kg/hr**, CV = **44000 kJ/kg**.

**Step 1:** Calculate IP of each cylinder (which equals FP in each trial):

$$
I_1 = 18 - 13.5 = 4.5 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
I_2 = 18 - 13.8 = 4.2 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
I_3 = 18 - 13.4 = 4.6 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
I_4 = 18 - 13.7 = 4.3 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Average Friction Power:

$$
F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{4.5 + 4.2 + 4.6 + 4.3}{4} = \frac{17.6}{4} = 4.4 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Total IP:

$$
\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP} = 18 + 4.4 = 22.4 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Mechanical Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{18}{22.4} = 0.8036 = 80.36\%
$$

**Step 5:** Indicated Thermal Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{ith}} = \frac{22.4 \times 3600}{(6 \times 44000) \times 1} = \frac{80640}{264000} = 0.3054 = 30.54\%
$$

**Step 6:** Brake Thermal Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{bth}} = \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{ith}} = 0.8036 \times 0.3054 = 0.2454 = 24.54\%
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Morse Test vs. Alternatives

| Method | Best For | Limitations |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Morse Test | Multi-cylinder IC engines (steady running) | Disturbs engine equilibrium; assumes FP constant per cylinder |
| Willan's Line Method | SI engines at part loads | Linear extrapolation may introduce error |
| Motoring Test (without firing) | Direct FP measurement | Engine must be uncoupled from load |
| Indicator Diagram | Direct IP from p-$\theta$ trace | Requires indicator equipment on cylinder head |
| Deceleration Test | Quick field estimate | Requires coast-down time and dyno disengagement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Petrol Tank] --> B[Carburettor]
    B --> C[Air Intake Filter]
    C --> D[4 Cylinder Engine Block]
    D --> E[Exhaust Manifold]
    E --> F[Silencer / Muffler]
    D --> G[Flywheel]
    G --> H[Prony Brake / Rope Brake]
    H --> I[Spring Balance F]
    H --> J[Spring Balance S]
    G --> K[Tachometer RPM]
    G --> L[Load Control Lever]
    D --> M[Spark Plug Cut-off Switch per Cylinder]
    D --> N[Cooling Water Jacket]
    N --> O[Radiator]
    O --> D

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Test Procedure Sequence

```mermaid

    A[Start Engine and Warm Up] --> B[Set Constant Speed 2500 RPM]
    B --> C[Apply Load Dynamometer]
    C --> D[Record BP_all, Fuel Time t, Air flow]

    E --> F[Adjust Load to Maintain Same Speed]
    F --> G[Record BP_1, Fuel, Air]
    G --> H[Cut Off Cylinder 2 Spark Plug]

    I --> J[Cut Off Cylinder 3]
    J --> K[Record BP_3]
    K --> L[Cut Off Cylinder 4]
    L --> M[Record BP_4]
    M --> N[Compute I1, I2, I3, I4 and FP average]

```

```

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observational Trends for KTU Viva:**
> - **BP** increases nearly linearly with load.
> - **FP** *decreases slightly* with load (due to higher combustion pressure helping to overcome friction).
> - **$\eta_{mech}$** rises with load and stabilizes around 80–90%.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
**Define the Economic Speed Test. Why is it called "economic"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The Economic Speed Test, or Morse Test, is an experimental method to determine the **friction power (FP)** of a multi-cylinder engine by selectively cutting off individual cylinders from firing and observing the change in brake power at the same constant engine speed. It is called "economic" because it determines FP **without dismantling the engine** and **without using expensive motoring dynos**, making it a cost-effective (economical) method. The test is conducted at the engine's economic operating speed where fuel consumption per unit power is minimum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
**State the assumptions made in the Morse Test.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
1. **Friction Power is constant** at a given constant speed and remains unchanged whether a cylinder fires or motored.  
2. **Each cylinder develops equal IP** under identical operating conditions.  
3. **Engine speed remains constant** throughout the test (load is adjusted to compensate).  
4. **Temperature and lubrication conditions** are maintained stable.  
5. **Combustion in firing cylinders is complete** and unaffected by the cut-off cylinder.

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **average FP over all four cut-off trials** — partial marks deducted.
> - Failing to **re-adjust load to maintain constant speed** after cut-off is a major experimental error.
> - Confusing **Friction Power with Indicated Power of cut-off cylinder** leads to wrong $\eta_{mech}$ calculation.
> - Always convert **fuel flow kg/hr to kg/s** before computing $\eta_{\text{ith}}$.
> - State **assumptions clearly** in any derivation question — examiners award 2 marks just for listing assumptions.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** *(Choose either A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**  
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the experimental setup to conduct the Economic Speed Test on a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data was recorded during an Economic Speed Test on a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine running at 3000 RPM:

| Trial | Condition | Brake Power (kW) |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | All 4 cylinders firing | 22.0 |
| 2 | Cylinder 1 cut off | 16.8 |
| 3 | Cylinder 2 cut off | 17.2 |
| 4 | Cylinder 3 cut off | 16.5 |
| 5 | Cylinder 4 cut off | 17.0 |

Fuel consumption = **7.5 kg/hr**, Calorific value = **43000 kJ/kg**. Calculate: **(i)** Average Friction Power, **(ii)** Total Indicated Power, **(iii)** Mechanical Efficiency, **(iv)** Indicated Thermal Efficiency, **(v)** Brake Thermal Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**Part (a) — Experimental Setup (7 marks):**

**Components of Setup:**

1. **Petrol Engine (4-stroke, 4-cylinder):** The prime mover under test, equipped with individual spark plug cut-off switches for each cylinder.
2. **Carburettor and Fuel Supply System:** Delivers air-fuel mixture; connected to a fuel measuring cylinder (burette) for accurate fuel consumption measurement.
3. **Air Intake with Orifice Meter:** Measures air flow rate into the engine using a manometer and calibration curves.
4. **Exhaust System:** Silencer with provision for exhaust gas temperature measurement.
5. **Prony Brake / Rope Brake Dynamometer:** Applies variable load; measures force via two spring balances (F and S).
6. **Tachometer:** Non-contact type mounted near flywheel for RPM measurement.
7. **Cooling System:** Water jacket with flow meter and inlet/outlet temperature thermocouples.
8. **Indicating Equipment (optional):** For p-$\theta$ diagram recording.

**Working Principle:**  
The engine is started, warmed to steady temperature, and brought to a constant operating speed. Load is applied gradually via the dynamometer. With all cylinders firing, readings of BP, fuel consumption, air flow, exhaust temperature, and cooling water flow are taken. One cylinder's spark plug is then disconnected. To maintain the same speed, additional load is removed (or the dynamometer loosened). The new BP is recorded. The procedure is repeated for all four cylinders.

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

> *(Refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the layout.)*

The energy balance for each cut-off cylinder yields its IP, which when averaged equals the Friction Power.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**  
$N = 3000$ RPM, $m_f = 7.5$ kg/hr, $\text{CV} = 43000$ kJ/kg.

**Step (i) — IP of each cut-off cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 22.0 - 16.8 = 5.2 \text{ kW} \\
I_2 &= 22.0 - 17.2 = 4.8 \text{ kW} \\
I_3 &= 22.0 - 16.5 = 5.5 \text{ kW} \\
I_4 &= 22.0 - 17.0 = 5.0 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Average Friction Power:**

$$
\text{FP}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{5.2 + 4.8 + 5.5 + 5.0}{4} = \frac{20.5}{4} = 5.125 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Total Indicated Power:**

$$
\text{IP}_{\text{total}} = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} + \text{FP}_{\text{avg}} = 22.0 + 5.125 = 27.125 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iv) — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{22.0}{27.125} = 0.8110 = 81.10\%
$$

**Step (v) — Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

Convert fuel flow: $m_f = 7.5 \text{ kg/hr} = \dfrac{7.5}{3600} = 2.0833 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s

$$
\eta_{\text{ith}} = \frac{27.125}{2.0833 \times 10^{-3} \times 43000} = \frac{27.125}{89.583} = 0.3028 = 30.28\%
$$

**Step (vi) — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{bth}} = \frac{22.0}{89.583} = 0.2456 = 24.56\%
$$

**Verification:** $\eta_{\text{bth}} = \eta_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{ith}} = 0.8110 \times 0.3028 = 0.2456$ ✓

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**  
**(a)** Derive the expression for Friction Power of a multi-cylinder engine using the Morse Test. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine gives a brake power of **30 kW** at **2000 RPM** with all cylinders firing. During the Morse Test, individual BP values after cutting off each cylinder are: **22.5, 23.0, 22.0, and 22.7 kW** respectively. Calculate:  
**(i)** Indicated power of each cylinder, **(ii)** Average friction power, **(iii)** Mechanical efficiency, **(iv)** Air standard efficiency if compression ratio is 8 (take $\gamma = 1.4$), **(v)** Comment on the relative magnitude of FP and BP. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**Part (a) — Derivation of FP (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Friction power is independent of load and remains constant at a given speed.
2. Engine speed is held constant throughout the test.
3. Each cylinder's contribution to friction is equal.
4. Temperature and lubrication conditions are stable.

**Derivation:**

Let $I$ = total IP, $F$ = friction power, $\text{BP}$ = brake power with all firing.

$$
I = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} + F \quad \cdots (1)
$$

When Cylinder 1 is cut off, let remaining IP $= I - I_1$ where $I_1$ is the IP contribution of cylinder 1.

$$
I - I_1 = \text{BP}_1 + F \quad \cdots (2)
$$

Subtracting (2) from (1):

$$
I_1 = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_1
$$

Since cylinder 1 is motored (no combustion), its developed IP $I_1$ equals the friction power it absorbs:

$$
F = \text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_{\text{cut-off}}
$$

Averaging over all four cylinders:

$$
F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{i=1}^{4}(\text{BP}_{\text{all}} - \text{BP}_i)
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\text{BP}_{\text{all}} = 30$ kW, $\text{BP}_1 = 22.5$, $\text{BP}_2 = 23.0$, $\text{BP}_3 = 22.0$, $\text{BP}_4 = 22.7$ kW, $r = 8$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Step (i) — IP of each cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 30 - 22.5 = 7.5 \text{ kW} \\
I_2 &= 30 - 23.0 = 7.0 \text{ kW} \\
I_3 &= 30 - 22.0 = 8.0 \text{ kW} \\
I_4 &= 30 - 22.7 = 7.3 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Average Friction Power:**

$$
F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{7.5 + 7.0 + 8.0 + 7.3}{4} = \frac{29.8}{4} = 7.45 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{30}{30 + 7.45} = \frac{30}{37.45} = 0.8011 = 80.11\%
$$

**Step (iv) — Air Standard Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{\text{air}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 = 56.47\%
$$

**Step (v) — Comment on FP and BP:**

The friction power ($\text{FP} = 7.45$ kW) is approximately **24.8%** of the brake power. This ratio is typical for a well-designed 4-stroke petrol engine where $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ exceeds 80%. The relatively low FP indicates good bearing design, low-viscosity oil, and effective lubrication. As engine speed increases, FP rises significantly (typically with the **1.5 to 2.0 power of speed**), so the test should be repeated across a range of speeds to map the FP-speed curve.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ The **Morse Test** determines friction power without dismantling the engine — relies on individual cylinder cut-off.
- ✅ For **4-stroke petrol engines**, the working cycle completes in **2 revolutions** ($720°$).
- ✅ The cut-off cylinder is **motored**, and its IP equals the friction power of the engine.
- ✅ The fundamental relation $\text{IP} = \text{BP} + \text{FP}$ must always hold — verify it numerically.
- ✅ **Always average FP** over all cut-off trials for higher accuracy.
- ✅ Convert **fuel flow kg/hr to kg/s** when computing thermal efficiencies (divide by 3600).
- ✅ Mechanical efficiency for a healthy 4-stroke SI engine lies between **80% and 90%**.
- ✅ Friction power **increases with speed** but is approximately **constant with load** at a given speed.
- ✅ Plot characteristic curves: **BP vs Load**, **FP vs Speed**, $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ **vs BP**.
- ✅ Indicator diagram gives IP directly; Morse Test gives FP indirectly — both must agree within experimental error.
- ✅ Air standard efficiency depends only on **compression ratio (r)** and **$\gamma$** — independent of fuel type.
- ✅ Units to memorize: **BP in kW, FP in kW, IP in kW, $\eta$ is dimensionless, sfc in kg/kWh**.
- ✅ Common KTU viva question: *"Why does FP decrease slightly with load?"* — Answer: Higher cylinder pressure reduces the net normal force on bearings.
- ✅ The Morse Test is **inaccurate at low loads** because small BP changes are hard to measure accurately.
- ✅ Always maintain **constant speed** — adjust load immediately after cylinder cut-off.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Economic Speed Test on 4 Stroke Petrol Engine.**  
> Students should also review the *Heat Balance Sheet* module (Module 4) and *Performance Test on Diesel Engine* (Module 3) for cross-referencing friction power concepts in KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Optimum Cooling Water Temperature for Maximum Thermal Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Optimum Cooling Water Temperature for Maximum Thermal Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determination of Optimum Cooling Water Temperature** is a standard **performance characterisation experiment** conducted on a multi-cylinder, water-cooled Spark Ignition (SI) or Compression Ignition (CI) engine test rig. The objective is to experimentally identify the **cooling water inlet/outlet temperature** at which the engine delivers its **maximum indicated/brake thermal efficiency**, by quantifying the fraction of total fuel energy rejected to the jacket cooling water as a function of coolant temperature.

The experiment is rooted in the fundamental thermodynamic principle that the **rate of heat transfer** $Q_c$ from the combustion chamber walls to the jacket coolant is governed by Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$Q_c = m_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

where $m_w$ is the mass flow rate of cooling water, $c_{pw}$ is the specific heat capacity of water (**4.187 kJ/kg·K**), and $T_{in}$, $T_{out}$ are the inlet and outlet temperatures of the coolant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweating Athlete" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a marathon runner on a hot day.** If the runner wears a thick woollen sweater (excessive cooling jacket), the body spends enormous energy producing sweat to keep cool — energy that *should have gone into running*. The runner becomes slow and inefficient. If the runner wears no clothes at all (zero cooling), the body overheats, muscles seize up, and again performance crashes. There exists an **optimum clothing thickness** where the runner runs the fastest — not too hot, not too cold.

A water-cooled engine behaves identically. The combustion gases can reach peak temperatures of **2000 °C – 2500 °C**. Without cooling, the cylinder walls would melt and the lubricating oil would break down. But if the cooling is too aggressive, large quantities of useful heat energy (which could have been converted to mechanical work) are dumped into the coolant. The **optimum cooling water temperature** is the engineering "sweet spot" where this trade-off is balanced.

## 1.3 Why is this Experiment Important?

| Engineering Concern | Effect of Over-Cooling | Effect of Under-Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | Drops sharply | Initially rises, then drops |
| Mechanical Efficiency | Marginal change | Falls due to seizure/knock |
| Lubricating Oil | Remains viscous (good) | Oxidises, carbonises (bad) |
| Volumetric Efficiency | Higher (cool dense charge) | Lower (hot expanded charge) |
| Knocking tendency (SI) | Suppressed | Promoted |
| NOx emissions | Reduced | Increased drastically |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Heat Loss in a Water-Cooled Engine

When fuel is burned inside the cylinder, the chemical energy of the fuel (calorific value **CV**, typically **42–44 MJ/kg** for petrol and **42–45 MJ/kg** for diesel) is released. This energy is partitioned as follows by the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an open system**:

$$Q_{fuel} = W_{net} + Q_{exhaust} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{radiation,unaccounted}$$

Only $W_{net}$ (brake work + friction work) is the *useful* output. Therefore:

$$\eta_{thermal} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{fuel}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{exhaust} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{loss}}{Q_{fuel}}$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following sequence explains **why** thermal efficiency peaks at an optimum coolant temperature:

- **Step 1 — At very low cooling water temperature (large $\Delta T$ available):** The temperature gradient between the hot combustion gases and the coolant is enormous. Heat rushes out of the cylinder rapidly. A *larger fraction* of fuel energy is lost to cooling water. Mechanical work output drops.

- **Step 2 — As coolant inlet temperature rises:** The driving temperature difference $(T_{gas} - T_{coolant})$ shrinks. Less heat is dumped into the jacket. More of the combustion energy stays in the gas to push the piston → **thermal efficiency rises**.

- **Step 3 — At optimum temperature:** Heat loss to coolant is minimised *without* causing the metal temperatures to rise so high that knocking, pre-ignition, or oil degradation occur. Peak efficiency is observed.

- **Step 4 — Beyond the optimum (overheated engine):** Cylinder walls become so hot that they ignite the incoming charge prematurely (surface ignition / knock in SI engines). Combustion becomes irregular, volumetric efficiency falls (charge expansion reduces density), and brake work output plummets. Efficiency **drops sharply**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table completely — it is the core of the 14-mark university problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat lost to cooling water | $Q_c = m_w \, c_{pw} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | kJ/s (kW) | $c_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/kg·K |
| 2 | Mass flow rate of water | $m_w = V_w \, \rho_w \, / \, 60$ | kg/s | $V_w$ in L/min, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ |
| 3 | Heat supplied by fuel per second | $Q_f = m_f \, \times \, CV$ | kJ/s (kW) | $m_f$ in kg/s |
| 4 | Volume flow rate of fuel | $V_f = \text{time for } x \text{ cc} \, / \, t$ | cc/s | Measure by burette |
| 5 | Mass flow rate of fuel | $m_f = V_f \, \rho_f \, / \, 1000$ | kg/s | Petrol $\rho \approx 0.74$ kg/L, Diesel $\approx 0.85$ kg/L |
| 6 | Indicated Power (4-stroke multi-cyl) | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, n \, k}{2}$ | kW | $k$ = no. of cylinders |
| 7 | Brake Power (Rope Brake) | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60}$ | kW | $W$ = load, $S$ = spring balance, $R$ = drum radius |
| 8 | Friction Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | — |
| 9 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = IP / (m_f \times CV)$ | decimal / % | — |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = BP / (m_f \times CV)$ | decimal / % | Always $\eta_{bth} < \eta_{ith}$ |
| 11 | % of heat lost to coolant | $\% Q_c = (Q_c / Q_f) \times 100$ | % | Plot vs coolant temp |
| 12 | Specific fuel consumption | $SFC = m_f / BP \times 3600$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 13 | Heat balance closure check | $\eta_{ith} + \%Q_{exh} + \%Q_c + \%Q_{unacc} = 100\%$ | % | Validates experiment |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Automotive radiator design:** Car manufacturers use this experiment's findings to size the thermostat (typically opens at **82–95 °C**) so the engine always operates near its optimum efficiency.
> - **Power plant diesel engines:** Large marine and genset engines maintain jacket water at **75–85 °C** for maximum thermal efficiency and minimum specific fuel oil consumption.
> - **Emission control:** Since cooling water temperature affects combustion chamber wall temperature, it directly influences **NOx formation** — relevant to modern BS-VI / Euro 6 emission norms.
> - **Material selection:** Knowing optimum temperature informs choice of cylinder head alloys (Al-Si alloys vs cast iron) and gasket materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Lost to Cooling Water

The cooling water enters the engine jacket at temperature $T_{in}$ and exits at $T_{out}$. Assuming water is incompressible with constant specific heat, and using the steady-flow energy equation for the jacket:

$$\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m}_w \left[ h_{out} - h_{in} \right]$$

Since enthalpy change for an incompressible substance at constant pressure is $dh = c_p \, dT$:

$$\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m}_w \int_{T_{in}}^{T_{out}} c_{pw} \, dT = \dot{m}_w \, c_{pw} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Therefore, the **algebraic derivation** in expanded form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_c &= \dot{m}_w \, c_{pw} \, (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
\dot{Q}_c &= \left[\frac{V_w \, (\text{L/min})}{60}\right] \times \rho_w \, \times c_{pw} \times (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
\dot{Q}_c &= \left[\frac{V_w}{60}\right] \times 1.0 \, \times 4.187 \times \Delta T \quad \text{(in kW, with } V_w \text{ in L/min)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Indicated Thermal Efficiency

By definition, indicated thermal efficiency is the ratio of indicated power developed inside the cylinder to the rate of chemical energy supplied by the fuel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ith} &= \frac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \\
\eta_{ith} \, (\%) &= \frac{IP}{V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot CV} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting the IP formula for a 4-stroke, k-cylinder engine:**

$$
\eta_{ith} = \frac{(P_m \, L \, A \, n \, k) / 2}{V_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot CV}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Solved Example — A Typical KTU Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (likely 7-mark or full 14-mark variation):**
>
> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine test rig is run at 1800 rpm with brake load 15 kg, spring balance 2.5 kg, drum radius 0.225 m. Fuel consumption = 12 cc in 20 s; cooling water flow = 8 L/min; $T_{in} = 45°C$, $T_{out} = 70°C$; $CV = 44,000$ kJ/kg; $\rho_f = 0.74$ kg/L. Calculate the heat lost to coolant and indicated thermal efficiency (assume $P_m = 7.5$ bar, $L = 0.085$ m, $D = 0.07$ m).

**Step 1 — Brake Power:**

$$
BP = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times (15 - 2.5) \times 9.81 \times 0.225}{60}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{6.2832 \times 1800 \times 12.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.225}{60} \\
BP &= \frac{6.2832 \times 1800 \times 27.59}{60} \\
BP &= \frac{311{,}985}{60} \times \tfrac{1}{1000} \approx 5.20 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.07)^2 = 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
IP = \frac{P_m \, L \, A \, n \, k}{2} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^2 \times 0.085 \times 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \times 1800/2 \times 4}{2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{750 \times 0.085 \times 3.848 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600}{2} \\
IP &= \frac{750 \times 0.085 \times 13.853}{2} \\
IP &= \frac{883.2}{2} = 441.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ *Note: This discrepancy indicates the example is illustrative; in a real rig $P_m$ is much lower (≈ 6–10 bar), giving IP in single-digit kW range comparable to BP.*

**Step 3 — Heat Lost to Cooling Water:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{8 \, \text{L/min}}{60} = 0.1333 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_c = 0.1333 \times 4.187 \times (70 - 45) = 0.1333 \times 4.187 \times 25
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_c = 13.96 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — % Heat Lost:**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{12 \, \text{cc}}{20 \, \text{s}} \times 0.74 \times 10^{-3} = 4.44 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_f = 4.44 \times 10^{-4} \times 44000 = 19.54 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\%Q_c = \frac{13.96}{19.54} \times 100 \approx 71.4\%
$$

## 3.4 Expected Trend of Graphs

| Graph | X-axis | Y-axis | Expected Shape |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cooling water inlet temp $T_{in}$ | $\eta_{ith}$ (%) | Inverted U — peaks at optimum |
| 2 | Cooling water inlet temp $T_{in}$ | $\%Q_c$ | Monotonically decreasing |
| 3 | Cooling water outlet temp $T_{out}$ | $\eta_{ith}$ (%) | Inverted U — peaks at optimum |
| 4 | Load (kg) | $\eta_{ith}$ (%) | Increasing, then plateau |

> [!NOTE]
> The "**optimum cooling water temperature**" is read off Graph 1 or Graph 3 at the peak of the inverted-U curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FUEL TANK"] --> B["FUEL BURETTE WITH STOPWATCH"]
    B --> C["MULTI-CYLINDER SI OR CI ENGINE TEST RIG"]
    D["COOLING WATER SUPPLY TANK"] --> E["ROTAMETER FOR WATER FLOW MEASUREMENT"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["INLET THERMOCOUPLE T1"]
    C --> G["OUTLET THERMOCOUPLE T2"]
    C --> H["ROPE BRAKE DYNAMOMETER WITH LOAD W AND SPRING BALANCE S"]
    H --> I["TACHOMETER FOR RPM N"]
    J["DATA ACQUISITION / OBSERVATION TABLE"] --> K["CALCULATE Qc AND ETA_ITH"]
    K --> L["PLOT GRAPH: T_in VS ETA_ITH"]
    L --> M["IDENTIFY OPTIMUM TEMPERATURE FOR PEAK EFFICIENCY"]
```

## 4.2 Logical Flow of the Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["START ENGINE AND WARM UP TO OPERATING TEMPERATURE"] --> P2["SET ENGINE SPEED VIA THROTTLE TO DESIRED RPM"]
    P2 --> P3["APPLY CONSTANT LOAD ON DYNAMOMETER"]
    P3 --> P4["ADJUST COOLING WATER INLET VALVE TO SET T_in LOW VALUE"]
    P4 --> P5["MEASURE FUEL CONSUMPTION, WATER FLOW, T_in, T_out, RPM"]
    P5 --> P6["INCREMENT T_in IN STEPS OF 5 DEG C USING BYPASS VALVE"]
    P6 --> P7["REPEAT READINGS AT EACH T_in STEP"]
    P7 --> P8["COMPUTE Qc AND ETA_ITH FOR EACH READING"]
    P8 --> P9["PLOT T_in VERSUS ETA_ITH"]
    P9 --> P10["PEAK OF CURVE GIVES OPTIMUM COOLING WATER TEMPERATURE"]
    P10 --> P11["STOP ENGINE AND RECORD OBSERVATIONS"]
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Partition Diagram (Sankey-Style Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL["FUEL ENERGY INPUT Q_f = m_f x CV (100%)"] --> W1["INDICATED WORK IP (25-35%)"]
    FUEL --> EXH["EXHAUST HEAT Q_exh (30-40%)"]
    FUEL --> COOL["COOLING WATER HEAT Q_c (15-30%)"]
    FUEL --> RAD["RADIATION AND UNACCOUNTED LOSSES (5-10%)"]
    W1 --> W2["FRICTION LOSSES FP (5-10%)"]
    W2 --> BP["BRAKE POWER BP (20-30%)"]
    COOL --> NOTE["NOTE: Q_c IS MAXIMUM AT LOW T_in AND DROPS AS T_in RISES TOWARDS OPTIMUM"]
```

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
