# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define optimum cooling water temperature. Why is it necessary to determine it experimentally for an IC engine?**

**Model Answer:**
The **optimum cooling water temperature** is the specific jacket coolant temperature (inlet or outlet) at which the engine delivers its **maximum indicated thermal efficiency** under a given load and speed condition.

Experimental determination is necessary because:
1. **Heat lost to coolant is temperature-dependent** — at low coolant temperatures, heat loss is excessive and efficiency drops; at high temperatures, knocking, oil degradation, and reduced volumetric efficiency lower the output.
2. **No universal value exists** — optimum temperature depends on fuel type (SI vs CI), compression ratio, load, and combustion chamber design.
3. It validates the **thermostat setting** in production vehicles.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Newton's Law of Cooling as applied to an engine jacket. Write the expression for heat lost to cooling water.**

**Model Answer:**
Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of heat transfer between a solid surface and a surrounding fluid is **directly proportional** to the temperature difference between them.

For the engine jacket:

$$
\dot{Q}_c = m_w \, c_{pw} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

where $m_w$ is the mass flow rate of water in kg/s, $c_{pw} = 4.187$ kJ/kg·K, and $(T_{out} - T_{in})$ is the temperature rise of the coolant across the jacket.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the experimental procedure to determine the optimum cooling water temperature for a multi-cylinder water-cooled SI engine. List the instruments used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment on a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine, the following observations were recorded at constant load and speed:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Engine speed | 1500 rpm |
| Brake drum load (W – S) | 14 kg |
| Drum radius | 0.225 m |
| Fuel consumed | 10 cc in 15 s |
| Cooling water flow | 6 L/min |
| $T_{in}$ / $T_{out}$ | 50 °C / 72 °C |
| CV of fuel | 44 MJ/kg |
| $\rho_f$ | 0.74 kg/L |

Calculate: (i) Brake power, (ii) Heat lost to cooling water in kW, (iii) Percentage of heat lost to coolant. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**(a) Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Setup inspection** — Check engine oil level, coolant level, fuel supply, and ensure all instruments (tachometer, thermometers, burette, rotameter, dynamometer) are functional.

2. **Start-up** — Start the engine using the starter motor and allow it to **idle for 5–10 minutes** to reach steady-state warm-up temperature.

3. **Set operating conditions** — Adjust the throttle to obtain the **desired engine speed** (read on tachometer). Apply the **desired load** on the rope-brake dynamometer.

4. **Maintain constant water flow** — Adjust the inlet valve to keep the **cooling water flow rate constant** (read from rotameter).

5. **Vary inlet temperature** — Use a **bypass valve** mixing hot return water with cold supply water to gradually **raise $T_{in}$** in steps of 5 °C from about 40 °C to 90 °C.

6. **Record readings** at each step:
   - Engine speed $N$ (rpm)
   - Load $W$, spring balance $S$ (kg)
   - Fuel consumption: volume $x$ cc in time $t$ seconds
   - Cooling water flow $V_w$ (L/min)
   - $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$ (°C)

7. **Compute** $\dot{Q}_c$ and $\eta_{ith}$ for each reading using standard formulas.

8. **Plot** $T_{in}$ (x-axis) vs $\eta_{ith}$ (y-axis). The **peak of the curve** gives the optimum cooling water temperature.

**Instruments Used:**
- Rope-brake dynamometer
- Tachometer (digital / mechanical)
- Fuel burette + stopwatch
- Cooling water rotameter
- Thermometers / thermocouples at jacket inlet and outlet
- U-tube manometer (for IP via $P_m$)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Brake Power:**

$$
BP = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R g}{60 \times 1000} \, \text{(kW)}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 14 \times 0.225 \times 9.81}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{6.2832 \times 1500 \times 14 \times 0.225 \times 9.81}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{291{,}300}{60{,}000} \\
BP &= 4.855 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Heat Lost to Cooling Water:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = \frac{6}{60} = 0.1 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_c = 0.1 \times 4.187 \times (72 - 50) = 0.1 \times 4.187 \times 22
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_c = 9.211 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Percentage of Heat Lost:**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{10}{15} \times 0.74 \times 10^{-3} = 4.933 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_f = 4.933 \times 10^{-4} \times 44{,}000 = 21.71 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\%Q_c = \frac{9.211}{21.71} \times 100 = 42.43\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with sketches the variation of indicated thermal efficiency with cooling water temperature. What happens at temperatures above the optimum? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder diesel engine develops BP of 5.5 kW at 2000 rpm with a brake specific fuel consumption of 0.35 kg/kWh. Cooling water flow = 10 L/min; $T_{in} = 60°C$, $T_{out} = 78°C$. If $\eta_{ith} = 0.78 \times \eta_{bth}$, calculate: (i) IP, (ii) Friction power, (iii) Heat lost to coolant per second, (iv) Percent heat lost. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**(a) Variation of $\eta_{ith}$ with Cooling Water Temperature:**

As $T_{in}$ rises from a low value (≈ 40 °C), $\eta_{ith}$ **increases** because the temperature gradient between the gas and coolant shrinks, reducing the heat dumped into the jacket. The curve reaches a **maximum (optimum)** typically in the range **70–80 °C for SI engines** and **75–90 °C for CI engines**.

Beyond the optimum:

- **Knocking / surface ignition** occurs in SI engines due to hot combustion chamber walls.
- **Lubricating oil** oxidises, carbon deposits form, and piston rings may seize.
- **Volumetric efficiency drops** as the inducted charge is heated and expands.
- **Combustion becomes irregular**, indicated power falls, and efficiency **drops sharply**.

The curve is therefore an **inverted "U"** shape — the peak gives the optimum temperature.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Indicated Power:**

Given $\eta_{ith} = 0.78 \times \eta_{bth}$, and $BP = 5.5$ kW.

First, find fuel consumption:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{0.35 \times 5.5}{3600} = 5.347 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

Brake thermal efficiency (assume CV = 42 MJ/kg for diesel):

$$
\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{5500}{5.347 \times 10^{-4} \times 42000}
$$

$$
\eta_{bth} = \frac{5500}{22460} = 0.2449 \approx 24.49\%
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{ith} = 0.78 \times 0.2449 = 0.1910 \approx 19.10\%
$$

$$
IP = \eta_{ith} \times \dot{m}_f \times CV = 0.1910 \times 5.347 \times 10^{-4} \times 42000
$$

$$
IP = 4.288 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (ii) — Friction Power:**

$$
FP = IP - BP = 4.288 - 5.5 = -1.212 \text{ kW}
$$

> ⚠️ *Negative value indicates assumed data is inconsistent; in real KTU problems the relation is reversed (i.e., $\eta_{bth} = 0.78 \times \eta_{ith}$), giving a positive FP. The examiner will mark on the correct procedure, not on the unrealistic data.*

**Correct interpretation** — Apply $FP = IP - BP$ with positive value. If $\eta_{bth} < \eta_{ith}$, then $BP < IP$, and the correct framework is:

$$
IP = \frac{\eta_{ith}}{\eta_{bth}} \times BP = \frac{1}{0.78} \times 5.5 = 7.05 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
FP = 7.05 - 5.5 = 1.55 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Heat Lost to Coolant:**

$$
\dot{Q}_c = \frac{10}{60} \times 4.187 \times (78 - 60) = 0.1667 \times 4.187 \times 18
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_c = 12.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iv) — Percentage of Heat Lost:**

$$
\dot{Q}_f = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 5.347 \times 10^{-4} \times 42000 = 22.46 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\%Q_c = \frac{12.56}{22.46} \times 100 = 55.92\%
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–3 marks each:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $V_w$ must be converted from L/min to kg/s by dividing by 60. $CV$ must be in kJ/kg when $\dot{m}_f$ is in kg/s. **Always write the units explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Confusing inlet and outlet temperatures** — Students often swap $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$. The heat transfer direction is **from engine to water**, so $T_{out} > T_{in}$, and the formula is always $(T_{out} - T_{in})$.
>
> 3. **Missing the factor of 2 in IP formula** — For a 4-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every 2 revolutions, hence the denominator "2". Omitting this gives double the correct IP.
>
> 4. **Not including $g = 9.81$ m/s²** in rope-brake BP calculation when load is given in kgf. Convert kgf to N by multiplying by 9.81.
>
> 5. **Failing to state Newton's Law of Cooling** in part (a) theory answers — it is a 1-mark compulsory citation.
>
> 6. **Drawing graphs without axis labels, units, and a clear title** — lose 1 mark easily.
>
> 7. **Not commenting on the trend** — students compute values but forget to state that efficiency follows an *inverted U-curve* and identify the optimum on the plot.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Optimum cooling water temperature is the **coolant temperature at which $\eta_{ith}$ is maximum**.
- ✅ Heat lost to coolant: $\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m}_w \, c_{pw} \, (T_{out} - T_{in})$ — uses Newton's Law of Cooling.
- ✅ Specific heat of water = **4.187 kJ/kg·K**.
- ✅ $c_{pw}$ is constant because water is treated as an **incompressible** substance in this context.
- ✅ $\eta_{ith} = IP / (\dot{m}_f \times CV)$; $\eta_{bth} = BP / (\dot{m}_f \times CV)$.
- ✅ IP formula for 4-stroke k-cylinder engine: $IP = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, n \, k}{2}$.
- ✅ BP from rope brake: $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N (W-S) R g}{60 \times 1000}$ kW.
- ✅ Always convert L/min → kg/s by dividing by 60 (since $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³).
- ✅ Typical optimum coolant temperature: **SI engine ≈ 70–80 °C**, **CI engine ≈ 75–90 °C**.
- ✅ The variation of $\eta_{ith}$ with $T_{in}$ is an **inverted-U curve**.
- ✅ Heat balance closure check: $\eta_{ith} + \%Q_{exh} + \%Q_c + \%Q_{unacc} = 100\%$.
- ✅ Instrument list: rope brake, tachometer, fuel burette, stopwatch, rotameter, thermometers, manometer.
- ✅ Graphs to plot: (1) $T_{in}$ vs $\eta_{ith}$ (2) $T_{in}$ vs $\%Q_c$ (3) $T_{out}$ vs $\eta_{ith}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Viscosity Using Redwood Viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Viscosity Using Redwood Viscometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscosity** is defined as the property of a fluid (liquid or gas) that offers resistance to flow or to the relative motion of its internal layers. It arises from internal friction between fluid molecules and from molecular momentum exchange between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

In engineering thermodynamics and fluid mechanics, viscosity is quantified in two principal forms:

- **Dynamic (or Absolute) Viscosity (μ):** The tangential force per unit area required to maintain a unit velocity gradient between two parallel fluid layers. SI unit: **Pa·s** (N·s/m²); CGS unit: **poise (P)** or **centipoise (cP)** where 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** The ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid. SI unit: **m²/s**; CGS unit: **stoke (St)** or **centistoke (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

The relationship between the two is:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

where ρ is the fluid density in kg/m³.

The **Redwood Viscometer** is a standardized, efflux-type (orifice-type) instrument used widely in petroleum and lubricant engineering for measuring the **kinematic viscosity** of lubricating oils, fuel oils, and other industrial fluids. It works on the principle of measuring the time (in seconds) taken by a fixed volume (50 mL) of the fluid to flow through a standard orifice under isothermal conditions at a prescribed test temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Two standardized variants exist — **Redwood Viscometer No. 1** (used for low-to-medium viscosity fluids like light lubricating oils, kerosene, diesel) and **Redwood Viscometer No. 2** (used for high-viscosity fluids like heavy fuel oils, bitumen, cylinder oils). The choice of viscometer is governed by the efflux time being above or below 100 seconds.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pouring thick honey and water side-by-side on a slanted board. The water slides down quickly in a thin, fast sheet; the honey creeps down slowly in a thick, sluggish blob. Why? Because honey has **higher internal friction** — its molecules cling to each other and resist being "pulled apart" into a flowing sheet. This internal "stickiness" between fluid layers is **viscosity**.

The Redwood Viscometer works just like measuring how fast a fixed "blob" of oil drains through a small hole. A thin oil drains in, say, 30 seconds. A thick oil takes 200 seconds. We don't need to weigh anything or measure force — we just **time** the drainage. The longer the drainage time, the higher the kinematic viscosity of the oil.

Think of it like this: in a coffee shop, if espresso and a thick milkshake were poured through identical holes in a cup lid, espresso finishes in 2 seconds, the milkshake takes 30. The **time itself becomes a measure of viscosity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept to Remember:** Viscosity is NOT the same as density. Mercury is very dense (13.6 g/cm³) but has very low viscosity (it flows easily). Honey is moderately dense but extremely viscous. Viscosity = resistance to flow, not resistance to motion itself.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard test temperatures** (as per IS 1448 / ASTM D445): **27 °C (80 °F)**, **40 °C**, **60 °C**, **98.9 °C (210 °F)**, and **100 °C**.
- **Standard sample volume:** **50 mL** (collected in the Kohlrausch flask).
- **Standard orifice diameter (No. 1):** **1.62 mm**.
- **Standard orifice diameter (No. 2):** **3.80 mm**.
- **Gravity constant:** g = **9.81 m/s²**.
- **Density of water at 4 °C:** ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³** = **1 g/cm³** (used as reference for specific gravity calculations).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Redwood Viscometer

The Redwood Viscometer is governed by **Poiseuille's Law of Viscous Flow**, combined with empirical corrections for **kinetic energy** and **momentum** effects at the orifice exit.

When a fluid flows through a small orifice under gravity, the driving head is not constant (it decreases as the level in the cup drops). However, the apparatus is calibrated such that the **time of efflux of 50 mL** is empirically correlated to kinematic viscosity.

### 2.1.1 Theoretical Basis — Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

For laminar flow of a viscous fluid through a circular capillary of length *L* and radius *r* under a pressure difference ΔP:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

For the Redwood viscometer, the orifice behaves approximately like a short capillary, but with significant **kinetic energy correction (kinetic energy of the efflux jet)** and **momentum correction** because the orifice length is small compared to its diameter. Empirical constants in the standard formula account for these corrections.

### 2.1.2 Why Two Variants?

The Redwood No. 2 has a **larger orifice (3.80 mm vs 1.62 mm)** specifically so that high-viscosity fluids drain in measurable times (typically < 2000 s) without being impractically slow. Using No. 1 for thick oil would yield times exceeding 30 minutes, which is experimentally undesirable.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Redwood Viscometer No. 1 procedure follows these stages:

1. **Setup & Cleaning**: The cup is thoroughly cleaned, dried, and the jet (orifice) is inspected for blockage. The leveling bubble ensures the cup is vertical.
2. **Filling**: The oil sample is poured into the cup up to the **fixed filling mark** (so that the initial head above the orifice is standardized).
3. **Temperature Stabilization**: A water bath (or oil bath) surrounding the cup brings the sample to the desired test temperature. A thermometer (±0.5 °C accuracy) monitors this. The sample is stirred gently without introducing air bubbles, and held at temperature for **at least 5 minutes** for thermal equilibrium.
4. **Inserting the Stopper**: A ball-headed stopper (or index needle) is placed over the orifice from below to close it.
5. **Pre-Collection**: A 50 mL **Kohlrausch flask** is placed beneath the orifice.
6. **Timing the Efflux**: The stopper is lifted slightly to let oil fill the orifice, and the stopwatch is started the **moment the oil meniscus reaches the upper graduation mark** of the Kohlrausch flask.
7. **Stopping**: The stopwatch is stopped the moment the meniscus reaches the **lower 50 mL graduation mark**. The recorded time *t* (in seconds) is the **Redwood No. 1 Viscosity (in seconds)**.
8. **Repetition**: The procedure is repeated at least 3 times at the same temperature, and the **mean time** is used for viscosity calculation.
9. **Viscosity Calculation**: Empirical formulas (given in Section 2.3) convert time to kinematic viscosity in stokes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

The following table consolidates all essential formulas for this experiment. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants | Applicable Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = A t - \dfrac{B}{t}$ | Empirical Redwood No. 1 formula for kinematic viscosity | A = **0.0026**, B = **1.72** (t in seconds, ν in stokes) | **34 s ≤ t ≤ 100 s** |
| 2 | $\nu = 0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | Standard simplified form (same as above) | ν in **stokes (St)**; multiply by 100 for cSt | For efflux times between 34 and 100 s |
| 3 | $\nu = 0.027 t - \dfrac{20}{t}$ | Empirical Redwood No. 2 formula | ν in stokes; t in seconds | For t > **100 s** (viscous oils) |
| 4 | $\mu = \rho \nu$ | Dynamic viscosity from kinematic viscosity | μ in Pa·s; ρ in kg/m³; ν in m²/s | Universal |
| 5 | $\text{SG} = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity of oil | Dimensionless | At the test temperature |
| 6 | $\rho_{oil} = \text{SG} \times 1000$ | Oil density from specific gravity (water at 4 °C reference) | ρ in kg/m³ | — |
| 7 | $\nu_{cSt} = \nu_{St} \times 100$ | Conversion | 1 St = 100 cSt | — |
| 8 | $\nu_{m^2/s} = \nu_{cSt} \times 10^{-6}$ | Conversion to SI | 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s | — |
| 9 | $\nu_{Pa \cdot s} = \nu_{kg/m \cdot s} \times 1$ (when ρ in kg/m³, ν in m²/s) | SI consistency check | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Pathway:** The Redwood formula gives ν in **stokes**. The KTU exam may ask the answer in **cSt, m²/s, or Pa·s**. Master the conversion: ν (St) → ×100 → cSt → ×10⁻⁶ → m²/s → ×ρ → Pa·s.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kinematic viscosity measured by the Redwood viscometer is critical for:

- **Lubricant grading** (e.g., ISO VG 32, VG 46 oils used in hydraulic systems).
- **Fuel injection calibration** in diesel engines — fuel viscosity affects spray atomization, droplet size, and combustion efficiency.
- **Pipeline design** for transporting crude oil, heavy fuel oils.
- **Heat transfer** calculations (Reynolds number, Prandtl number depend on ν).
- **Pump selection and bearing design** (oil film thickness in journal bearings depends on viscosity).
- **ASTM Viscosity Index (VI) determination** — measures how viscosity of oil changes with temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why ν = At − B/t?

The Redwood viscometer is empirically calibrated. The theoretical basis comes from **Poiseuille's law with end corrections**. For laminar flow through a short orifice of length *L* and diameter *d*, the volumetric flow rate *Q* is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi d^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L_{eff}}$$

where *L_eff* = *L* + *n·d* is the effective length (with *n* being an empirical end-correction factor that accounts for vena contracta and momentum effects).

In the Redwood No. 1, the orifice is short, so kinetic energy correction dominates. The efflux time *t* for a fixed volume *V* = 50 mL is empirically correlated as:

$$\nu = A t - \frac{B}{t}$$

where:
- **A** accounts for the **viscous (Poiseuille) resistance** component — proportional to time.
- **B/t** accounts for the **kinetic energy correction** — inversely proportional to time, because faster flow (smaller t) causes more turbulent kinetic energy loss.

The constants **A = 0.0026** and **B = 1.72** were determined experimentally by Redwood himself and have been verified over a century of industrial use.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Step-by-Step Solution

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A sample of lubricating oil is tested in a Redwood Viscometer No. 1 at **60 °C**. The following observations are recorded:

| Trial | Time for 50 mL efflux (s) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 68.4 |
| 2 | 68.9 |
| 3 | 69.1 |

The specific gravity of the oil at 60 °C is **0.882**.

**Calculate:**
1. The mean Redwood No. 1 viscosity (in seconds).
2. The kinematic viscosity in **stokes** and **centistokes**.
3. The kinematic viscosity in **m²/s**.
4. The dynamic viscosity in **Pa·s** and **centipoise (cP)**.

### 3.2.2 Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Mean Efflux Time**

$$\bar{t} = \frac{68.4 + 68.9 + 69.1}{3} = \frac{206.4}{3} = 68.8 \text{ s}$$

Since **34 s ≤ 68.8 s ≤ 100 s**, Redwood No. 1 formula applies.

**Step 2: Kinematic Viscosity (Stokes)**

Using the empirical formula:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t}$$

$$\nu = (0.0026 \times 68.8) - \frac{1.72}{68.8}$$

$$\nu = 0.17888 - 0.02500$$

$$\nu = 0.15388 \text{ stokes}$$

**Step 3: Kinematic Viscosity (Centistokes)**

$$\nu = 0.15388 \times 100 = 15.388 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 4: Kinematic Viscosity (m²/s)**

$$\nu = 15.388 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5388 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Density of Oil**

$$\rho_{oil} = \text{SG} \times 1000 = 0.882 \times 1000 = 882 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Dynamic Viscosity (Pa·s)**

$$\mu = \rho \times \nu = 882 \times 1.5388 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\mu = 1.3574 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s} = 0.01357 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 7: Dynamic Viscosity (centipoise)**

$$\mu = 0.01357 \times 1000 = 13.57 \text{ cP}$$

### 3.2.3 Final Tabulated Result

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Mean time $\bar{t}$ | **68.8 s** |
| Kinematic viscosity ν (stokes) | **0.15388 St** |
| Kinematic viscosity ν (centistokes) | **15.388 cSt** |
| Kinematic viscosity ν (m²/s) | **1.5388 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |
| Density ρ | **882 kg/m³** |
| Dynamic viscosity μ (Pa·s) | **0.01357 Pa·s** |
| Dynamic viscosity μ (cP) | **13.57 cP** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Range Applicability of Formulas

The choice of formula depends on the efflux time. The following decision matrix is critical for KTU exam answers:

| Condition on Time (t) | Instrument | Formula | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t \lt 34 \text{ s}$ | Redwood No. 1 | Formula not strictly valid; use Saybolt or direct measurement | Very thin oils (rare) |
| $34 \text{ s} \leq t \leq 100 \text{ s}$ | **Redwood No. 1** | $\nu = 0.0026 t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | Lubricating oils, light fuels |
| $t \gt 100 \text{ s}$ | **Redwood No. 2** | $\nu = 0.027 t - \dfrac{20}{t}$ | Heavy fuel oils, bitumen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often blindly apply $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ for **all** times. If the efflux time is, say, 250 s, you **MUST** switch to Redwood No. 2 formula. Marks are deducted for incorrect formula selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Redwood Viscometer No. 1 Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oil Sample] --> B[Pour into Cup up to Filling Mark]
    B --> C[Heat via Water or Oil Bath to Test Temperature]
    C --> D[Insert Ball Stopper to Close Orifice]
    D --> E[Place Kohlrausch Flask 50 mL Below Jet]
    E --> F[Start Stopwatch as Meniscus Reaches Upper Mark]
    F --> G[Oil Efflux Through 1.62 mm Orifice]
    G --> H[Stop Stopwatch at Lower 50 mL Mark]
    H --> I[Record Efflux Time t in seconds]
    I --> J{Is t between 34 and 100 seconds}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Redwood No 1 Formula]
    J -->|No, t greater than 100| L[Apply Redwood No 2 Formula]
    K --> M[Calculate Kinematic Viscosity in stokes]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Convert to cSt and m squared per s]
    N --> O[Multiply by Density to Get Dynamic Viscosity]
    O --> P[Final Result mu in Pa s]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Architecture — Component Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1[Leveling Screws]

    end

        B3[Stirrer]
    end

        C2[Thermometer Pockets]
        C3[Jet Orifice 1.62 mm]
        C4[Ball Valve Stopper]
    end

    C3 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Procedural Flow Chart — Experimental Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Experiment] --> S1[Clean Cup and Jet Thoroughly]
    S1 --> S2[Verify Verticality via Spirit Level]
    S2 --> S3[Pour Oil up to Filling Mark]
    S3 --> S4[Stabilize Temperature for 5 Minutes]
    S4 --> S5[Insert Stopper and Position Flask]
    S5 --> S6[Begin Timing Efflux of 50 mL]
    S6 --> S7[Record Time t]
    S7 --> S8{Repeat Minimum 3 Trials}
    S8 -->|Yes| S3
    S8 -->|No| S9[Compute Mean Time]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Empirical Formula]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define kinematic viscosity. How is it measured using a Redwood Viscometer No. 1?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Kinematic viscosity (ν)** is defined as the ratio of the dynamic viscosity (μ) of a fluid to its density (ρ):

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Its SI unit is **m²/s**, and the CGS unit is **stoke (St)** or **centistoke (cSt)**, where 1 St = 100 cSt = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.

**Measurement using Redwood Viscometer No. 1:** The Redwood Viscometer No. 1 is an efflux-type viscometer. A fixed volume of oil (50 mL) is allowed to drain through a standard orifice of **1.62 mm diameter** under gravity at a controlled test temperature (typically 27 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, or 98.9 °C). The time *t* (in seconds) taken for 50 mL of oil to collect in a Kohlrausch flask is the **Redwood viscosity in seconds**. This time is then converted to kinematic viscosity using the empirical formula:

$$\nu = 0.0026 t - \frac{1.72}{t} \text{ stokes}$$

valid for $34 \text{ s} \leq t \leq 100 \text{ s}$. The dynamic viscosity is then obtained as $\mu = \rho \nu$.

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Redwood Viscometer No. 1 and No. 2. When is each used?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Redwood No. 1 | Redwood No. 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Orifice diameter | **1.62 mm** | **3.80 mm** |
| Sample volume | 50 mL | 50 mL |
| Applicable efflux time | 34 s to 100 s | > 100 s |
| Empirical formula | $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ (stokes) | $\nu = 0.027t - 20/t$ (stokes) |
| Application | Light lubricating oils, kerosene, diesel | Heavy fuel oils, bitumen, cylinder oils |

**Usage rule:** If the efflux time exceeds 100 s in Redwood No. 1, switch to No. 2 (larger orifice) so that the time becomes measurable in a practical range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B (A): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Redwood Viscometer No. 1 with a neat labeled diagram. **Discuss the empirical formula used.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a Redwood Viscometer No. 1 test at **40 °C**, three readings of 50 mL efflux time are recorded as: **85.2 s, 85.6 s, and 85.0 s**. The specific gravity of the oil at 40 °C is **0.890**. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in cSt and the dynamic viscosity in Pa·s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction & Working of Redwood Viscometer No. 1 (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
- **Oil cup (brass)**: A cylindrical cup of standard dimensions to hold the oil sample up to a fixed filling mark.
- **Standard jet/orifice**: A precisely bored hole of **1.62 mm diameter** at the bottom center of the cup. Made of stainless steel or brass with smooth internal finish.
- **Heating bath**: An outer jacket surrounding the cup is filled with water or oil. Equipped with a heating element, stirrer, and thermometer (±0.5 °C accuracy) to maintain uniform test temperature.
- **Spirit level and leveling screws**: Three leveling screws at the base ensure the jet is perfectly vertical so that flow is purely axial (no asymmetric drainage).
- **Ball-headed stopper (jet cover)**: A small metal sphere held by a wire that can be raised/lowered to start or stop the flow without disturbing the oil.
- **Kohlrausch flask (50 mL)**: A specially graduated receiving flask with two etched marks indicating **50 mL volume**.

**Working Principle:**
1. Clean the cup and orifice thoroughly to remove any residue or foreign particles.
2. Level the apparatus using the spirit level.
3. Fill the cup with the test oil up to the fixed mark.
4. Heat/cool the surrounding bath to bring the oil to the desired test temperature. Maintain for **5 minutes** with gentle stirring for thermal equilibrium.
5. Place the 50 mL Kohlrausch flask directly beneath the orifice.
6. Insert the ball stopper to close the jet.
7. Lift the stopper carefully; simultaneously start the stopwatch as the meniscus of the effluxing oil reaches the **upper 50 mL mark** on the Kohlrausch flask.
8. Stop the stopwatch the instant the meniscus touches the **lower 50 mL mark**.
9. Record time *t* (Redwood seconds). Repeat **3 times** for repeatability.

**Empirical Formula:**
The mean efflux time $\bar{t}$ is converted to kinematic viscosity (in stokes) using:

$$\nu = 0.0026 \bar{t} - \frac{1.72}{\bar{t}} \text{ stokes}$$

This formula is valid for $34 \text{ s} \leq \bar{t} \leq 100 \text{ s}$. It is empirical in nature, derived from Poiseuille's law with kinetic energy correction.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- Efflux times: 85.2 s, 85.6 s, 85.0 s
- Specific gravity = 0.890 at 40 °C

**Step 1: Mean time**

$$\bar{t} = \frac{85.2 + 85.6 + 85.0}{3} = \frac{255.8}{3} = 85.27 \text{ s}$$

Since 34 s ≤ 85.27 s ≤ 100 s, Redwood No. 1 formula applies.

**Step 2: Kinematic viscosity (stokes)**

$$\nu = (0.0026 \times 85.27) - \frac{1.72}{85.27}$$

$$\nu = 0.22170 - 0.02017 = 0.20153 \text{ stokes}$$

**Step 3: Convert to cSt**

$$\nu = 0.20153 \times 100 = 20.153 \text{ cSt}$$

**Step 4: Density of oil**

$$\rho_{oil} = 0.890 \times 1000 = 890 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Kinematic viscosity in m²/s**

$$\nu = 20.153 \times 10^{-6} = 2.0153 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Dynamic viscosity in Pa·s**

$$\mu = \rho \nu = 890 \times 2.0153 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\mu = 1.7936 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s} = 0.01794 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Kinematic viscosity ν = **20.15 cSt**
- Dynamic viscosity μ = **0.01794 Pa·s**

---

### **Question B (B): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI and CGS units. Explain why Redwood Viscometer is preferred for lubricating oils over capillary-type viscometers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heavy fuel oil gives a mean efflux time of **450 seconds** in Redwood Viscometer No. 2 at **98.9 °C**. The density of the oil at this temperature is **940 kg/m³**. Calculate the kinematic viscosity in **m²/s** and the dynamic viscosity in **centipoise (cP)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions & Preference of Redwood Viscometer (7 marks)**

**Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** It is defined as the tangential force per unit area required to sustain a unit relative velocity between two parallel fluid layers at unit distance apart. Mathematically:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ is the shear stress (N/m²) and $du/dy$ is the velocity gradient (s⁻¹).
- **SI unit:** Pa·s (= N·s/m²)
- **CGS unit:** poise (P); 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s; 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.

**Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- **SI unit:** m²/s
- **CGS unit:** stoke (St) or centistoke (cSt); 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s; 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

**Why Redwood Viscometer is Preferred for Lubricating Oils:**
1. **Simple operation**: Only the time of efflux is measured; no force, pressure, or weight measurement needed.
2. **Robust design**: No fragile capillary tube that can clog with viscous or dirty oils.
3. **Suitable for opaque fluids**: Unlike capillary viscometers that require transparent fluids for meniscus observation, the Redwood simply measures volume collected in a flask.
4. **Standardized for petroleum industry**: Direct empirical correlation with ISO VG grades used in lubrication.
5. **Temperature control**: Built-in bath allows easy isothermal testing at multiple standardized temperatures (27 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, 98.9 °C, 100 °C).
6. **Two variants** (No. 1 and No. 2) cover wide viscosity range from light kerosene to bitumen.
7. **Repeatability**: Industrial standard IS 1448 / ASTM D88 specifications ensure reproducibility across labs.

**(b) Numerical Solution — Redwood No. 2 (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- Mean efflux time $\bar{t}$ = 450 s
- Density ρ = 940 kg/m³
- Test temperature = 98.9 °C
- Since $t = 450 \text{ s} \gt 100 \text{ s}$, **Redwood No. 2 formula applies**.

**Step 1: Kinematic viscosity (stokes)**

$$\nu = 0.027 \bar{t} - \frac{20}{\bar{t}}$$

$$\nu = (0.027 \times 450) - \frac{20}{450}$$

$$\nu = 12.15 - 0.04444 = 12.1056 \text{ stokes}$$

**Step 2: Convert to m²/s**

$$\nu = 12.1056 \times 10^{-4} = 1.21056 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Dynamic viscosity in Pa·s**

$$\mu = \rho \nu = 940 \times 1.21056 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\mu = 1.13793 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to centipoise (cP)**

$$\mu = 1.13793 \times 1000 = 1137.93 \text{ cP}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Kinematic viscosity ν = **1.211 × 10⁻³ m²/s**
- Dynamic viscosity μ = **1137.93 cP** (or **1.138 Pa·s**)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Formula Selection Error (–2 marks):** Using No. 1 formula ($\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$) when time exceeds 100 seconds. Always verify: if $t \gt 100 \text{ s}$, switch to No. 2 formula. The examiner looks at this **first**.
>
> 2. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark each occurrence):** Mixing up stokes (St), centistokes (cSt), m²/s, Pa·s, poise, and centipoise. Remember the **master conversion chain:** St → ×100 → cSt → ×10⁻⁶ → m²/s → ×ρ → Pa·s → ×10 → poise → ×1000 → cP.
>
> 3. **Specific Gravity vs Density (–1 mark):** Forgetting that density = SG × 1000 (when water at 4 °C is reference). Density in kg/m³ is required for SI calculations; do not multiply SG × 1000 and then claim the answer is in g/cm³.
>
> 4. **Mean Time Calculation (–0.5 to 1 mark):** Reporting only one trial's time instead of the arithmetic mean of 3 trials.
>
> 5. **No 5-minute Equilibration Mention (–1 mark):** In procedure-based questions, forgetting to mention the 5-minute isothermal hold leads to marks lost.
>
> 6. **Dynamic vs Kinematic Mislabeling (–1 mark):** Writing μ = 0.153 stokes instead of ν = 0.153 stokes. Viscosity values must be clearly labeled with μ or ν.
>
> 7. **Not stating temperature:** Always mention the test temperature in the final answer — viscosity is **strongly temperature-dependent**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Redwood Viscometer Experiment:**
>
> - **Viscosity** = internal friction between fluid layers resisting flow. **Two types:** dynamic μ and kinematic ν, related by $\nu = \mu/\rho$.
>
> - **Redwood No. 1** has orifice **1.62 mm**, used when $34 \text{ s} \leq t \leq 100 \text{ s}$; formula: $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ stokes.
>
> - **Redwood No. 2** has orifice **3.80 mm**, used when $t \gt 100 \text{ s}$; formula: $\nu = 0.027t - 20/t$ stokes.
>
> - **Standard test temperatures:** 27 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, 98.9 °C, 100 °C. Sample is held for **5 minutes** at temperature for isothermal equilibrium.
>
> - **Sample volume** measured: **50 mL** in a Kohlrausch flask; timing starts at upper mark and stops at lower mark.
>
> - **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 St = 100 cSt = 10⁻⁴ m²/s. 1 Pa·s = 10 poise = 1000 cP.
>
> - **Density from SG:** $\rho_{oil} = \text{SG} \times 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (water at 4 °C as reference).
>
> - **Dynamic viscosity in SI:** $\mu (\text{Pa·s}) = \rho (\text{kg/m}^3) \times \nu (\text{m}^2/\text{s})$.
>
> - **Typical exam sequence:** Compute mean *t* → select correct formula → compute ν in stokes → convert to cSt and m²/s → compute ρ from SG → compute μ in Pa·s → optionally convert to cP.
>
> - **Engineering applications:** Lubricant grading (ISO VG), fuel injection calibration, pump selection, bearing design, viscosity index (VI) determination.
>
> - **Apparatus components to mention in descriptive answers:** Oil cup, jet/orifice, heating bath with stirrer, thermometer, leveling screws with spirit level, ball stopper, Kohlrausch flask.
>
> - **Experimental precautions to mention:** Vertical leveling, clean jet, no air bubbles, 5-min temperature equilibration, repeated trials (min 3), use of stopwatch with ±0.2 s accuracy, choosing correct viscometer (No. 1 vs No. 2) based on initial trial.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Multistage Reciprocating Air Compressor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Multistage Reciprocating Air Compressor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multistage Reciprocating Air Compressor** is a positive-displacement mechanical device that raises the pressure of air in **two or more successive stages**, with **intercooling** between stages, to achieve high delivery pressures (typically above 7–8 bar) at improved thermodynamic efficiency.

A **Performance Test** on such a compressor is a standardized laboratory experiment conducted to evaluate operational parameters such as:
- Actual volume of air delivered per unit time (V_a)
- Free air delivery (FAD)
- Isothermal and adiabatic (polytropic) work of compression
- **Isothermal efficiency (η_IT)**, **Volumetric efficiency (η_v)**, **Mechanical efficiency (η_m)**, and **Overall efficiency (η_overall)**
- Shaft power input and specific power consumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is mapped to Course Outcome **CO2** — *Apply thermodynamic principles to determine the performance of thermal and IC engines.* Bloom's Level: **Apply / Analyze (L3–L4)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine inflating a heavy-duty truck tire using a small bicycle pump:

- **Single-stage pump**: You push very hard, the air inside gets *very hot*, and after just a few strokes you cannot push any further because the back-pressure exceeds your strength. **Work is wasted as heat.**
- **Multistage pump**: After a few strokes, you *release* the air into an intermediate tank (the **intercooler**), let it cool down, then push it further in a second, smaller cylinder. The cool air is denser, easier to compress, and requires *less effort per kg of air*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Compressing hot air is hard and inefficient. Splitting the compression into stages and **cooling between stages** (intercooling) dramatically reduces the *total compression work* required for a given pressure rise.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm)** = **1.01325 bar** ≈ **1.013 × 10⁵ N/m²**
- **Specific heat ratio of air (γ)** = **1.4** (for isentropic compression)
- **Polytropic index (n)** = **1.3** (typically assumed in KTU problems for reciprocating compressors)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Gas constant for air (R_air)** = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- **Standard Free Air Condition:** T₀ = **15 °C (288 K)**, P₀ = **1.01325 bar**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clearance Volume (C)** is a *non-dimensional ratio* = (Clearance Volume / Swept Volume). Typical industrial values: **C = 2 % to 8 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

A multistage compressor operates in the following sequence:

1. **Induction (Stage 1):** Air at atmospheric pressure **P₁** and ambient temperature **T₁** enters the low-pressure (LP) cylinder through suction valves.
2. **Compression (Stage 1):** Air is compressed polytropically from **P₁** to **P_x** (intermediate pressure). Temperature rises from **T₁** to **T_x′**.
3. **Intercooling:** Hot compressed air passes through an **intercooler** (water-cooled heat exchanger). It is cooled back to **T₁** (perfect intercooling) at constant pressure **P_x**.
4. **Induction (Stage 2):** Cool, dense air at **P_x, T₁** enters the high-pressure (HP) cylinder.
5. **Compression (Stage 2):** Air is compressed polytropically from **P_x** to **P₂** (final delivery pressure).
6. **Delivery:** Compressed air at **P₂, T₂** is delivered to the receiver tank.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multistage?** High pressure ratios in a single stage cause (a) excessive discharge temperature, (b) large clearance losses, (c) mechanical stress, and (d) high work input. Multistaging + intercooling solves all four.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below contains **all** formulas required to solve KTU numerical problems on this experiment. Use `\vert` (not `\vert` pipes) to denote absolute values if needed elsewhere.

### Table 2.1 — Core Performance Formulas

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Polytropic work (single stage, no clearance) | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | J/cycle |
| 2 | Polytropic work (single stage, with clearance) | $W = \dfrac{P_1V_s}{n-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]\!\left[1+C - C\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}}\right]$ | J/cycle |
| 3 | **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}}$ | — (decimal) |
| 4 | **Optimum Pressure Ratio per stage (r_p,opt)** | $r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{N}}$ | — |
| 5 | Minimum total work for **N stages with perfect intercooling** | $W_{\text{min}} = N\,\dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{nN}} - 1\right]$ | J/cycle |
| 6 | Isothermal work (theoretical minimum) | $W_{IT} = P_1V_1\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | J/cycle |
| 7 | **Isothermal Efficiency (η_IT)** | $\eta_{IT} = \dfrac{W_{IT}}{W_{\text{actual (shaft)}}}\times 100$ | % |
| 8 | **Mechanical Efficiency (η_m)** | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{IHP}}{\text{BHP}}\times 100$ | % |
| 9 | **Free Air Delivery (FAD)** | $FAD = V_a \times \dfrac{P_d\,T_{atm}}{P_{atm}\,T_d}$ | m³/min |
| 10 | Overall isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{W_{IT}}{W_{\text{shaft}}}\times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Indicated Power (per cylinder) | $IP = \dfrac{W \times N_r \times k}{60}$ | kW |
| 12 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1\dot{V_1}}{R\,T_1}$ | kg/s |
| 13 | Discharge temperature after polytropic compression | $T_2 = T_1\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | K |
| 14 | Indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) | $P_{mi} = \dfrac{W}{V_s}$ | N/m² |

### Table 2.2 — Boundary Conditions & Assumptions (KTU Standard)

| Condition | Value / Statement |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Perfect intercooling assumption** | $T_{\text{after cooler}} = T_{\text{suction (Stage 1)}}$ |
| **Optimum condition criterion** | All stages must have *equal pressure ratio* |
| **Polytropic index for reciprocating compressor (KTU default)** | $n = 1.3$ |
| **Adiabatic index for air** | $\gamma = 1.4$ |
| **Standard free air density** | $\rho_0 = 1.225~\text{kg/m}^3$ at STP |
| **Clearance ratio (typical industrial)** | $C = 0.02~\text{to}~0.08$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Multistage compressors are the workhorses of **gas turbine starting systems**, **pneumatic tools in workshops**, **oil & gas pipeline boosting**, **CNG refueling stations**, **submarine ballast systems**, and **air-blast circuit breakers**. Performance testing ensures that warranty-bound efficiency claims are verified before commercial deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Work Done in a Multistage Reciprocating Compressor with Perfect Intercooling

### Step 1 — Work done in a single polytropic stage (without clearance)

For a single compression stroke following the law $PV^n = \text{constant}$, the boundary work is:

$$
W_1 = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,\mathrm{d}V
$$

Using $P = P_1 V_1^n \, V^{-n}$:

$$
W_1 = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n}\,\mathrm{d}V
$$

Performing the integration:

$$
W_1 = P_1 V_1^n \left[\dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2}
$$

$$
W_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n}\!\left(V_2^{\,1-n} - V_1^{\,1-n}\right)
$$

Applying the polytropic identity $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$, and substituting $V_2^{\,1-n} = V_1^{\,1-n}(P_1/P_2)^{(n-1)/n}$:

$$
\boxed{W_1 = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]}
$$

This is the **work input per cycle for one polytropic stage**.

### Step 2 — Work done in N stages with perfect intercooling

For **N identical stages** (each raising pressure from $P_i$ to $P_{i+1}$ by the same ratio $r_p$), and with perfect intercooling resetting the temperature back to $T_1$ after each stage, the work per stage is identical:

$$
W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[(r_p)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

Therefore, total work for N stages:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = N \times W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{N\,n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[(r_p)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Optimum pressure ratio

The overall pressure rise is $\dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_1} = (r_p)^N$.

Differentiating total work with respect to $r_p$ and setting $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}W_{\text{total}}}{\mathrm{d}r_p} = 0$ yields the optimum:

$$
r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{N}}
$$

Substituting this optimum back into the total-work expression:

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{N\,n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{final}}}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{nN}} - 1\right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At the optimum condition, **all stages share the load equally**. If one stage is forced to compress more than the others, its work input rises disproportionately, increasing the *total* shaft work — a classic KTU numerical trap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance

Consider the **P-V indicator diagram** of a reciprocating compressor with clearance volume $V_c$ and swept volume $V_s$:

- Effective suction volume $V_1 = V_s + V_c - V_4$
- Air at delivery pressure $P_2$ trapped in clearance $V_c$ expands along the polytropic expansion line $3\rightarrow 4$ to $V_4$.
- $V_4 = V_c \!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}}$

Effective swept volume delivering air at $P_1$:

$$
V_{\text{eff}} = V_s - (V_4 - V_c) = V_s - V_c\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

Dividing by swept volume $V_s$:

$$
\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}}}
$$

where $C = V_c / V_s$ is the **clearance ratio**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked KTU Numerical

**Problem:** A two-stage reciprocating compressor with perfect intercooling compresses air from **1 bar to 9 bar**. The suction temperature is **300 K**, and the polytropic index is **n = 1.3**. Determine:
(a) Optimum pressure ratio per stage  
(b) Minimum work of compression per kg of air  
(c) Volumetric efficiency of each stage (assume C = 0.04)

### Solution

**(a) Optimum pressure ratio**

For $N = 2$ stages:

$$
r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{N}} = \left(\dfrac{9}{1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{2}} = 3
$$

So **each stage compresses from 1 bar → 3 bar → 9 bar**.

**(b) Minimum work of compression per kg of air**

For 1 kg of air at inlet: $V_1 = RT_1/P_1 = (287 \times 300)/(1\times 10^5) = 0.861~\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$.

$$
W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{N\,n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{nN}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2 \times 1.3}{1.3 - 1}\times (1\times 10^5)\times 0.861\times\!\left[(9)^{\frac{0.3}{1.3\times 2}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
= \dfrac{2.6}{0.3}\times 86100 \times \left[9^{0.1154} - 1\right]
$$

$$
= 8.667\times 86100\times [1.2898 - 1]
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{min}} \approx 2.16 \times 10^5~\text{J/kg} = 216~\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**(c) Volumetric efficiency**

For each stage, pressure ratio = 3:

$$
\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04\,(3)^{1/1.3} = 1.04 - 0.04\times 3^{0.7692}
$$

$$
= 1.04 - 0.04\times 2.323 = 1.04 - 0.0929
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_v \approx 0.9471 \text{ or } 94.71\%}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two-Stage Compressor Test Rig

The diagram below maps the **complete experimental flow** of a two-stage reciprocating compressor test rig, showing all measurement nodes and inter-component interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC MOTOR DRIVE 3 PHASE] --> B[FLYWHEEL AND CRANKSHAFT ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[STAGE 1 LP CYLINDER SUCTION VALVE]
    C --> D[STAGE 1 LP CYLINDER POLYTROPIC COMPRESSION 1 BAR TO 3 BAR]
    D --> E[INTERCOOLER WATER JACKET HEAT EXCHANGER]
    E --> F[STAGE 2 HP CYLINDER SUCTION VALVE]
    F --> G[STAGE 2 HP CYLINDER POLYTROPIC COMPRESSION 3 BAR TO 9 BAR]
    G --> H[DELIVERY CHECK VALVE]
    H --> I[RECEIVER TANK WITH SAFETY VALVE]
    I --> J[DISCHARGE TO ATMOSPHERE VIA ORIFICE METER]
    J --> K[MANOMETER U TUBE FOR ORIFICE DP]
    I --> L[PRESSURE GAUGE PG1 DELIVERY 9 BAR]
    D --> M[PRESSURE GAUGE PG2 INTERMEDIATE 3 BAR]
    C --> N[VACUUM GAUGE OR SUCTION PRESSURE]
    D --> O[THERMOMETER T1 INTERCOOLER OUTLET]
    E --> P[THERMOMETER T2 INTERCOOLER WATER INLET]
    E --> Q[THERMOMETER T3 INTERCOOLER WATER OUTLET]
    A --> R[ENERGY METER FOR POWER INPUT]
    B --> S[TACHOMETER FOR RPM MEASUREMENT]
    I --> T[STOP WATCH FOR TANK METHOD]
```

## 4.2 Instrumentation Summary Table

| Node in Diagram | Instrument | Purpose |
|-----------------|------------|---------|
| L, M, N | Bourdon-type Pressure Gauges | Measure stage-wise pressures |
| O, P, Q | Mercury-in-glass Thermometers | Measure temperatures (air and cooling water) |
| K | U-tube Manometer | Measure orifice pressure differential Δh |
| R | Single-phase Energy Meter | Measure electrical power input to motor |
| S | Digital Tachometer | Measure crankshaft RPM |
| T | Stop Watch | Used in **tank method** for FAD calculation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Methods of FAD Measurement:**
> 1. **Orifice Meter Method** — preferred for continuous-running compressors; uses Bernoulli's equation with discharge coefficient $C_d$.
> 2. **Tank Method (Rising-Pressure Method)** — used for small compressors; receiver tank is charged from atmospheric to delivery pressure; FAD is computed from $V_{\text{tank}}\,\Delta P / (P_{\text{atm}}\,\Delta t)$ corrected to free air conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define isothermal efficiency and mechanical efficiency of a reciprocating air compressor.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Isothermal efficiency (η_IT)** is the ratio of the *theoretical isothermal work* (minimum possible work for an isothermal process) to the *actual work input delivered to the shaft* of the compressor.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$\eta_{IT} = \dfrac{W_{IT}}{W_{\text{shaft}}}\times 100 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)}{W_{\text{shaft}}}\times 100$$
>
> **Mechanical efficiency (η_m)** is the ratio of the *indicated power developed inside the cylinder* (IHP) to the *brake power delivered by the motor to the crankshaft* (BHP):
>
> $$\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{IHP}}{\text{BHP}}\times 100$$
>
> Isothermal efficiency quantifies the **thermodynamic quality** of compression; mechanical efficiency quantifies the **quality of mechanical power transmission** (bearings, crank-pin friction, etc.).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Why is intercooling employed between stages of a multistage compressor? List any two effects.**

**Model Answer:**

Intercooling is employed **between successive compression stages** to reduce the temperature of the partially compressed air before it enters the next stage.

**Two primary effects:**

1. **Reduction of compression work:** Cooling the air back to the suction temperature reduces its specific volume, so the *next stage compresses a denser fluid* — the total work input is significantly lowered (approaches the ideal isothermal work).

2. **Reduction of discharge temperature:** Without intercooling, a high overall pressure ratio in one stage would produce dangerously high discharge temperatures (lube-oil breakdown, valve seizure). Intercooling keeps discharge temperatures within safe limits.

> *(Bonus 3rd point for marks: improved volumetric efficiency of the succeeding stage, due to cooler and denser suction.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum work of compression in a multistage reciprocating air compressor with perfect intercooling. State the condition for optimum pressure distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from **1 bar to 8 bar**. The suction temperature is **310 K** and the polytropic index is **1.35**. Calculate:
   (i) Optimum pressure ratio per stage  
   (ii) Minimum work of compression per kg of air  
   (iii) Indicated power required for a delivery of **0.05 m³/s** at free air conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1:** For a single stage compressing polytropically from $P_1$ to $P_2$:

$$
W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** For N stages with equal pressure ratio $r_p$ and perfect intercooling, the work per stage is identical, so total work:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = N\cdot \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[(r_p)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 3:** The overall pressure ratio is $(P_2/P_1) = (r_p)^N$, hence $r_p = (P_2/P_1)^{1/N}$.

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{N\,n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\!\frac{n-1}{nN}} - 1\right]}
$$

**Condition for optimum pressure distribution:** All stages must compress air through the **same pressure ratio** (i.e., $r_p$ is identical for every stage). This is obtained by differentiating $W_{\text{total}}$ with respect to $r_p$ and equating to zero.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:** $P_1 = 1~\text{bar} = 10^5~\text{N/m}^2$; $P_2 = 8~\text{bar}$; $T_1 = 310~\text{K}$; $n = 1.35$; $N = 2$; $\dot V = 0.05~\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**(i) Optimum pressure ratio:**

$$
r_{p,\text{opt}} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{N}} = (8)^{1/2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828
$$

Each stage compresses **1 → 2.828 → 8 bar**.

**(ii) Minimum work per kg of air:**

$V_1 = RT_1/P_1 = (287)(310)/(10^5) = 0.8897~\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$

Exponent: $\dfrac{n-1}{nN} = \dfrac{0.35}{1.35\times 2} = 0.1296$

$$
W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.35}{0.35}\times 10^5 \times 0.8897 \times \left[8^{0.1296} - 1\right]
$$

$$
= 7.714\times 88970 \times [1.3471 - 1]
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\text{min}} \approx 2.38 \times 10^5~\text{J/kg} = 238~\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**(iii) Indicated Power:**

For 1 kg, $V_1 = 0.8897~\text{m}^3$. Hence the volume flow at suction $\dot V_1 = 0.05~\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ corresponds to mass flow:

$$
\dot m = \dfrac{\dot V_1 P_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 10^5}{287 \times 310} = 0.0562~\text{kg/s}
$$

Indicated Power:

$$
IP = \dot m \times W_{\text{min}} = 0.0562 \times 2.38 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\boxed{IP \approx 13.37~\text{kW}}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, explain the experimental setup and procedure for conducting the performance test on a two-stage reciprocating air compressor. List the instruments used and the readings recorded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define volumetric efficiency. Derive its expression for a reciprocating air compressor with clearance. A single-stage compressor has a clearance ratio of **5 %** and a pressure ratio of **5**. Find the volumetric efficiency. Take **n = 1.3**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Experimental Setup

Refer to the **block diagram in SECTION 4.1** for the complete flow.

**Line Diagram Description (text-based for writing in exam):**
A two-stage compressor has a **low-pressure (LP) cylinder** and a **high-pressure (HP) cylinder** driven from a common crankshaft through an electric motor. Air enters the LP cylinder through a suction filter and silencer, gets compressed, and is discharged into an **intercooler** (water-cooled shell-and-tube type). The cooled air then enters the HP cylinder, is further compressed, and delivered into the **receiver tank** through a delivery check valve.

**Instruments used:**
- Bourdon pressure gauges at suction, intermediate, and delivery
- Thermometers at intercooler air inlet/outlet and cooling water inlet/outlet
- Orifice meter with U-tube manometer (or receiver tank with stop-watch for FAD)
- Tachometer for RPM
- Energy meter for input power

**Procedure:**
1. Run the compressor at no-load for ~10 minutes to reach steady thermal state.
2. Open the delivery valve gradually to achieve a steady delivery pressure.
3. Note delivery pressure $P_d$, intermediate pressure $P_x$, suction pressure $P_s$.
4. Note temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_{w1}, T_{w2}$ and manometer reading Δh.
5. Note RPM (N) and time for 10 revolutions of energy meter disc (for power).
6. Repeat at 4–5 different delivery pressures.
7. Calculate FAD, η_v, η_IT, η_m for each load and plot performance curves.

---

#### Part (b) — Volumetric Efficiency Derivation & Numerical

**Derivation (summarised from Section 3.2):**

$$
\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{1}{n}}}
$$

**Numerical:**
Given $C = 0.05$, $P_2/P_1 = 5$, $n = 1.3$:

Exponent = $1/n = 1/1.3 = 0.7692$

$$
(5)^{0.7692} = 5^{0.7692} \approx 3.466
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05\times 3.466 = 1.05 - 0.1733
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_v = 0.8767 \text{ or } 87.67\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar → N/m²:** Many students use $P_1 = 1$ in formula directly, yielding wrong units. Always convert to SI (Pa) when using $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1}P_1V_1[\ldots]$.
> 2. **Mixing $n$ and $\gamma$:** The question explicitly states $n = 1.3$ (polytropic). Do **not** substitute $\gamma = 1.4$.
> 3. **Skipping the $V_1$ calculation:** $V_1$ per kg must come from the ideal gas equation $V_1 = RT_1/P_1$. Using $V_1 = 1~\text{m}^3$ by default is a guaranteed zero.
> 4. **Confusing FAD with actual delivered volume:** FAD is the *volume the delivered air would occupy at free air conditions*. Use $FAD = V_a \cdot \dfrac{P_d}{P_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_d}$.
> 5. **Optimum condition trap:** In a 2-stage problem with given $P_2/P_1 = 9$, students often wrongly compute $r_p = 9/2 = 4.5$ per stage instead of the geometric mean $r_p = \sqrt{9} = 3$.
> 6. **Missing units in final answer:** KTU strictly penalizes unit-less numerical answers — always write kJ/kg, kW, or % explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Multistage compression + intercooling** lowers total work input and reduces discharge temperature simultaneously.
- **Optimum condition = equal pressure ratio in every stage**; for N stages, $r_{p,\text{opt}} = (P_{\text{final}}/P_1)^{1/N}$.
- **Minimum total work formula:** $W_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{Nn}{n-1}P_1V_1\!\left[(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/(nN)} - 1\right]$ — **memorize** this; it is the single most-tested KTU formula for this module.
- **Perfect intercooling** means the air enters the next stage at the **original suction temperature** $T_1$.
- **Volumetric efficiency** drops with rising pressure ratio and rising clearance; for multistage, the LP cylinder has the **lowest** $\eta_v$ because its delivery ratio is largest.
- **Performance test instruments:** Pressure gauges (P), thermometers (T), orifice manometer (FAD), energy meter (power), tachometer (N).
- **Two FAD measurement methods:** Orifice meter (continuous flow) and **tank method** (small compressors, intermittent).
- **Efficiencies reported in KTU viva:** $\eta_v$, $\eta_{IT}$, $\eta_m$, $\eta_{\text{overall}}$; always tabulate them vs. delivery pressure.
- **Default KTU problem parameters:** $n = 1.3$, $C = 0.04$–$0.05$, air as ideal gas, perfect intercooling unless stated otherwise.
- **Indicator diagram area** corresponds to indicated work per cycle; **IMEP** = $W_{\text{cycle}}/V_s$.
- **Free air density** $\rho_0 = 1.225~\text{kg/m}^3$ at **15 °C, 1.01325 bar** — use only when explicitly stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Rotary Compressor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Test on Rotary Compressor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rotary Compressor:** A rotary compressor is a *positive-displacement* fluid (air) handling machine in which a rotating member (impeller, screw, vane, gear, or scroll) continuously traps, compresses, and discharges a fixed volume of working fluid per revolution. Energy is transferred to the fluid by a positive mechanical action, in contrast to a turbo-machine where energy transfer is by momentum exchange with a rapidly moving fluid stream. For laboratory testing under the KTU 2024 scheme, the most commonly evaluated unit is the **Roots-type** (two-lobe) **positive-displacement rotary blower/compressor** or the **vane-type** rotary compressor. The performance test quantifies the compressor's ability to deliver a specified volume/pressure of air against a given shaft input. ### 1.2 Classification of Rotary Compressors (Syllabus Snapshot) | Category | Representative Type | Compression Mechanism | |---|---|---| | **Positive Displacement (Rotary)** | Roots blower, Vane, Screw, Scroll, Lobe | Mechanical entrapment of fixed volume | | **Dynamic (Rotary / Turbo)** | Centrifugal, Axial | Kinetic energy conversion to pressure rise | | **Lab-Relevant Unit** | **Roots Blower (Two-Lobe)** | Trapping & external backflow suppression | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Mechanical Lung" of Industry > [!NOTE] > **The Bicycle Pump Analogy:** A bicycle pump compresses air by a *reciprocating* piston — push, push, push. A rotary compressor does the exact same job (suck → compress → deliver) but uses a *continuously spinning* element. Imagine turning a slowly-spinning screw inside a sealed chamber: every time the screw makes a turn, a pocket of air is sealed, squeezed, and squirted out. That is the heart of a rotary compressor — instead of "push–pull," it is "swirl, trap, and expel." > > In the **Roots blower**, two figure-8 shaped lobes (lobes A and B) interlock; the air caught between the lobe and the casing is *carried* from inlet to outlet without internal compression (it is discharged against the receiver pressure, causing internal back-flow that determines losses). ### 1.4 Standard Physical Quantities & Reference Constants - **Universal Gas Constant (Air):** $R = 0.287 \ \mathrm{kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$ - **Specific Heat Ratio (Air):** $\gamma = 1.4$ - **Polytropic Index (typical for compression):** $n = 1.30 \ \text{to} \ 1.35$ - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \ \mathrm{kPa} = 1.01325 \ \mathrm{bar}$ - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298 \ \mathrm{K} \ (25^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ ### 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme — Course Outcome Mapping | CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | Topic Coverage | |---|---|---| | **CO2** | Conduct performance tests on thermal/RAC equipment and interpret results | ✓ Direct evaluation of efficiency indices | | **CO3** | Apply thermodynamic relations to compute work and efficiency | ✓ Isothermal & adiabatic work derivations | | **CO5** | Document laboratory results as per industrial standards | ✓ Tabulation of test data and graphs | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Roots-Type Rotary Compressor (Lab Unit) The Roots blower works on the principle of **"external compression"** — the trapped volume is not internally reduced; rather, the air is *carried* from the suction side to the delivery side, where it is suddenly exposed to the receiver pressure $P_2$. The actual compression occurs by backflow through the small clearances between the lobes and the casing. > **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** > 1. The two-lobe rotor pair is driven at high speed (typically 1500 – 3000 rpm) by an electric motor coupled through a belt drive or directly. > 2. At the **suction port** (low-pressure side, $P_1$), lobes A and B rotate; a fixed pocket of air $V_1$ is trapped between the lobe surface and the casing. > 3. The pocket is **transported** (without reduction) to the **delivery port** (high-pressure side, $P_2$). > 4. At the delivery port, the trapped volume $V_1$ is suddenly back-flowed into the receiver (which is at $P_2 > P_1$) until its pressure rises to $P_2$. The clearance leakage and the sudden backflow represent the **compressor losses**. > 5. The compressed air is delivered into a receiver tank fitted with a **pressure gauge** and a **safety valve**. ### 2.2 Components of the Experimental Test Rig A standard KTU-lab rotary compressor test rig consists of: - **Electric Motor** (prime mover, with a wattmeter to measure input electrical power). - **Rotary Compressor Unit** (Roots blower / twin-lobe / vane). - **Receiver Tank** (pressure storage, fitted with a pressure gauge, safety valve, and drain cock). - **Orifice Meter** placed in the discharge line (with a U-tube / differential manometer) to measure the actual mass flow rate of air. - **Thermometers** at the inlet ($T_1$) and outlet ($T_2$) of the compressor. - **Atmospheric Pressure Barometer** for absolute inlet pressure measurement. - **Tachometer** to measure the compressor speed $N$ (rpm). - **Loading Valve / Throttle Valve** to vary the receiver pressure $P_2$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **mandatory** for the KTU 2024 lab viva and end-semester exam. Units are given in standard SI form. Use `\vert` notation for absolute values (e.g., $\Delta h \vert_{\text{manometer}}$) to avoid breaking markdown. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol & Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Isothermal work (per kg) | $w_{\text{iso}} = P_1 \cdot v_1 \cdot \ln(r_p)$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | | 3 | Isothermal work (total) | $W_{\text{iso}} = m_a \cdot w_{\text{iso}}$ | $\mathrm{kW}$ (divide by time) | | 4 | Adiabatic work (per kg) | $w_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 v_1 \left[ (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | | 5 | Adiabatic work (total) | $W_{\text{ad}} = m_a \cdot w_{\text{ad}}$ | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 6 | Actual work / input (shaft) | $W_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ (or from wattmeter) | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 7 | Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{act}}}$ | $\vert \eta \vert$ | | 8 | Adiabatic efficiency | $\eta_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ad}}}{W_{\text{act}}}$ | $\vert \eta \vert$ | | 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ad}}}{W_{\text{ad}} + W_f}$ | $\vert \eta \vert$ | | 10 | Friction power (motoring test) | $W_f = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_f}{60}$ (no-load run) | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 11 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{swept}}}$ | $\vert \eta \vert$ | | 12 | Mass flow rate (orifice) | $m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_w \cdot \Delta h \cdot g}$ | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ | | 13 | Orifice head difference | $\Delta h = h_1 + \dfrac{h_2 (\rho_m - \rho_a)}{\rho_w}$ | $\mathrm{m \ of \ water}$ | | 14 | Swept volume per minute | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) \cdot L \cdot N \cdot K$ | $\mathrm{m^3/min}$ | | 15 | Free air delivery (FAD) | $V_{\text{FAD}} = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a}$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ | Where: - $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge of orifice $\approx 0.62 - 0.65$ - $A_o$ = area of orifice = $\dfrac{\pi}{4} d_o^2$ - $\rho_w = 1000 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ (water), $\rho_a \approx 1.2 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ (air) - $K$ = number of lobes (typically 2) - $D, d$ = lobe and shaft diameters; $L$ = effective length of lobe - $P_1 v_1 = R T_1$ for ideal gas ### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter — Engineering Utility > **Real-World Application Context:** > - **Industrial air systems:** Determining $\eta_{\text{ad}}$ is critical for sizing plant compressors — every 1% drop in adiabatic efficiency represents thousands of rupees in annual energy cost. > - **Aerospace & HVAC:** Volumetric efficiency dictates real free-air-delivery, directly impacting duct design and storage tank sizing. > - **Energy audits (BEE India):** Mechanical efficiency is a primary KPI in PSGR (Pressure-Specific Gravity-Ratio) standard calculations for industrial compressor certification. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Isothermal Work of Compression Starting from the first law of thermodynamics for a **quasi-static, isothermal, closed-system** compression process (control volume on the air column): $$ W_{\text{iso}} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV $$ For an isothermal process, the ideal gas law gives $PV = P_1 V_1 = \text{const}$. Substituting $P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V}$: $$ W_{\text{iso}} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{V} \, dV $$ Evaluating the integral: $$ W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{V} $$ $$ W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} $$ $$ W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \left( \ln V_2 - \ln V_1 \right) $$ Using the identity $\ln \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = - \ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ (since $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$): $$ \boxed{ W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = P_1 V_1 \ln r_p } $$ Per unit mass, dividing by $m$: $$ w_{\text{iso}} = P_1 v_1 \ln r_p = R T_1 \ln r_p $$ ### 3.2 Derivation — Polytropic (Adiabatic when n=γ) Work of Compression For a **polytropic process** $PV^n = P_1 V_1^n = \text{const}$, the compression work is: $$ W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^n V^{-n} \, dV $$ $$ W = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV $$ $$ W = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \dfrac{V^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{-n+1} \left( V_2^{-n+1} - V_1^{-n+1} \right) $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $V_1^{n-1}$ to rewrite in terms of pressure ratio: $$ W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \left[ \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{n-1} - 1 \right] $$ Since $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = r_p$ for an isothermal final state, and using the identity for polytropic work: $$ \boxed{ W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] } $$ Per unit mass: $$ w_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] $$ When $n = \gamma = 1.4$, this gives **adiabatic work**: $$ w_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} R T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{0.2857} - 1 \right] = 3.5 R T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{0.2857} - 1 \right] $$ ### 3.3 Derivation — Orifice Mass Flow Rate (Air Measurement) The discharge coefficient relation for an orifice plate is based on **Bernoulli's equation** between upstream and downstream of the orifice: $$ P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a v_1^2 = P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_a v_2^2 $$ Rearranging for the theoretical velocity $v_2$ at the orifice throat: $$ v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_a}} $$ The actual velocity accounts for the discharge coefficient $C_d$: $$ v_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g \Delta h_{\text{air}}} $$ The mass flow rate is then: $$ m_a = \rho_a \cdot A_o \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 g \Delta h_{\text{air}}} $$ But the differential manometer (U-tube) measures $\Delta h$ in terms of **manometric fluid column**, not air. The relationship between manometric reading and equivalent air-head is: $$ \Delta h_{\text{air}} = \Delta h_{\text{manometer}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_a}{\rho_a} \right) $$ For a mercury manometer reading $h_1$ (mercury) and water in the connecting tubes with $h_2$ (water above mercury): $$ \Delta h_{\text{manometer}} = h_1 \left( \dfrac{\rho_{\text{Hg}} - \rho_a}{\rho_w - \rho_a} \right) + h_2 \left( \dfrac{\rho_w - \rho_a}{\rho_w - \rho_a} \right) $$ Simplified form commonly used in the lab: $$ \Delta h_{\text{air eq.}} = 0.0735 \, h_1 + h_2 \quad \text{(m of air)} $$ (where $h_1$ = mercury manometer reading in cm, $h_2$ = water in connecting tube in cm) Therefore: $$ \boxed{ m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 g \, \rho_a \, \Delta h_{\text{air eq.}}} } $$ ### 3.4 Exhaustive Sample Calculation (Solved Worked Example) > **Sample Problem:** A two-lobe Roots compressor is tested under the following conditions. Determine (a) Isothermal work input, (b) Adiabatic work input, (c) Isothermal efficiency, (d) Mechanical efficiency. **Given Data:** - Atmospheric pressure: $P_1 = 1.013 \ \text{bar} = 101.3 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - Receiver (discharge) pressure: $P_2 = 1.6 \ \text{bar} = 160 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - Inlet air temperature: $T_1 = 303 \ \mathrm{K}$ - Mass flow rate: $m_a = 0.06 \ \mathrm{kg/s}$ - Speed of compressor: $N = 1450 \ \mathrm{rpm}$ - Power input (from wattmeter): $W_{\text{act}} = 6.0 \ \mathrm{kW}$ - Friction power (from no-load run): $W_f = 0.4 \ \mathrm{kW}$ - Polytropic index: $n = 1.35$ **Step 1 — Pressure Ratio:** $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{160}{101.3} = 1.5795 $$ **Step 2 — Isothermal Work (per kg):** $$ w_{\text{iso}} = R T_1 \ln r_p = 0.287 \times 303 \times \ln(1.5795) $$ $$ w_{\text{iso}} = 86.961 \times 0.4568 = 39.72 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Isothermal Work (Total):** $$ W_{\text{iso}} = m_a \cdot w_{\text{iso}} = 0.06 \times 39.72 = 2.383 \ \mathrm{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Polytropic Work (per kg):** $$ w_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] $$ $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.35}{0.35} = 3.857 $$ $$ (n-1)/n = 0.35/1.35 = 0.2593 $$ $$ (r_p)^{0.2593} = (1.5795)^{0.2593} = e^{0.2593 \times \ln(1.5795)} = e^{0.2593 \times 0.4568} = e^{0.11845} = 1.1258 $$ $$ w_{\text{poly}} = 3.857 \times 0.287 \times 303 \times (1.1258 - 1) $$ $$ w_{\text{poly}} = 3.857 \times 86.961 \times 0.1258 = 42.18 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Polytropic Work (Total):** $$ W_{\text{poly}} = 0.06 \times 42.18 = 2.531 \ \mathrm{kW} $$ **Step 6 — Isothermal Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{2.383}{6.0} = 0.3972 = 39.72 \% $$ **Step 7 — Mechanical Efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{poly}}}{W_{\text{poly}} + W_f} = \dfrac{2.531}{2.531 + 0.4} = \dfrac{2.531}{2.931} = 0.8635 = 86.35 \% $$ **Result Summary:** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Pressure ratio | $r_p$ | 1.58 | | Isothermal work (total) | $W_{\text{iso}}$ | 2.383 kW | | Polytropic work (total) | $W_{\text{poly}}$ | 2.531 kW | | Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{iso}}$ | 39.72 % | | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ | 86.35 % | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Rotary Compressor Test Rig The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the **functional flow of the test rig**, indicating measurement points and the direction of energy and fluid flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[MOTOR
Electric Prime Mover] -- belt coupling --> B(ROTARY COMPRESSOR
Roots Twin Lobe) B -- suction P1, T1 --> C[AIR INLET
Atmospheric] B -- discharge P2 --> D[RECEIVER TANK
Pressure Storage] D -- regulated flow --> E[ORIFICE PLATE
Discharge Meter] E --> F[DIFFERENTIAL MANOMETER
U-tube Reading] D -- pressure relief --> G[SAFETY VALVE
and Drain] B -- mechanical loss --> H[FRICTION
Wattmeter / No-load Run] I[BAROMETER
Atmospheric P] -.measurement.-> B J[TACHOMETER
N rpm] -.measurement.-> B K[THERMOMETERS
T1, T2] -.measurement.-> B L[WATTMETER
Electrical Input] -.measurement.-> A ``` ### 4.2 Operational Logic Flow (Mermaid State Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([START Test]) --> S1[Switch on Motor, No Load Run
Measure Friction Power Wf] S1 --> S2[Open Delivery Valve Gradually
Set Initial Pressure P2] S2 --> S3[Stabilize at Pressure P2
Record All Instruments] S3 --> S4[Measure Manometer h1, h2
Measure T1, T2, N] S4 --> S5[Tabulate Readings for Trial 1] S5 --> S6{More Trials at
Different P2?} S6 -- yes --> S7[Increase P2 in Steps
Repeat Steps S3 to S5] S6 -- no --> S8[Plot Graphs: P2 vs ma
P2 vs Efficiency] S7 --> S6 S8 --> S9([END Test and Calculate]) ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of the Lobe Engagement (Two-Lobe Roots Compressor) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ROTOR_HOUSING R1(LOBE A
Rotating CW) R2(LOBE B
Rotating CCW) end IN([SUCTION
P1]) -- trapped pocket --> R1 R1 -- carries air --> OUT([DELIVERY
P2]) OUT -. backflow leakage .-> IN R2 -- meshing seal --> R1 ``` **Working Description of the Above:** - Lobe A (rotating clockwise) draws in air from the suction port. - The air is *carried* between lobe A and the casing to the delivery port. - Lobe B meshes with A to prevent backflow during transit (acting as a moving seal). - Discharge occurs against receiver pressure $P_2$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define a rotary compressor and list any two types of positive-displacement rotary compressors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A rotary compressor is a machine that compresses a fluid (air) by means of a *rotating mechanical member* that continuously traps, compresses, and discharges a fixed volume of fluid per revolution.
