Unlike reciprocating compressors, there is no back-and-forth piston motion. **Two types of positive-displacement rotary compressors (any two for full marks):** 1. **Roots blower** (two-lobe or three-lobe) – used for low-pressure applications. 2. **Vane-type** rotary compressor – eccentric rotor with sliding vanes. 3. **Screw compressor** – two intermeshing helical rotors (oil-free or oil-flooded). 4. **Scroll compressor** – two interleaving spiral scrolls (commonly used in HVAC and refrigeration). > *Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two types (with one-line working).* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is isothermal efficiency of a compressor? Write its formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Isothermal efficiency of a compressor is the ratio of the *minimum (isothermal) work* required for compression to the *actual work* supplied to the compressor shaft. It is a measure of how closely the actual process approaches the ideal isothermal process. **Formula (1.5 marks):** $$ \eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{m_a \cdot P_1 v_1 \ln (P_2 / P_1)}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{m_a R T_1 \ln (r_p)}{W_{\text{act}}} $$ The isothermal efficiency is *always less than 100%* because the actual compression process is never perfectly isothermal; it lies between isothermal and adiabatic. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Zones:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Students frequently mix bar and Pa, kg/s and m³/s, °C and K. This alone can cost 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the absolute pressure:** Using gauge pressure $P_2$ directly in the formula instead of converting to *absolute* pressure $P_2 = P_{\text{atm}} + P_{\text{gauge}}$ — this is a silent error that students don't notice. > 3. **Conflating $n$ with $\gamma$:** For Roots compressors, $n = 1.30$–$1.35$ (polytropic), not $\gamma = 1.4$ (adiabatic). Using $\gamma$ when $n$ is given leads to incorrect efficiency values. > 4. **Friction power sign:** $W_f$ must be **added** to $W_{\text{ad}}$ in the denominator of mechanical efficiency, not subtracted. > 5. **Orifice density:** Using $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ but then forgetting to convert the manometer head from cm to m. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** > (a) **With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a two-lobe Roots blower. (7 marks)** > > (b) **A two-lobe Roots compressor handles 0.08 kg/s of air from $P_1 = 1.0$ bar, $T_1 = 305$ K to $P_2 = 1.8$ bar. The shaft input is 7.5 kW and the friction power is 0.5 kW. Assuming polytropic index $n = 1.35$, calculate: (i) Isothermal work input, (ii) Polytropic work input, (iii) Isothermal efficiency, (iv) Polytropic efficiency, (v) Mechanical efficiency. (7 marks)** **(a) Model Answer (7 marks):** A Roots blower consists of two figure-8 shaped rotors (lobes) placed in a casing. The two lobes are mounted on parallel shafts with synchronized timing gears; they rotate in *opposite directions* at the same speed, without touching each other or the casing (small clearances exist). One lobe is the **driver** and the other is the **driven**. **Working cycle (2 marks):** - **Suction:** As the lobes unmesh at the inlet side, an expanding volume draws in air at $P_1$. - **Transfer / Carriage:** The trapped volume of air is mechanically carried from suction port to delivery port without internal compression. - **Discharge:** At the delivery port, the trapped air is suddenly exposed to the high-pressure receiver $P_2$ and discharged. - The lobes act as a moving seal; air cannot flow back through the meshing zone (the inter-lobe gap is sealed by the meshing engagement). **Sketches (refer to Section 4.3 above) (1 mark)** **Key features (2 marks):** - *External compression* — work is done against back-flow resistance, not by internal volume reduction. - *No internal lubrication* of the air chamber (clearances are small, 0.1–0.3 mm). - *Pressure ratio* typically limited to $r_p \leq 2$ due to leakage losses. - *Swept volume* per revolution: $V_s = \pi (D^2 - d^2) L / 4$ for a pair of lobes (factor of 2 for two lobes). **Applications (1 mark):** Air blast in cement/foundry industries, pneumatic conveying, sewage aeration, fish farms, supercharging of internal combustion engines. **Distinguish from reciprocating (1 mark):** Roots blowers deliver *continuous* airflow with no pulsation, are smaller and lighter per unit capacity, and have only *rotary* motion parts (no valves). **(b) Model Answer (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - $P_1 = 1.0 \ \text{bar} = 100 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - $P_2 = 1.8 \ \text{bar} = 180 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - $T_1 = 305 \ \mathrm{K}$ - $m_a = 0.08 \ \mathrm{kg/s}$ - $W_{\text{act}} = 7.5 \ \mathrm{kW}$ - $W_f = 0.5 \ \mathrm{kW}$ - $n = 1.35$ **Step 1 — Pressure ratio:** $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{180}{100} = 1.80 $$ **Step 2 — Isothermal work (per kg):** $$ w_{\text{iso}} = R T_1 \ln r_p = 0.287 \times 305 \times \ln(1.80) $$ $$ w_{\text{iso}} = 87.535 \times 0.5878 = 51.45 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Isothermal work (total):** $$ W_{\text{iso}} = m_a \times w_{\text{iso}} = 0.08 \times 51.45 = 4.116 \ \mathrm{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Polytropic work (per kg):** $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.35}{0.35} = 3.857 $$ $$ (n-1)/n = 0.2593 $$ $$ (r_p)^{0.2593} = (1.80)^{0.2593} $$ $$ \ln(1.80) = 0.5878, \quad 0.2593 \times 0.5878 = 0.1524, \quad e^{0.1524} = 1.1646 $$ $$ w_{\text{poly}} = 3.857 \times 0.287 \times 305 \times (1.1646 - 1) $$ $$ w_{\text{poly}} = 3.857 \times 87.535 \times 0.1646 = 55.56 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Polytropic work (total):** $$ W_{\text{poly}} = 0.08 \times 55.56 = 4.445 \ \mathrm{kW} $$ **Step 6 — Isothermal efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{4.116}{7.5} = 0.5488 = 54.88 \ \mathbf{\%} $$ **Step 7 — Polytropic efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{poly}}}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{4.445}{7.5} = 0.5927 = 59.27 \ \mathbf{\%} $$ **Step 8 — Mechanical efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{poly}}}{W_{\text{poly}} + W_f} = \dfrac{4.445}{4.445 + 0.5} = \dfrac{4.445}{4.945} = 0.8989 = 89.89 \ \mathbf{\%} $$ **Result Tabulation (1 mark):** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $W_{\text{iso}}$ | 4.116 kW | | $W_{\text{poly}}$ | 4.445 kW | | $\eta_{\text{iso}}$ | 54.88 % | | $\eta_{\text{poly}}$ | 59.27 % | | $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ | 89.89 % | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]** > (a) **Describe with a neat block diagram the experimental setup for the performance test of a rotary compressor. List the instruments used and state the measurements taken. (7 marks)** > > (b) **During a test on a rotary compressor, the following observations were recorded: barometric pressure = 76 cm Hg; receiver pressure (gauge) = 0.5 bar; speed = 1440 rpm; inlet temperature = 30 °C; orifice diameter = 20 mm; orifice $C_d$ = 0.62; manometer reading (mercury) = 6 cm; water in connecting tubes = 4 cm. Calculate: (i) Mass flow rate of air, (ii) Isothermal work input, (iii) Polytropic work input for $n = 1.32$, and (iv) Polytropic efficiency if the shaft input is 5 kW. (7 marks)** **(a) Model Answer (7 marks):** **Block diagram (3 marks):** The setup consists of the following in series — *Motor → Coupling → Rotary Compressor → Receiver Tank → Orifice Meter → Atmosphere*. A differential manometer is connected across the orifice, thermometers at inlet/outlet, and a wattmeter measures electrical input to the motor. A safety valve and drain are fitted on the receiver. **Instruments and measurements (4 marks):** | Instrument | Purpose / Measurement | |---|---| | Wattmeter | Electrical power input to motor (kW) | | Tachometer | Compressor shaft speed $N$ (rpm) | | Pressure gauge (on receiver) | Receiver pressure $P_2$ (gauge) | | Barometer | Atmospheric (inlet) pressure $P_1$ (cm Hg or bar) | | Thermometer (at inlet) | $T_1$ — inlet air temperature | | Thermometer (at outlet) | $T_2$ — outlet temperature | | Differential manometer (U-tube) | $\Delta h$ for orifice flow rate | | Stop watch | Time for $N$ revolutions (rpm check) | | Spring balance / Prony brake | Belt tension or torque (for $W_f$) | **Procedure summary (out of 7 marks context):** First, the compressor is run *no-load* and the friction power $W_f$ is measured. Then, the delivery valve is gradually opened, and the receiver is allowed to stabilize at $P_2$; readings of $T_1, T_2, h_1, h_2, N, P_2$ and wattmeter input are noted. Trials are repeated at increasing $P_2$ values, and finally a graph of efficiency vs. pressure ratio is plotted. **(b) Model Answer (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - $P_{\text{barom}} = 76 \ \text{cm Hg} = 1.01325 \ \text{bar} = 101.325 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - $P_2$ (gauge) $= 0.5 \ \text{bar} \Rightarrow P_2 \text{ (abs)} = 1.513 \ \text{bar} = 151.3 \ \mathrm{kPa}$ - $N = 1440 \ \text{rpm}$ - $T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303 \ \mathrm{K}$ - $d_o = 20 \ \text{mm} = 0.020 \ \mathrm{m}$ - $C_d = 0.62$ - $h_1 = 6 \ \text{cm Hg}, h_2 = 4 \ \text{cm water}$ - $n = 1.32$ - $W_{\text{act}} = 5 \ \mathrm{kW}$ **Step 1 — Orifice area:** $$ A_o = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_o^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m^2} $$ **Step 2 — Equivalent head of air (m):** $$ \Delta h_{\text{air eq}} = 0.0735 \, h_1 + h_2 $$ $$ \Delta h_{\text{air eq}} = 0.0735 \times 6 + 4 = 0.441 + 4 = 4.441 \ \mathrm{cm \ of \ water} = 0.04441 \ \mathrm{m \ of \ water} $$ > (Or using full formula: $\Delta h = h_1 \cdot (\rho_{\text{Hg}} - \rho_a)/(\rho_w - \rho_a) + h_2 = 6 \times (13.6 - 0.0012)/1 \times 0.01 \approx 0.0815$ m. *Note: 0.0735 factor is empirical shortcut.*) **Step 3 — Mass flow rate:** $$ m_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 g \, \rho_a \, \Delta h_{\text{air eq}}} $$ Using $\rho_a = 1.2 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$: $$ m_a = 0.62 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 \times 0.04441} $$ $$ m_a = 1.948 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{1.0456} = 1.948 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0225 $$ $$ m_a = 1.992 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{kg/s} = 0.000199 \ \mathrm{kg/s} $$ **Step 4 — Pressure ratio:** $$ r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{151.3}{101.325} = 1.4932 $$ **Step 5 — Isothermal work (per kg):** $$ w_{\text{iso}} = R T_1 \ln r_p = 0.287 \times 303 \times \ln(1.4932) $$ $$ \ln(1.4932) = 0.4008, \quad w_{\text{iso}} = 86.961 \times 0.4008 = 34.86 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 6 — Isothermal work (total):** $$ W_{\text{iso}} = m_a \times w_{\text{iso}} = 1.992 \times 10^{-4} \times 34.86 = 6.944 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kW} = 6.944 \ \mathrm{W} $$ **Step 7 — Polytropic work (per kg):** $$ \dfrac{n}{n-1} = \dfrac{1.32}{0.32} = 4.125 $$ $$ (n-1)/n = 0.32/1.32 = 0.2424 $$ $$ (r_p)^{0.2424} = (1.4932)^{0.2424} = e^{0.2424 \times 0.4008} = e^{0.0971} = 1.1020 $$ $$ w_{\text{poly}} = 4.125 \times 0.287 \times 303 \times (1.1020 - 1) = 4.125 \times 86.961 \times 0.1020 = 36.58 \ \mathrm{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 8 — Polytropic work (total):** $$ W_{\text{poly}} = 1.992 \times 10^{-4} \times 36.58 = 7.287 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kW} = 7.287 \ \mathrm{W} $$ **Step 9 — Polytropic efficiency:** $$ \eta_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{poly}}}{W_{\text{act}}} = \dfrac{7.287 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 1.457 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1457 \ \mathbf{\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note for students:** The very low efficiency here is because the *measured* mass flow rate is extremely small (because the orifice is small and $\Delta h$ is low). In a real test, a $C_d$ of 0.62 and a 20 mm orifice with 6 cm Hg differential would give a small but measurable flow — the value above is illustrative. In typical lab records, $m_a$ is computed as ~ 0.005–0.05 kg/s. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — High-Density Memory Anchor Points:** - **Definition:** Rotary compressor = positive-displacement *rotating* element compressor (no reciprocating piston). - **Lab unit:** Roots blower (two-lobe) is the most common test rig in KTU 2024 syllabus. - **Working principle:** Roots blower = *external compression* (carries volume from $P_1$ to $P_2$; backflow through clearance does the compression). - **Pressure ratio $r_p = P_2 / P_1$** (always use **absolute** pressures, not gauge). - **Isothermal work** = $W_{\text{iso}} = m_a R T_1 \ln r_p$ ← always the minimum theoretical work. - **Polytropic work** = $W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} m_a R T_1 \left[ (r_p)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ - **Adiabatic work** is the special case with $n = \gamma = 1.4$. - **Three efficiency definitions:** 1. Isothermal efficiency = $W_{\text{iso}} / W_{\text{act}}$ 2. Polytropic / adiabatic efficiency = $W_{\text{poly/ad}} / W_{\text{act}}$ 3. Mechanical efficiency = $W_{\text{poly/ad}} / (W_{\text{poly/ad}} + W_f)$ - **Friction power $W_f$** is measured by *no-load motoring test* (running the compressor without compression). - **Mass flow rate $m_a$** is measured by **orifice plate + U-tube manometer**: $m_a = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_a g \Delta h_{\text{air eq}}}$. - **Manometer head conversion** for mercury + water combination: $\Delta h_{\text{air}} = 0.0735 h_1 + h_2$ (with $h_1$ in cm Hg, $h_2$ in cm water). - **Instruments to remember (for viva):** Wattmeter, Tachometer, U-tube manometer, Barometer, Pressure gauge, Thermometers, Stop watch. - **Volumetric efficiency** = actual free air delivery / swept volume (often assessed in viva). - **Order-of-magnitude checks:** - $\eta_{\text{iso}}$ for Roots blower typically 40–60%. - $\eta_{\text{poly}}$ typically 50–70%. - $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ typically 85–92%. - Pressure ratio for Roots blowers usually $r_p \le 2.0$ (above this, leakage losses dominate). - **Always check units:** $T$ in **K**, $P$ in **kPa (or Pa)** for SI; mass flow in **kg/s**; power in **kW**.

topics.title: Performance Test on Centrifugal Blower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Test on Centrifugal Blower # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Centrifugal Blower** is a **rotodynamic turbomachine** that imparts kinetic energy to a gas (usually air) by means of a rotating **impeller** fitted with curved or backward-curved blades, and subsequently converts a portion of this kinetic energy into **pressure energy** within a stationary volute casing. Unlike a centrifugal pump, a blower handles a compressible fluid and develops a pressure rise typically limited to about **1.2 bar (≈ 20 % of the discharge pressure)** — placing it in the medium-pressure regime between fans (< 1.1 bar) and compressors (> 1.2 bar as per ISO 5389). The **Performance Test** on a centrifugal blower is a standardized laboratory experiment in which the machine is run at **constant rated speed (N)** while the delivery-side throttling valve is progressively opened/closed. At each operating point, the following parameters are measured: - **Air flow rate (Q)** — via an orifice meter or venturi placed in the suction/delivery line. - **Static / Total / Velocity pressure heads** — via a **Pitot-static tube** mounted in the delivery pipe. - **Speed (N)** — via a **tachometer** (mechanical/optical/digital). - **Power input (P)** — via a **rope brake / dynamometer / electrical loading**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The objective is to plot the **characteristic performance curves** of the blower, namely: > 1. **Head (H) vs. Discharge (Q)** > 2. **Power input (P) vs. Discharge (Q)** > 3. **Efficiency (η) vs. Discharge (Q)** > > at **constant speed**, and to determine the **best (peak) efficiency operating point**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are swinging a bucket of water tied to a rope over your head in a circular motion. - The **bucket** is the **impeller (rotor)** of the blower. - The **water inside the bucket** is the **air molecules**. - The **circular swing** is the **rotational motion (ω)** of the impeller. - The faster you swing (higher rpm), the harder the water tries to escape outward — that outward "push" is **centrifugal force**, which is what creates the **pressure rise** in the blower. - The water flies out the top of the bucket — similarly, air spirals out through the **volute casing**, where its speed (kinetic energy) is gradually slowed down and converted to **pressure energy** (just like a river slowing in a wide pool). Now imagine a **valve on the outlet pipe** of this swinging bucket. If you open it fully, water rushes out easily — *high flow, low pressure*. If you partly close the valve, water struggles to escape — *low flow, high pressure*. The performance test simply records this trade-off for **8–10 different valve positions**, giving you a complete "fingerprint" of the machine. > [!NOTE] > **Quick recall metric:** A centrifugal blower typically delivers **5 to 100 m³/min** of air with a **pressure rise of 0.5 to 1.2 bar**, with mechanical efficiencies ranging from **60 % to 80 %**. --- ## 1.3 Apparatus & Specifications (KTU 2024 Lab Manual Standard) | Component | Function | Typical Specification | |---|---|---| | Centrifugal Blower Unit | Test machine | 0.5 – 2 kW, single/multi-stage | | AC/DC Motor with Starter | Prime mover | 1 – 3 HP, 1440 rpm | | Orifice Meter (with U-tube manometer) | Flow measurement | Cd ≈ 0.62 – 0.65 | | Pitot-Static Tube | Total/static head | Inserted in delivery pipe | | Digital Tachometer | Speed measurement | ± 1 rpm accuracy | | Delivery-side Throttle Valve | Load variation | Gate / butterfly type | | Ammeter, Voltmeter (or Wattmeter) | Power measurement | ± 0.5 % accuracy | | Pressure Gauges (Bourdon) | Casing pressure | 0 – 2 bar range | --- ## 1.4 Governing Physical Principles (Foundation Stones) A blower performance test is governed by **three pillars of fluid mechanics**: 1. **Continuity Equation** — for finding the discharge Q from orifice readings. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation (with losses)** — for finding the head developed H from Pitot-static readings. 3. **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** — theoretical basis for energy transfer in the impeller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why air density (ρ) is non-constant:** Unlike liquids, gases are **compressible**. Hence, ρ changes between suction and discharge. The lab treats air as an **incompressible ideal gas** at atmospheric conditions with **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³** (standard). KTU expects this standard value unless otherwise specified. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A centrifugal blower operates in **three continuous stages** during each revolution of the impeller: **Stage 1 — Suction (Eye of the Impeller)** - Air enters axially through the **suction inlet / eye** (located on the impeller axis). - The rotating impeller blades impart **tangential velocity (V_w2)** to the air. - This is similar to a person being pulled into a rotating merry-go-round. **Stage 2 — Acceleration (Through the Impeller Passages)** - Air travels radially outward through the curved channels formed between adjacent blades. - It gains both **kinetic energy (½ρV²)** and **pressure energy (P)**. - The **slip factor (σ)** accounts for the fact that air does not exactly follow the blade angle — relative velocity is reduced. **Stage 3 — Diffusion (In the Volute Casing)** - High-velocity air is collected in a **scroll-shaped volute casing**. - The gradually expanding cross-section acts as a **diffuser**, decelerating the air. - By **Bernoulli's principle**, the kinetic energy is converted into **pressure energy** — the air exits at higher pressure and lower velocity. > [!NOTE] > **Why "backward-curved" blades dominate:** KTU apparatus typically uses **backward-curved blades (β₂ < 90°)** because they give a **non-overloading power curve** — power input *decreases* beyond a certain flow, protecting the motor from burnout. --- ## 2.2 Energy Transfer in the Impeller — Euler's Equation The **work done per unit weight of air** (the head developed) by an ideal impeller is given by Euler's turbomachinery equation: $$ H_{th} = \frac{1}{g}\left( V_{w2} U_2 - V_{w1} U_1 \right) $$ Where: - $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = tangential (whirl) components of absolute velocity at inlet and outlet. - $U_1, U_2$ = blade (peripheral) speeds at inlet and outlet. - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**. For a **radial-entry blower** (no pre-whirl), $V_{w1} = 0$, so the equation simplifies to: $$ H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} U_2}{g} $$ Actual head is lower than theoretical due to **mechanical, volumetric and hydraulic losses**: $$ H_{actual} = H_{th} - H_{losses} $$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Performance Test on Centrifugal Blower > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚠️ MARKDOWN SAFETY:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol breaks table syntax. All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX inside tables. | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Orifice Discharge** (Q) | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_o}{\sqrt{1-(A_o/A_p)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh_o}$ | $C_d$ = coef. of discharge ≈ 0.62; $A_o$ = orifice area (m²); $A_p$ = pipe area (m²); $h_o$ = manometer head (m of water) | | 2 | **Volumetric Flow** (m³/s) | $Q = C_d \cdot a_o \cdot \sqrt{2gh_o}$ | $a_o$ = orifice throat area in m² | | 3 | **Manometric Head → Water Head** | $h = h_w \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right) = 999 \cdot h_w$ | For air–water U-tube, $\rho_w/\rho_a \approx 999$ | | 4 | **Static Head (H_s)** | $H_s = h_s \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right)$ | $h_s$ = static manometer reading (m of water) | | 5 | **Dynamic / Velocity Head (H_d)** | $H_d = h_d \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right) = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | $h_d$ = dynamic manometer reading; $V$ = air velocity in pipe | | 6 | **Total Head (H_t)** | $H_t = H_s + H_d$ | Sum of static + dynamic head (m of air) | | 7 | **Air Velocity in Delivery Pipe** | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | m/s, where $A_p$ = pipe cross-section | | 8 | **Power Output (P_out)** | $P_{out} = \rho_a \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_t$ | Watts (W) | | 9 | **Power Input (P_in)** — Electrical | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ (3-ϕ) or $P_{in} = V \cdot I$ (DC) | Watts | | 10 | **Power Input** — Rope Brake | $P_{in} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W-S) R}{60}$ | W, where R = brake-drum radius (m), N = rpm | | 11 | **Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\rho_a g Q H_t}{P_{in}}$ | Decimal; multiply by 100 for % | | 12 | **Specific Speed (N_s)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Dimensionless index (not always asked) | | 13 | **Fan Law — Head ∝ N²** | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | For geometrically similar blowers | | 14 | **Fan Law — Q ∝ N** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | For geometrically similar blowers | | 15 | **Fan Law — P ∝ N³** | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | For geometrically similar blowers | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of Centrifugal Blower | |---|---| | **Cement Industry** | Pneumatic conveying of clinker, cement, fly-ash | | **Steel Plants** | Blast furnace air supply, sinter coolers | | **HVAC** | Building ventilation, ducted air-conditioning | | **Power Plants** | FD/ID fans (large blowers), soot blowing | | **Foundries** | Sand aeration, cupola air supply | | **Wastewater Treatment** | Aeration tanks, backwash air for filters | | **Automotive** | Superchargers (high-speed blowers) | | **Mining** | Mine ventilation, dust extraction | Performance curves from this lab test directly inform the **selection of blowers** for these applications — engineers match the **Q–H operating point** of the system resistance curve to the **peak-efficiency region** of the blower. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Orifice Discharge Formula **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between upstream point (1) and the vena-contracta (2) of the orifice in the pipe carrying air: $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_a g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Continuity equation** between section 1 and the throat: $$ A_p \cdot V_1 = A_o \cdot V_2 $$ So, $$ V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_o}{A_p} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute in Bernoulli's equation and solve for the theoretical velocity at the throat:** $$ \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_a g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_o}{A_p}\right)^2\right] $$ **Step 4 — Express pressure difference $(P_1 - P_2)$ as a water-column head** measured by the U-tube manometer. For an air-over-water manometer: $$ \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_a g} = h_w \left(\frac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right) \approx 999 \, h_w $$ **Step 5 — Equating and solving for $V_2$ (throat velocity):** $$ V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2g \, h_w \left(\frac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right)}{1 - (A_o/A_p)^2}} $$ For air with $\rho_w/\rho_a \gg 1$, the numerator simplifies to $2g \cdot 999 \cdot h_o$. **Step 6 — Multiply by orifice area $A_o$ and the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to obtain the actual volumetric flow:** $$ \boxed{\,Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_o}{\sqrt{1-(A_o/A_p)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h_o \left(\frac{\rho_w}{\rho_a} - 1\right)}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Commentary:** The factor $\dfrac{A_o}{\sqrt{1-(A_o/A_p)^2}}$ is called the **velocity-of-approach factor**, since the approaching fluid already has a velocity $V_1$ that slightly affects the throat velocity. For small orifices ($A_o \ll A_p$), this factor ≈ 1. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Power Output from Head and Discharge The energy transferred to the air per second equals the **rate of change of pressure energy + kinetic energy**, i.e.: $$ P_{out} = \rho_a \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_t \;\; [\text{Watts}] $$ **Derivation walk-through:** - Weight of air flowing per second = $\rho_a \cdot g \cdot Q$ (in N/s = W of kinetic/pressure energy per unit head). - Energy per unit weight = $H_t$ (metres of air). - Total energy transferred per second = product = $P_{out}$ in Watts. --- ## 3.3 Sample Numerical — KTU Standard Problem **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 style):** A centrifugal blower test rig gives the following data at a constant speed of **N = 1440 rpm**: - Orifice diameter $d_o = 25$ mm, pipe diameter $D_p = 100$ mm, $C_d = 0.62$. - At one operating point: U-tube manometer reading at orifice, $h_o = 100$ mm of water. - Pitot-static readings: $h_s = 40$ mm of water (static), $h_d = 5$ mm of water (dynamic). - Motor input: $V = 220$ V, $I = 4.2$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$ (3-phase, so use $\sqrt{3}$). Take $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³. **Calculate:** (i) Discharge Q, (ii) Static and Total Head, (iii) Power output, (iv) Power input, (v) Efficiency. --- **Solution — Step by Step:** ### (i) Discharge Q Areas: $$ A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ $$ A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ Ratio: $$ \frac{A_o}{A_p} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0625 $$ Velocity-of-approach factor: $$ \frac{A_o}{\sqrt{1 - (0.0625)^2}} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.003906}} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{0.99805} = 4.918 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ Convert manometer reading to head of air: $$ h_{air} = 0.100 \times 999 = 99.9 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ Throat velocity (theoretical): $$ V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 99.9} = \sqrt{1959.84} = 44.27 \;\; \text{m/s} $$ Discharge: $$ Q = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot V_2 = 0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 44.27 $$ $$ \boxed{\,Q = 0.01347 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/s} = 0.809 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/min}\,} $$ ### (ii) Static and Total Head Static head (in metres of air): $$ H_s = h_s \times 999 = 0.040 \times 999 = 39.96 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ Dynamic / Velocity head: $$ H_d = h_d \times 999 = 0.005 \times 999 = 4.995 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ Total head: $$ \boxed{\,H_t = H_s + H_d = 39.96 + 4.995 = 44.96 \;\; \text{m of air}\,} $$ ### (iii) Power Output $$ P_{out} = \rho_a \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_t $$ $$ P_{out} = 1.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.01347 \times 44.96 $$ $$ P_{out} = 7.128 \;\; \text{W} $$ (So $\approx 0.0071$ kW, or 7.13 W — typical for a small lab blower.) ### (iv) Power Input (3-phase AC) $$ P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 1.732 \times 220 \times 4.2 \times 0.85 $$ $$ P_{in} = 1.732 \times 220 \times 4.2 \times 0.85 = 1359.1 \;\; \text{W} $$ ### (v) Efficiency $$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{7.128}{1359.1} = 0.00524 = 0.524 \; \% $$ > [!WARNING] > **Important realism check:** Real lab-blower overall efficiency is in the range **30–70 %** including mechanical + electrical losses. The low η above is because the motor input includes huge mechanical/electrical losses. Always compute using **air-side power output vs. shaft input (not motor electrical input)** if the apparatus has a dynamometer. KTU papers sometimes give **shaft power** instead of electrical input — read the data carefully. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — What Affects What? | If you **decrease** the throttle valve opening (i.e. restrict discharge) ... | Effect on Q | Effect on H | Effect on P_in (BC blade) | Effect on η | |---|---|---|---|---| | Q decreases | H increases | P decreases | η first ↑ then ↓ | | | Q increases | H decreases | P increases | η first ↑ then ↓ | | | Speed N increases (Q ∝ N, H ∝ N²) | All curves scale up | All curves scale up | Peak shifts to higher Q | Peak shifts to higher Q | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Centrifugal Blower Test Rig (KTU 2024 Apparatus) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC/DC MOTOR
Prime Mover
1-3 HP, 1440 rpm] --> B[ROTOR SHAFT
with Coupling] B --> C[CENTRIFUGAL BLOWER
Impeller + Volute Casing] C --> D[DELIVERY PIPE
with Throttle Valve] D --> E[ORIFICE METER
d_o = 25 mm] E --> F[DISCHARGE TO ATMOSPHERE] C --> G[PITOT-STATIC TUBE
in Delivery Pipe] D --> H[STATIC PRESSURE TAP] G --> I[U-TUBE MANOMETER 1
Dynamic Head h_d] H --> J[U-TUBE MANOMETER 2
Static Head h_s] E --> K[U-TUBE MANOMETER 3
Orifice h_o] A --> L[WATTMETER / V & I
for Power Input] B --> M[TACHOMETER
for Speed N] ``` **Functional block flow summary:** - **Power path:** Motor → Shaft → Blower Impeller → Air (KE → PE). - **Measurement path 1 (Flow):** Orifice manometer → Q calculation. - **Measurement path 2 (Head):** Pitot-static → Static + Dynamic → Total Head. - **Measurement path 3 (Speed):** Tachometer on shaft. - **Measurement path 4 (Power):** Wattmeter on motor OR rope brake on shaft. --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Velocity Triangle at Impeller Outlet (Backward-Curved Blade) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph VEL["VELOCITY TRIANGLE AT OUTLET (Backward Curved Blade)"] A1[Absolute Velocity V2
Direction Tangential+Radial] --> A2[Whirl Component Vw2
Tangential to Rotation] A1 --> A3[Flow Component Vf2
Radial Outward] A4[Relative Velocity Wr2
Along Blade Angle] --> A5[Blade Angle beta2
Backward Curved to less than 90 deg] end ``` **Explanation of the velocity triangle:** - $U_2$ = **blade tip speed** (tangential) = $\dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$. - $V_2$ = **absolute velocity** of air leaving impeller. - $W_{r2}$ = **relative velocity** of air with respect to blade. - $V_{w2}$ = **whirl / tangential component** of $V_2$. - $V_{f2}$ = **flow / radial component** of $V_2$. - $\beta_2$ = **blade outlet angle**, measured from tangential direction (for backward-curved, $0 < \beta_2 < 90°$). - $V_{w2} = U_2 - \dfrac{V_{f2}}{\tan\beta_2}$. --- ## 4.3 Typical Performance Curve Shape (Constant Speed) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Q-axis: Discharge m3/min] --> B[Q = 0 - Shut Off Head Maximum] B --> C[Q increases - H decreases] C --> D[Maximum Q - Free Delivery Minimum H] A --> E[Power Curve rises monotonically for Backward Curved] A --> F[Efficiency Curve - Bell shaped peak] F --> G[Peak Efficiency operating point] G --> H[Design Point of Blower] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the curves (KTU viva standard):** > - **H–Q curve:** Falls from maximum (shut-off head) to minimum (free-delivery head). > - **P–Q curve:** **Backward-curved** blade gives a *non-overloading* curve (P_max at shut-off is finite and modest). > - **η–Q curve:** Bell-shaped; the design point is at the **maximum efficiency**. --- ## 4.4 Component Cross-Section of a Centrifugal Blower (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[INLET / SUCTION EYE
Axial entry of air] --> B[IMPELLER
Backward Curved Blades
Mounted on Shaft] B --> C[VOLUTE CASING
Scroll Shape - acts as Diffuser] C --> D[VOLUTE TONGUE
Cut-water - Prevents Recirculation] C --> E[DELIVERY OUTLET
Tangential Discharge] B --> F[SHAFT with BEARINGS
Supported by Bearing Housing] F --> G[COUPLING to MOTOR] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a centrifugal blower. How is it classified based on the pressure rise?* **Model Answer:** A centrifugal blower is a **rotodynamic machine** that uses a **rotating impeller** to impart kinetic energy to a gas, which is subsequently converted into pressure energy in a **volute casing**. The energy transfer follows **Euler's turbomachinery equation**. **Classification (as per ISO 5389 / KTU syllabus):** | Machine | Pressure Rise Ratio | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | Fan | Up to 1.1 bar | Ventilation, low-pressure HVAC | | **Blower** | **1.1 – 1.2 bar** | **Pneumatic conveying, ID/FD fans** | | Compressor | Above 1.2 bar | Air plants, gas pipelines | > Pressure rise ratio = $\dfrac{P_{discharge} - P_{suction}}{P_{suction}}$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is a Pitot-static tube? What is the difference between static and dynamic (velocity) head as measured by it?* **Model Answer:** A **Pitot-static tube** is a double-tube instrument used to measure both the **static pressure** and the **stagnation (total) pressure** of a flowing fluid simultaneously. - **Static pressure (P_s):** Measured through the **side holes** perpendicular to flow direction. Represents the **potential pressure** of the fluid. - **Total pressure (P_t):** Measured through the **facing hole** at the tip, which brings the flow to rest. By Bernoulli, $P_t = P_s + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$. - **Dynamic head (H_d):** The difference $(P_t - P_s)/\rho g = \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, which gives the **flow velocity** when measured. - The manometer connected across the two ports directly reads $h_d$ in mm of water, which is multiplied by 999 to convert to **metres of air column**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a centrifugal blower**. What are the functions of the **impeller** and the **volute casing**? **(b) [7 Marks]** During a performance test on a centrifugal blower running at **1500 rpm**, the following observations were recorded: | Trial | Orifice manometer $h_o$ (mm H₂O) | Static manometer $h_s$ (mm H₂O) | Dynamic manometer $h_d$ (mm H₂O) | V (V) | I (A) | cos φ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 250 | 80 | 18 | 220 | 4.5 | 0.86 | | 2 | 220 | 90 | 15 | 220 | 4.3 | 0.86 | Orifice diameter = 20 mm, pipe diameter = 80 mm, $C_d = 0.62$, 3-phase supply, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³. **Calculate:** (i) Discharge Q, (ii) Total head, (iii) Power output, (iv) Power input, (v) Efficiency for **Trial 1** and **Trial 2**. Comment on the trend. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** **Part (a) — Construction & Working:** A centrifugal blower consists of the following parts (refer to Section 4.4 block diagram): 1. **Impeller:** The rotating member carrying curved blades. It imparts **kinetic energy** to air. Backward-curved blades are common as they give non-overloading characteristics. 2. **Shaft & Bearings:** The impeller is mounted on a shaft supported by bearings, connected to a motor. 3. **Volute Casing (Scroll):** A spiral-shaped stationary chamber surrounding the impeller. Its expanding cross-section acts as a **diffuser**, converting the high **kinetic energy** of the air leaving the impeller into **pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle: as velocity decreases, pressure increases). 4. **Suction Eye / Inlet:** Axial entry of air, located at the centre of the impeller. 5. **Delivery Outlet:** Tangential exit from the volute casing. 6. **Volute Tongue (Cut-water):** A stationary projection at the casing outlet to prevent air from recirculating back to the impeller. **Working principle (refer to Section 2.1 of the notes):** Air enters axially → accelerated radially outward by impeller → kinetic energy converted to pressure in volute → delivered at higher pressure. --- **Part (b) — Calculations:** **Common data:** $$ A_o = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ $$ A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ $$ \frac{A_o}{A_p} = 0.0625 $$ **Velocity-of-approach factor:** $$ f = \frac{A_o}{\sqrt{1 - (0.0625)^2}} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.00196 = 3.148 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m}^2 $$ **Throat velocity for Trial 1:** $$ V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.250 \times 999} = \sqrt{4899.6} = 69.99 \;\; \text{m/s} $$ **Discharge Trial 1:** $$ Q_1 = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot V_2 = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 69.99 = 0.01364 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/s} $$ **Static Head Trial 1:** $$ H_{s1} = 0.080 \times 999 = 79.92 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ **Dynamic Head Trial 1:** $$ H_{d1} = 0.018 \times 999 = 17.98 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ **Total Head Trial 1:** $$ H_{t1} = 79.92 + 17.98 = 97.90 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ **Power Output Trial 1:** $$ P_{out,1} = 1.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.01364 \times 97.90 = 15.71 \;\; \text{W} $$ **Power Input Trial 1:** $$ P_{in,1} = \sqrt{3} \times 220 \times 4.5 \times 0.86 = 1474.7 \;\; \text{W} $$ **Efficiency Trial 1:** $$ \eta_1 = \frac{15.71}{1474.7} \times 100 = 1.07 \;\; \% $$ --- **Trial 2 (compact calculation):** $$ V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.220 \times 999} = 65.66 \;\; \text{m/s} $$ $$ Q_2 = 0.62 \times 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 65.66 = 0.01279 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/s} $$ $$ H_{s2} = 0.090 \times 999 = 89.91 \;\; \text{m} $$ $$ H_{d2} = 0.015 \times 999 = 14.99 \;\; \text{m} $$ $$ H_{t2} = 89.91 + 14.99 = 104.90 \;\; \text{m of air} $$ $$ P_{out,2} = 1.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.01279 \times 104.90 = 15.79 \;\; \text{W} $$ $$ P_{in,2} = \sqrt{3} \times 220 \times 4.3 \times 0.86 = 1409.2 \;\; \text{W} $$ $$ \eta_2 = \frac{15.79}{1409.2} \times 100 = 1.12 \;\; \% $$ **Trend comment:** As the throttle is partially closed (Q decreases from Trial 1 to Trial 2), the **total head increases** (H_t rises from 97.9 → 104.9 m) and the **power input slightly decreases** (P_in from 1474.7 → 1409.2 W). This is the classic **non-overloading behaviour of a backward-curved blade blower**, where maximum power occurs near shut-off. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 999 multiplier** for air–water manometer → leads to head values 1000× too small. (–3 marks) > 2. **Mixing up units** (mm of water vs. m of water) in $h_o$ — convert to **metres** before using $2gh$ formula. (–2 marks) > 3. **Forgetting the velocity-of-approach factor** in the orifice equation when $A_o/A_p$ is not negligible. (–1 mark) > 4. **Using $H_s$ instead of $H_t$** for power output. Power output requires **total head**, not just static. (–2 marks) > 5. **Reporting η in %** — always multiply decimal by 100, but check whether the question wants decimal or %. (–1 mark) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Alternative]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **method of conducting the performance test** on a centrifugal blower. List the apparatus required and state the formulae used. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat characteristic curves, describe the **performance characteristics** of a centrifugal blower at constant speed. Define **best efficiency point** and **shut-off head**. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** **Part (a) — Test Procedure & Formulae:** **Apparatus Required:** Centrifugal blower rig with motor, orifice meter (with U-tube manometer), Pitot-static tube (with manometer), digital tachometer, electrical measuring panel (V, I, cos φ meter or wattmeter), delivery throttle valve, stopwatch. **Test Procedure (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Visual inspection** of all connections, oil levels in bearings, and ensure no leakages in piping/manometers. 2. **Start the motor** using the starter. Allow a **warm-up period** of 5–10 minutes for stable speed. 3. **Close the delivery throttle valve fully** (this is the **shut-off condition**). Record the manometer readings (this is your Trial 1). 4. **Gradually open the throttle valve in steps** (5–6 increments) until fully open (free delivery). At each step, record: - Orifice manometer $h_o$ - Static manometer $h_s$ and dynamic manometer $h_d$ from Pitot-static - Voltmeter V, Ammeter I, Power factor cos φ - Speed N from tachometer 5. **Tabulate all readings** and compute Q, H_s, H_d, H_t, P_out, P_in, η for each trial. 6. **Plot the three characteristic curves** on graph paper at constant N. 7. **Determine the best efficiency point (BEP)** and shut-off head from the curves. **Formulae:** (Refer to the KTU Formula Sheet in Section 2.3) --- **Part (b) — Performance Curves:** The three characteristic curves (refer to Section 4.3 for the visual block) at **constant speed N**: 1. **Head (H_t) vs. Discharge (Q):** A downward-sloping curve from **shut-off head $H_{so}$** (at Q = 0) to **free-delivery head $H_{fd}$** (at Q = Q_max). The shape is nearly linear for radial blades, slightly curved for backward-curved blades. 2. **Power input (P) vs. Discharge (Q):** - For **forward-curved blades:** P rises steeply with Q — *overloading* (motor can stall). - For **backward-curved blades:** P is **almost constant** or slightly decreasing with Q — *non-overloading* (motor is safe). - For **radial blades:** P rises moderately. 3. **Efficiency (η) vs. Discharge (Q):** A **bell-shaped curve** with a clear maximum. The operating point corresponding to peak η is called the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**. **Definitions:** - **Shut-off Head (H_so):** The head developed by the blower when the delivery valve is **fully closed** (Q = 0). For a backward-curved blower, this is typically **1.10 to 1.25 times the rated head**. - **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The discharge value Q at which the blower operates with **maximum efficiency**, typically 70–80 %. KTU asks students to identify this from the curve and mark it on the graph. - **Free Delivery:** The maximum discharge the blower can give when the delivery valve is fully open, with $H_t \approx H_s$ (very low velocity head). > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B specific:** > 1. **Forgetting to label axes** with units (H in m, Q in m³/s, η in %) → –2 marks. > 2. **Drawing the efficiency curve as a straight line or parabola** — it is **bell-shaped (asymmetric)**, slightly skewed toward higher Q. (–1 mark) > 3. **Not mentioning the non-overloading property** of backward-curved blades — this is a **favourite KTU viva topic** (2 marks reserved). > 4. **Confusing fan laws with pump affinity laws** — fan laws use Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³; the same as pump laws. (No deduction, but often asked separately.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Blower Performance Test** - ✅ A centrifugal blower operates in three stages: **suction → acceleration → diffusion (volute)**. - ✅ The **volute casing** acts as a **diffuser**, converting kinetic energy to pressure energy (Bernoulli). - ✅ **Euler's equation** governs energy transfer: $H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} U_2}{g}$ for radial entry. - ✅ **Back-ward curved blades** → non-overloading; **forward-curved** → overloading; **radial** → moderate. - ✅ **Orifice formula** for Q includes the **velocity-of-approach factor**: $C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_o}{\sqrt{1-(A_o/A_p)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh_o}$. - ✅ **U-tube water manometer** measuring air pressure → multiply reading by **999** to get **metres of air column**. - ✅ **Pitot-static tube** gives both static ($h_s$) and dynamic ($h_d$) heads → total head $H_t = H_s + H_d$. - ✅ **Power output** uses $H_t$ (not $H_s$): $P_{out} = \rho_a g Q H_t$ in Watts. - ✅ **Power input** uses $\sqrt{3} V I \cos\phi$ for 3-phase AC; $V \cdot I$ for DC; rope brake for shaft power. - ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$; report in **%** by multiplying decimal by 100. - ✅ **Three characteristic curves** to plot: **H–Q, P–Q, η–Q at constant N**. - ✅ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** = peak of the bell-shaped η–Q curve. - ✅ **Shut-off head** = head at Q = 0 (delivery valve fully closed). - ✅ **Free delivery** = max Q at H_t ≈ H_s (delivery valve fully open). - ✅ **Fan Laws** (geometrically similar blowers): Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³. - ✅ **Standard atmospheric values**: $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². - ✅ **Common KTU traps**: 999-factor missing, mm-vs-m confusion, $H_s$ used instead of $H_t$, missing velocity-of-approach factor. > **End of Topic — Performance Test on Centrifugal Blower (PCMUL508, Module Experiment 2)**

topics.title: Determination of Flash and Fire Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Flash and Fire Point

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FLASH POINT** is the lowest temperature of a lubricating oil (or any petroleum product) at which the application of a small test flame causes the **vapours above the oil surface to ignite momentarily** (a brief flash of light) under specified test conditions, but the vapours do **NOT** sustain combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRE POINT** is the lowest temperature of the oil at which the oil vapours, once ignited by a test flame, **continue to burn steadily for at least 5 seconds** under the specified test conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**: The fire point is **always higher** than the flash point by a margin of typically **5 °C to 40 °C**, depending on the volatility of the oil. The flash point is the boundary of *incipient* combustion; the fire point is the boundary of *sustained* combustion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a frying pan with cooking oil left on a switched-off stove.

- As the pan slowly cools, at some temperature you can briefly pass a lit matchstick over the oil and see a tiny **blue whoosh of flame** that vanishes immediately. That momentary ignition temperature is the **flash point** — the vapours were *just enough* to ignite but not enough to keep burning.
- Keep cooling (or think of heating up the pan to a higher temperature), and at some still-lower (or still-higher, depending on direction) temperature the match flame will set the oil on **continuous fire** that does not self-extinguish in 5 seconds. That is the **fire point**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in engineering**: A lubricant inside a hot engine sump, gearbox, or transformer must have a flash point **far above** its operating temperature. Otherwise the oil can produce flammable vapours inside crankcases, leading to fire hazards and explosions in IC engines, turbines, and hydraulic systems.

### 1.3 Standard Apparatus Used (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

| Apparatus | Standard Designation | Cup Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** | ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P:69 | Open, wide cup | Lubricating oils, viscous petroleum products |
| Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) | ASTM D93 / IS 1448 P:21 | Closed cup with stirrer | Fuel oils, cut-back bitumens |
| Abel Closed Cup | IP 170 | Closed cup | Petroleum stocks, light distillates |

The KTU experiment uses the **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus** as it is the standard academic lab instrument for testing **lubricating oils**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Test Atmosphere**: Atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg)** at a temperature of **25 °C (298 K)**.
- **Heating Rate (COC)**: Oil temperature is raised at **5 to 6 °C per minute** initially, then reduced to **3 °C per minute** as the flash point is approached.
- **Test Flame Application**: Test flame is passed across the cup every **2 °C** rise near the expected flash temperature.
- **Standard Ignition Duration**: Fire point is declared when combustion continues for **≥ 5 seconds**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Thermodynamic Principle

A liquid oil is in equilibrium with its own vapour at any temperature. The vapour pressure above the oil increases with temperature according to the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation**:

$$\frac{d(\ln P_v)}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R T^{2}}$$

where:

- $P_v$ = saturated vapour pressure of the oil (Pa)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation of the oil (J/kg)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

The flash point is reached when $P_v$ becomes a critical fraction of ambient pressure such that the vapour–air mixture falls within the **flammability limits** (Lower Explosive Limit, LEL).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Stepwise Procedure (Cleveland Open Cup)

1. **Cleaning**: The cup is thoroughly cleaned, dried, and filled with the oil sample up to the filling mark.
2. **Initial Heating**: The oil is heated at a steady rate of **5–6 °C per minute**.
3. **Reduced Heating**: At about **17 °C below the expected flash point**, the heating rate is reduced to **3 °C per minute**.
4. **Flame Application**: A small test flame (diameter ≈ **3.2 to 4.8 mm**) is passed across the cup at intervals of every **2 °C** rise.
5. **Flash Point Reading**: The temperature at which a **distinct flash** appears on the oil surface is recorded as the flash point.
6. **Continued Heating**: Heating is continued, and flame tests repeated at 2 °C intervals.
7. **Fire Point Reading**: The temperature at which the oil **continues to burn for at least 5 seconds** after flame application is recorded as the fire point.
8. **Re-Testing**: The procedure is repeated for a fresh sample and the **mean** value is calculated.

### 2.3 Why Heat-Up Rate and Flame Size Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why**: A faster heating rate causes a **thermal lag** between the bulk oil and the surface vapours, leading to a falsely high reading. A larger test flame introduces additional energy and ignites lean mixtures, again giving a higher reading. KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand this *cause–effect* linkage.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vapour pressure vs. temperature | $\ln P_v = -\dfrac{h_{fg}}{R T} + C$ | $C$ is integration constant |
| 2 | Flash point criterion | $P_v \geq P_{\text{LEL}}$ | LEL = Lower Explosive Limit |
| 3 | Mean flash point | $T_f = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$ | Arithmetic mean of two trials |
| 4 | Atmospheric correction (ASTM D92) | $T_{c} = T_{o} + C \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - P)$ | $T_c$ in °C |
| 5 | Coefficient $C$ for correction | Depends on observed flash point (see Table) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Fire–Flash difference | $\Delta T = T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{flash}}$ | Typically **5 °C – 40 °C** |
| 7 | Heating rate (initial) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt}\Big\vert_{\text{init}} = 5 \text{ to } 6$ | °C/min |
| 8 | Heating rate (near flash) | $\dfrac{dT}{dt}\Big\vert_{\text{final}} = 3$ | °C/min |
| 9 | Test flame diameter | $d = 3.2 \text{ to } 4.8$ | mm |

### 2.5 Coefficient $C$ Table for Atmospheric Correction

| Observed Flash Point ($T_o$ in °C) | Coefficient $C$ |
|---|---|
| 100 to 150 | 0.555 |
| 150 to 200 | 0.580 |
| 200 to 250 | 0.610 |
| 250 to 300 | 0.640 |
| 300 to 350 | 0.660 |
| 350 to 400 | 0.680 |

> [!NOTE]
> When the **atmospheric pressure $P$ is less than 760 mm Hg**, the observed flash point will be **lower** than the true value (vapours reach the LEL more easily at low pressure). Hence the **correction is positive**. When $P > 760$ mm Hg, the correction is negative.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Selection of Crankcase Lubricants**: Flash point must exceed the sump temperature by a wide margin (typically ≥ 200 °C for engine oils).
- **Transformer Oils**: A high flash point (≥ 140 °C) is mandatory to prevent fires from electrical faults.
- **Hydraulic Fluids**: Fire-resistant hydraulic fluids are graded using flash and fire points (HFA, HFB, HFC, HFD categories per ISO 6743).
- **Storage and Transport Safety**: Petroleum classifications (Class A, B, C) by flash point dictate warehouse design, tanker construction, and shipping regulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Atmospheric Pressure Correction for Flash Point

Let us derive the empirical ASTM D92 correction formula from first principles of partial-pressure combustion.

Let $P_v$ = partial pressure of oil vapour at flash point.
Let $P_{\text{LEL}}$ = partial pressure corresponding to the lower explosive limit (a property of the vapour–air mixture).

At the flash point, the criterion is:

$$P_v = P_{\text{LEL}} \cdot \frac{P_{\text{atm, std}}}{P_{\text{atm, actual}}}$$

Using the linearised Clausius–Clapeyron around a reference temperature $T_0$:

$$P_v = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{h_{fg}}{R T}\right)$$

Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P_v = P_{\text{LEL}}$ gives:

$$\frac{h_{fg}}{R T^{2}} \cdot dT = \frac{dP_{\text{atm}}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$

For small changes, integrating and substituting the empirical constants used by ASTM yields the engineering approximation:

$$\Delta T \approx C \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - P)$$

Therefore the **corrected flash point** is:

$$T_{c} = T_{o} + C \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - P)$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem**: A Cleveland Open Cup test on a sample of lubricating oil gave the following observations. Calculate the **corrected flash point** and **corrected fire point**.

**Given Data:**
- Trial 1: Flash point = 218 °C, Fire point = 242 °C
- Trial 2: Flash point = 220 °C, Fire point = 244 °C
- Atmospheric pressure during test: $P = 745$ mm Hg

**Step 1 — Mean of Two Trials (Flash Point)**

$$T_{o,\text{flash}} = \frac{218 + 220}{2} = \frac{438}{2} = 219 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Mean of Two Trials (Fire Point)**

$$T_{o,\text{fire}} = \frac{242 + 244}{2} = \frac{486}{2} = 243 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Identify Coefficient $C$**

From the Coefficient Table for flash point between **200 °C to 250 °C**, we use **$C = 0.610$**.
For fire point between **250 °C to 300 °C**, we use **$C = 0.640$**.

**Step 4 — Atmospheric Correction for Flash Point**

$$\Delta T_{\text{flash}} = C \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - 745)$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{flash}} = 0.610 \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (15)$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{flash}} = 0.610 \cdot 0.0015 = 0.000915 \text{ °C} \approx 0.001 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Atmospheric Correction for Fire Point**

$$\Delta T_{\text{fire}} = 0.640 \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (15) = 0.00096 \text{ °C} \approx 0.001 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 — Final Corrected Values**

$$T_{c,\text{flash}} = 219 + 0.001 = 219.001 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{c,\text{fire}} = 243 + 0.001 = 243.001 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 7 — Fire–Flash Margin**

$$\Delta T = T_{c,\text{fire}} - T_{c,\text{flash}} = 243.001 - 219.001 = 24 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A fire–flash margin of **24 °C** is **typical for good-grade lubricating oils**. Higher volatility fuels (petrol, naphtha) show margins as low as **5 °C**, while heavy residual oils may show margins of **30–40 °C**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Test Methods

| Property | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) |
|---|---|---|
| Cup condition | Open to atmosphere | Closed, with lid and stirrer |
| ASTM Standard | D92 | D93 |
| Heating rate | 5–6 °C/min then 3 °C/min | 5–6 °C/min with continuous stirring |
| Stirring | None or manual | Mechanical, 90–250 rpm |
| Test flame | Moved manually across surface | Dipped through lid opening |
| Flash point reading | Slightly **higher** than PMCC | Slightly **lower** (richer vapour space) |
| Typical use | Lubricating oils, asphalts | Fuel oils, diesel, kerosene |
| Air current sensitivity | **High** (open cup) | Low (enclosed) |

### 3.4 Sources of Error (Frequent in KTU Viva)

1. **Non-uniform heating** — temperature gradient between bottom and surface of oil.
2. **Incorrect flame size** — test flame too large gives falsely low flash point.
3. **Skipping the 17 °C pre-conditioning step** — incorrect heating rate near flash point.
4. **Dirty cup residue** — leftover carbon deposits lower the flash point artificially.
5. **Air drafts** — COC is sensitive; labs must have a draught-free enclosure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cleveland Open Cup Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Heat Source Bunsen Burner or Electric Heater] --> B[Heating Plate Tripo]
    B --> C[Test Cup Brass Cleveland Cup]
    C --> D[Oil Sample Liquid Lubricant]
    C --> E[Thermometer ASTM 11C or 12C]
    C --> F[Test Flame Pilot Flame 4mm]
    F --> G[Flame Application Device Sweep Mechanism]
    H[Air Surroundings Draught Free Zone] --> C
    I[Operator] --> G
    I --> A
    I --> E
    J[Stopwatch] --> I

    classDef hardware fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#145a32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F hardware
    class G,H process
    class I,J control
```

### 4.2 Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Start] --> B1[Clean and Dry Cleveland Cup]
    B1 --> C1[Pour Oil Sample up to Mark]
    C1 --> D1[Place Thermometer and Position Cup on Tripod]
    D1 --> E1[Heat at 5 to 6 degree C per minute]
    E1 --> F1{17 degree C below expected flash}
    F1 -- No --> E1
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[Reduce Heating to 3 degree C per minute]
    G1 --> H1[Pass Test Flame Every 2 degree C Rise]
    H1 --> I1{Flash Observed?}
    I1 -- No --> H1
    I1 -- Yes --> J1[Record Flash Point Temperature T1]
    J1 --> K1[Continue Heating and Flame Testing]
    K1 --> L1{Burn for 5 Seconds?}
    L1 -- No --> K1
    L1 -- Yes --> M1[Record Fire Point Temperature T2]
    M1 --> N1[Repeat with Fresh Sample for Trial 2]
    N1 --> O1[Calculate Mean and Apply Atmospheric Correction]
    O1 --> P1[Report Final Flash and Fire Point]

    classDef decision fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#1b2631,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class F1,I1,L1 decision
    class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,G1,H1,J1,K1,M1,N1,O1 step
    class P1 final
```

### 4.3 Functional Layout of the Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Operator Panel] --> P2[Heat Control Knob]
    P2 --> P3[Heating Coil]
    P3 --> P4[Brass Test Cup]
    P4 --> P5[Oil Surface Vapour Zone]
    P6[Thermometer Probe] --> P4
    P7[Pilot Flame Source] --> P8[Flame Nozzle]
    P8 --> P5

    classDef panel fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hardware fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#935116,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class P1,P2 panel
    class P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 hardware
```
