<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Flash Point and Fire Point of a lubricating oil. Why is fire point always greater than flash point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Flash Point (1 mark)**: It is the lowest temperature at which the oil vapours, when a small test flame is passed over the surface, **ignite momentarily with a brief flash** but do not continue to burn.
- **Fire Point (1 mark)**: It is the lowest temperature at which the oil vapours, once ignited, **continue to burn steadily for at least 5 seconds**.
- **Reason for Fire Point > Flash Point (1 mark)**: The flash point marks the **incipient combustion** condition where vapour concentration just reaches the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL). At this temperature, vapours are insufficient to sustain combustion. As temperature rises further, vapour concentration increases, eventually reaching a value that supports continuous combustion — this is the fire point.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. State the standard heating rates prescribed by ASTM D92 (Cleveland Open Cup method) for the determination of flash point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Initial heating rate (1 mark)**: Oil is heated at **5 to 6 °C per minute** from ambient up to about **17 °C below the expected flash point**.
- **Final heating rate (1 mark)**: From 17 °C below the expected flash point up to the flash point, the heating rate is reduced to **3 °C per minute**.
- **Flame application (1 mark)**: A small test flame of diameter **3.2 to 4.8 mm** is passed across the cup at intervals of every **2 °C rise** in temperature.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
> **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Cleveland Open Cup apparatus** used for the determination of flash and fire point of a lubricating oil. List the safety precautions to be observed during the test. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Apparatus Description (4 marks):**
The Cleveland Open Cup apparatus consists of:

- **Test cup**: A brass cup of standard dimensions, open at the top, with a filling mark.
- **Heating plate**: A tripod stand with a bunsen burner or electric heater below the cup.
- **Thermometer**: ASTM 11C (range -6 to 400 °C) or ASTM 12C, immersed in the oil.
- **Test flame applicator**: A small gas jet producing a flame of diameter **3.2 to 4.8 mm**, mounted on a sweep arm to pass the flame across the cup.
- **Draught shield**: A shield around the apparatus to prevent air currents.
- **Stopwatch**: To time the 5-second duration for fire point.

**(ii) Safety Precautions (3 marks):**

1. Conduct the test in a **draught-free**, well-ventilated area away from flammable materials.
2. **Never heat the oil unattended**; the operator must remain present at all times.
3. Use **heat-resistant gloves** and a **face shield** when handling the hot cup after the test.
4. Ensure **proper extinguishing equipment** (CO2 extinguisher, fire blanket) is available.
5. Discard the oil sample only after it has **cooled below 50 °C**.
6. The test flame must be applied **quickly** (within 1 second) and the operator must not lean over the cup.

---

**(b)** A Cleveland Open Cup test on a sample of lubricating oil gave the following results: **[7 marks]**

- Trial 1: Flash point = 215 °C, Fire point = 238 °C
- Trial 2: Flash point = 217 °C, Fire point = 240 °C

Atmospheric pressure = **748 mm Hg**. Determine the **corrected flash point** and **corrected fire point** using the ASTM D92 correction. Also compute the fire–flash margin and comment on the oil quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Mean values:**

$$\bar{T}_{\text{flash}} = \frac{215 + 217}{2} = 216 \text{ °C}$$

$$\bar{T}_{\text{fire}} = \frac{238 + 240}{2} = 239 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient $C$ selection:**

- Flash point 216 °C lies in **200–250 °C** range → $C = 0.610$
- Fire point 239 °C lies in **200–250 °C** range → $C = 0.610$

**Step 3 — Atmospheric correction:**

$$\Delta T_{\text{flash}} = 0.610 \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - 748) = 0.610 \cdot 0.0012 = 0.000732 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{\text{fire}} = 0.610 \cdot 0.0012 = 0.000732 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Corrected values:**

$$T_{c,\text{flash}} = 216 + 0.0007 \approx 216.001 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{c,\text{fire}} = 239 + 0.0007 \approx 239.001 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Fire–Flash margin:**

$$\Delta T = 239.001 - 216.001 = 23 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on oil quality (1 mark):**

> [!TIP]
> A fire–flash margin of **23 °C** and a flash point above **200 °C** indicate a **good-quality lubricating oil** suitable for use in **IC engine crankcases, gearboxes, and circulating lubrication systems** where bulk oil temperatures may reach 100–130 °C.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
> **Question B (14 marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a)** Explain with theory the **significance of flash and fire point** in the selection of lubricants for IC engines and transformer oils. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) IC Engine Crankcase Lubricants (3.5 marks):**

- The sump oil in an IC engine operates at temperatures of **80 °C to 130 °C** under normal conditions and may exceed **150 °C** under heavy load.
- A lubricant with a **flash point above 200 °C** is essential to prevent formation of flammable vapour–air mixtures inside the crankcase.
- A high fire point ensures the oil will **not sustain combustion** even if ignited by a hot spot, exhaust leak, or electrical spark.
- Flash point also indicates the **volatility** of the oil — a low flash point indicates high volatility, leading to **high oil consumption** and frequent top-up.

**(ii) Transformer Oils (3.5 marks):**

- Transformer oil serves as both **insulator** and **coolant**, operating at temperatures up to **90–100 °C** continuously and up to **140 °C** under overload.
- A high flash point (≥ **140 °C** as per IS 335) ensures the oil does not produce flammable vapours inside the sealed tank.
- A high **fire point** (≥ **160 °C**) is also mandated to ensure that any arc-induced ignition does not lead to sustained tank fire or explosion.
- Flash point is therefore a **safety classification parameter** for electrical insulating oils.

---

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for determining flash and fire point of a given lubricating oil using the Cleveland Open Cup apparatus. Mention the standard heating rates and flame application intervals. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Step | Action | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clean and dry the brass test cup thoroughly | No residue from previous test |
| 2 | Fill the cup with oil up to the filling mark | As per ASTM D92 |
| 3 | Position cup on tripod, place thermometer | Thermometer bulb immersed, not touching bottom |
| 4 | Heat at initial rate | **5–6 °C per minute** |
| 5 | At 17 °C below expected flash, reduce heating | **3 °C per minute** |
| 6 | Pass test flame at intervals | Every **2 °C rise** |
| 7 | Record temperature of momentary flash | This is the **flash point** |
| 8 | Continue heating and flame testing | As per step 6 |
| 9 | Record temperature at which oil burns continuously for **≥ 5 seconds** | This is the **fire point** |
| 10 | Repeat with fresh sample and take mean | Improves accuracy |

> Each step above is worth approximately **0.7 marks**, with extra marks for correctly stating the heating rates and the **ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P:69** standard reference.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Flash Point with Fire Point** in viva voce — examiners deliberately use these interchangeably to test depth of understanding.
> 2. **Forgetting the reduced heating rate (3 °C/min)** near the flash point — full marks are not awarded unless this is explicitly stated.
> 3. **Wrong sign of atmospheric correction** — when pressure is below 760 mm Hg (most Kerala labs are below standard pressure), the correction is **additive**, not subtractive.
> 4. **Using COC for fuel oils** — wrong apparatus selection; PMCC must be used for fuels.
> 5. **Failing to mention safety precautions** — KTU examiners specifically award marks for safety in the 14-mark procedure question.
> 6. **No mention of duplicate trials and mean value reporting** — single-trial results lose method marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flash and Fire Point Determination**

- ✅ **Flash Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil vapours ignite momentarily but do not sustain combustion.
- ✅ **Fire Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil vapours burn continuously for ≥ 5 seconds.
- ✅ **Fire Point is ALWAYS greater than Flash Point** by 5 °C to 40 °C.
- ✅ **Standard Apparatus**: **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** — ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P:69 (used in KTU lab).
- ✅ **Initial Heating Rate**: 5–6 °C/min.
- ✅ **Heating Rate near Flash Point**: 3 °C/min (from 17 °C below expected flash).
- ✅ **Test Flame Diameter**: 3.2 to 4.8 mm.
- ✅ **Flame Application Interval**: Every 2 °C rise in temperature.
- ✅ **Fire Point Duration Criterion**: Continuous burning for at least 5 seconds.
- ✅ **Atmospheric Correction Formula**: $T_c = T_o + C \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (760 - P)$, where $C$ depends on observed flash point.
- ✅ **Coefficient $C$**: 0.555 (100–150 °C), 0.580 (150–200 °C), 0.610 (200–250 °C), 0.640 (250–300 °C), 0.660 (300–350 °C), 0.680 (350–400 °C).
- ✅ **Physical Principle**: Vapour pressure reaches the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL).
- ✅ **Engineering Significance**: Determines fire safety classification, storage rules, and suitability for crankcase, gearbox, and transformer applications.
- ✅ **Closed Cup vs Open Cup**: PMCC (closed) gives a slightly lower reading due to richer vapour–air mixture; COC (open) is sensitive to air drafts.
- ✅ **Safety**: Always work in a draught-free zone, never leave the test unattended, and have CO2 extinguisher ready.
- ✅ **Reporting**: Always report the **mean of two trials** with the **atmospheric correction applied**.
- ✅ **Common Error**: Confusing flash point with fire-resistivity or auto-ignition temperature — they are three distinct properties.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of COP of Vapour Compression Refrigeration System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of COP of Vapour Compression Refrigeration System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is a closed thermodynamic cycle that uses a circulating refrigerant as the working fluid, which undergoes phase change (evaporation and condensation) to transfer heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cooled space) to a high-temperature reservoir (surroundings). The system is the working principle behind household refrigerators, air-conditioners, deep freezers, and cold-storage plants. The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigeration system is formally defined as the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the net work input supplied to the compressor. Unlike an engine's efficiency, COP of a refrigerator is always **greater than unity**. $$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (Refrigerating Effect)}}{\text{Net Work Input}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{\text{net}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** COP is a *dimensionless performance metric* — it is NOT an efficiency. For a domestic refrigerator, a typical COP ranges from **2.0 to 3.5**, meaning for every **1 kW** of electrical input, the system removes **2 to 3.5 kW** of heat from the food compartment. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pump of Heat" Imagine a bucket of hot water on the floor and you want to cool it. You can use a sponge to absorb heat from the water, walk over to an open window, and squeeze the heat outside. Then walk back and repeat. You are essentially **pumping heat uphill** — from cold to hot — which goes against natural heat flow. A refrigeration system does exactly this, but mechanically: - The **sponge** is the **refrigerant** (e.g., R-134a, R-22). - The **walking cycle** is the **compressor + condenser + expansion valve + evaporator** loop. - The **squeezing (heat rejection)** happens at the **condenser**. - The **absorbing (heat pickup)** happens at the **evaporator**. > [!NOTE] > **Second Law Connection:** Heat naturally flows from hot to cold. To move heat from cold to hot (refrigeration), external work MUST be supplied. This is exactly what the compressor does — it acts as the "leg muscle" of the cycle. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Bold | Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Refrigerating Effect** | $RE$ | **kJ/kg** or **kW** | Heat absorbed in evaporator | | **Compressor Work** | $W_c$ | **kJ/kg** or **kW** | Work input to compressor | | **Condenser Heat Rejection** | $Q_c$ | **kJ/kg** or **kW** | Heat rejected at condenser | | **Coefficient of Performance** | $COP$ | **Dimensionless** | Overall system performance | | **Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** | Circulation rate of refrigerant | By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to the cyclic system: $$\dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q}_e + \dot{W}_c$$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle — Four Processes The ideal VCRS consists of **four steady-flow processes** traced on a Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram: ### Process 1→2 : Compression in the Compressor - **What:** Low-pressure, low-temperature saturated vapour is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature **superheated vapour**. - **How:** Compressor (reciprocating/centrifugal/scroll) consumes work $W_c$. - **State:** $h_1$ (saturated vapour at evaporator pressure) → $h_2$ (superheated vapour at condenser pressure). ### Process 2→3 : Condensation in the Condenser - **What:** High-pressure superheated vapour rejects heat to surroundings and becomes **saturated liquid**. - **How:** Air-cooled or water-cooled heat exchanger. - **State:** $h_2$ → $h_3$ (saturated liquid at condenser pressure). ### Process 3→4 : Throttling (Expansion) through the Expansion Valve - **What:** High-pressure liquid is expanded isenthalpically to a low-pressure, low-temperature **two-phase mixture**. - **How:** Throttle valve, capillary tube, or expansion device. - **Critical Property:** Isenthalpic process → $h_3 = h_4$. ### Process 4→1 : Evaporation in the Evaporator - **What:** Low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat from the cooled space and becomes **saturated vapour**. - **How:** Heat flows from cold space to refrigerant. - **State:** $h_4$ → $h_1$ (saturated vapour at evaporator pressure). > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Trick:** "**C**ompressor **C**ondenses, **C**ompresses; **E**vaporator **E**vaporates, **E**xpands" — the device name describes the phase change happening AT THE INLET/EXIT of that component. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Refrigerating Effect per kg** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant | | 2 | **Compressor Work per kg** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Work done ON the refrigerant | | 3 | **Condenser Heat Rejection per kg** | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Heat dumped to surroundings | | 4 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $\displaystyle COP_{\text{ref}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | **Most asked formula** | | 5 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $\displaystyle COP_{\text{hp}} = \frac{h_2 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | Heating mode COP | | 6 | **Relation** | $COP_{\text{hp}} = COP_{\text{ref}} + 1$ | Dimensionless | Energy conservation | | 7 | **Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Where $\dot{Q}_e$ is in kW | | 8 | **Power Input to Compressor** | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Actual shaft power | | 9 | **Volumetric Refrigerating Capacity** | $\dot{Q}_v = \dot{m} \cdot v_1$ | m³/s | For compressor sizing | | 10 | **Isenthalpic Expansion Check** | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Constant enthalpy throttling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note:** In all the formula table rows above, the subtraction operator is **minus (−)**, NOT the absolute-value pipe character. The enthalpy difference $h_1 - h_4$ is computed as a *signed scalar* from refrigerant property tables, not as $\vert h_1 - h_4 \vert$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HVAC Industry:** Sizing AC compressors for residential and commercial buildings. - **Cold Chain Logistics:** Designing refrigerated trucks and cold-storage warehouses. - **Food Preservation:** Domestic refrigerators (R-134a), freezers (R-404A). - **Cryogenics & Pharmaceuticals:** Medical cold storage (vaccines, blood plasma). - **Process Industries:** LNG plants, chemical industry, petrochemical refineries. - **Green Refrigerants:** Shift from R-22 (HCFC) to R-134a, R-410A, R-744 (CO₂), R-290 (propane) due to ozone-depletion and global-warming concerns. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of COP from First Principles ### Step 1 — Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to Each Component For a steady-flow control volume with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{in}})$$ ### Step 2 — Apply to Compressor (Process 1→2, adiabatic) Adiabatic compression → $\dot{Q} = 0$. Compressor consumes work, so $\dot{W}_c$ enters the CV (negative from system's perspective): $$0 - (-\dot{W}_c) = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$ $$\therefore \dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply to Condenser (Process 2→3, constant pressure) No work done. Heat is rejected to surroundings (negative for refrigerant): $$-\dot{Q}_c - 0 = \dot{m}(h_3 - h_2)$$ $$\therefore \dot{Q}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply to Expansion Valve (Process 3→4, throttling) Throttling is **adiabatic** with **no work** and the kinetic-energy change is negligible, so: $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ This is the *isenthalpic* property — the cornerstone of the throttling process. ### Step 5 — Apply to Evaporator (Process 4→1, constant pressure) Heat is absorbed by refrigerant from the cold space: $$\dot{Q}_e - 0 = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$ $$\therefore \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ ### Step 6 — Apply Definition of COP By definition, COP of a refrigerator is the desired refrigerating effect divided by the work input: $$\begin{aligned} COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{\text{Refrigerating Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} \\ &= \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{\dot{W}_c} \\ &= \frac{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)} \\ COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **master equation** the KTU examiner expects every student to derive on the answer sheet. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Exam Problem) > **Problem Statement:** > A vapour compression refrigeration system uses **R-134a** as refrigerant. The following enthalpy values are observed at the four state points of the cycle: > > $h_1 = 240 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (compressor inlet, saturated vapour) > $h_2 = 270 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (compressor outlet, superheated vapour) > $h_3 = 115 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (condenser outlet, saturated liquid) > $h_4 = 115 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (expansion valve outlet, two-phase mixture) > > The refrigerant mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}$. > > **Determine:** (a) Refrigerating Effect, (b) Compressor Work, (c) Condenser Heat Rejection, (d) COP of the system, (e) Power input to compressor. ### Solution: **Part (a) — Refrigerating Effect per kg of refrigerant:** $$\begin{aligned} RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\ &= 240 - 115 \\ &= 125 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Total Refrigerating Effect (in kW):** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{Q}_e &= \dot{m} \times RE \\ &= 0.05 \times 125 \\ &= 6.25 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b) — Compressor Work per kg:** $$\begin{aligned} w_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\ &= 270 - 240 \\ &= 30 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (c) — Condenser Heat Rejection per kg:** $$\begin{aligned} q_c &= h_2 - h_3 \\ &= 270 - 115 \\ &= 155 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification via Energy Balance:** $q_c = RE + w_c = 125 + 30 = 155 \text{ kJ/kg}$ ✔ (Energy is conserved.) **Part (d) — Coefficient of Performance:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\ &= \frac{125}{30} \\ COP_{\text{ref}} &= 4.167 \text{ (dimensionless)} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (e) — Power Input to Compressor:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{W}_c &= \dot{m} \times w_c \\ &= 0.05 \times 30 \\ &= 1.5 \text{ kW} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** With just **1.5 kW** of electrical input, the system extracts **6.25 kW** of heat from the cold space — a remarkable demonstration of how VCRS multiplies the utility of electrical energy for cooling. ## 3.3 Effect of Subcooling and Superheating on COP In **actual** (non-ideal) cycles, the refrigerant is often **subcooled** below the saturation temperature at condenser exit and **superheated** above the saturation temperature at compressor inlet. This modifies the enthalpies: $$\begin{aligned} \text{With Subcooling: } & h_3' < h_3, \text{ thus } h_4' = h_3' < h_4 \\ \Rightarrow & COP_{\text{subcooled}} > COP_{\text{unsaturated}} \\ \text{With Superheating: } & h_1' > h_1 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{COP may increase OR decrease depending on compression process (isentropic vs actual)} \end{aligned}$$ For an **isentropic compressor** with superheat, COP generally **decreases** because $h_2$ rises more steeply than $h_1$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR state1((State 1
Saturated Vapour
h1)) comp[Compressor
Wc in] state2((State 2
Superheated Vapour
h2)) cond[Condenser
Qc out]) state3((State 3
Saturated Liquid
h3)) expv[Expansion Valve
h3 equals h4]) state4((State 4
Liquid Vapour Mix
h4)) evap[Evaporator
Qe in]) state1 --> comp comp --> state2 state2 --> cond cond --> state3 state3 --> expv expv --> state4 state4 --> evap evap --> state1 qein[/Qe Refrigerating Effect/] wcin[/Wc Work Input/] qcout[/Qc Heat Rejected/] evap -.- qein comp -.- wcin cond -.- qcout ``` ## 4.2 Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) Diagram Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHD["PRESSURE ENTHALPY P h DIAGRAM"] dome["Saturation Dome
Liquid Line left
Vapour Line right"] crit["Critical Point"] point1["Point 1
Saturated Vapour
Evaporator Pressure"] point2["Point 2
Superheated Vapour
Condenser Pressure"] point3["Point 3

Condenser Pressure"] point4["Point 4
Two Phase Mix

ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION"] cond_line["Process 2 to 3
CONSTANT P CONDENSATION"] throt_line["Process 3 to 4
ISENTHALPIC THROTTLING"] evap_line["Process 4 to 1
CONSTANT P EVAPORATION"] end point1 --- comp_line --- point2 point2 --- cond_line --- point3 point3 --- throt_line --- point4 point4 --- evap_line --- point1 point1 --- dome point3 --- dome ``` ## 4.3 Experimental Setup Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EXP["LABORATORY VCRS TEST RIG"] comp_unit[Hermetic Compressor
with Wattmeter] cond_unit[Water Cooled Condenser
with Flowmeter] exp_unit[Capillary Expansion Device] evap_unit[Evaporator Coil in Insulated Tank] t1[Thermometer T1
Compressor Inlet] t2[Thermometer T2
Compressor Outlet] t3[Thermometer T3
Condenser Outlet] t4[Thermometer T4
Evaporator Inlet] p1[Pressure Gauge P1
Low Side] p2[Pressure Gauge P2
High Side] end comp_unit --> cond_unit --> exp_unit --> evap_unit --> comp_unit t1 -.-> comp_unit t2 -.-> cond_unit p1 -.-> evap_unit p2 -.-> cond_unit ``` > [!NOTE] > **Lab Instrument Mapping:** Temperatures T1, T2, T3, T4 are measured using calibrated **thermocouples or RTDs**, while pressures P1, P2 are measured using **bourdon-tube pressure gauges**. The wattmeter reading provides the actual electrical power input $\dot{W}_c$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system. Why is it always greater than unity?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the refrigerating effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input supplied to the compressor: $$COP_{\text{ref}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{\dot{W}_c}$$ **Why COP > 1:** A refrigerator's desired output is *heat extracted*, not net work output. The heat absorbed from the cold space PLUS the equivalent work input both get rejected at the condenser. Since $\dot{Q}_e$ and $\dot{W}_c$ are both positive quantities contributing to $\dot{Q}_e$, the numerator is always larger than the denominator alone, ensuring COP > 1. Typical household refrigerator COPs range from **2 to 3.5**. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the four processes of an ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle and identify the components in which they occur.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Process | Description | Component | |---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic compression of vapour | Compressor | | 2 → 3 | Constant-pressure condensation | Condenser | | 3 → 4 | Isenthalpic (throttling) expansion | Expansion Valve | | 4 → 1 | Constant-pressure evaporation | Evaporator | A neat labelled P-h diagram (2 marks) and process description (1 mark) is the typical marking split. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the COP of an ideal vapour compression refrigeration system in terms of refrigerant enthalpies at the four state points. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In an experiment on a VCRS using R-134a, the following observations are recorded: $h_1 = 247 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 281 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = 116 \text{ kJ/kg}$. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is $0.04 \text{ kg/s}$. Calculate the COP, refrigerating effect in kW, and compressor power input. **(7 Marks)** #### Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** Apply SFEE to the compressor (adiabatic): $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Apply SFEE to the expansion valve (throttling, no work, adiabatic): $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ Apply SFEE to the evaporator (constant pressure, heat absorbed): $$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) \quad \text{...(3)}$$ Substituting (2) into (3): $$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_3) \quad \text{...(4)}$$ COP by definition: $$\begin{aligned} COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{\dot{W}_c} \\ &= \frac{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_3)}{\dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)} \\ COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** Using $h_3 = h_4 = 116 \text{ kJ/kg}$: **Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 247 - 116 = 131 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Refrigerating Effect (kW):** $$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.04 \times 131 = 5.24 \text{ kW}$$ **Compressor Work per kg:** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 281 - 247 = 34 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Compressor Power Input:** $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \times w_c = 0.04 \times 34 = 1.36 \text{ kW}$$ **COP of the system:** $$\begin{aligned} COP_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\ &= \frac{131}{34} \\ COP_{\text{ref}} &= 3.85 \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Draw a neat labelled Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram of a vapour compression refrigeration system and explain the four processes. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A refrigerator operates on the ideal Vapour Compression cycle using R-12 refrigerant. The enthalpies at various state points are: $h_1 = 183 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 209 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = 71 \text{ kJ/kg}$. If the compressor consumes **2.5 kW** of power, find the mass flow rate of refrigerant and the refrigerating capacity in **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)**. **(7 Marks)** #### Solution: **Part (a) — P-h Diagram and Processes (7 Marks):** Refer to the schematic in SECTION 4.2. Labelled diagram = 4 marks, process explanation = 3 marks. Key features to mark: - Saturation dome (liquid line on left, vapour line on right). - Critical point marked at top-right of dome. - Point 1 inside or on vapour line at evaporator pressure. - Point 2 to the right of Point 1 at condenser pressure (superheated region). - Point 3 on liquid line at condenser pressure. - Point 4 inside the dome directly below Point 3 (vertical line, indicating $h_3 = h_4$). **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** Given: $h_1 = 183$, $h_2 = 209$, $h_3 = 71$ kJ/kg. Therefore $h_4 = h_3 = 71$ kJ/kg. **Step 1 — Compressor Work per kg:** $$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 209 - 183 = 26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant:** Using $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot w_c$: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{W}_c}{w_c} \\ &= \frac{2.5 \text{ kW}}{26 \text{ kJ/kg}} \\ \dot{m} &= 0.0962 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Refrigerating Effect per kg:** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 183 - 71 = 112 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Refrigerating Capacity (kW):** $$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.0962 \times 112 = 10.77 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to Tonnes of Refrigeration (TR):** $$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Capacity in TR} &= \frac{10.77}{3.517} \\ &= 3.06 \text{ TR} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in VCRS Lab Exam:** > > 1. **Confusing COP and Efficiency:** Students write "COP = efficiency = 70%". COP is **dimensionless** and is **NOT a percentage**. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > > 2. **Forgetting Energy Balance Verification:** Always show $\dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q}_e + \dot{W}_c$. KTU examiners award bonus marks when energy conservation is verified. > > 3. **Sign Error in Throttling:** Writing $h_4 > h_3$ instead of $h_4 = h_3$. Throttling is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy), not constant entropy. > > 4. **Missing Lab Viva Points:** Examiners frequently ask: (a) Why is the expansion valve not a turbine? (b) What is the role of the accumulator/drier? (c) Why is superheat desirable at compressor inlet? Read your lab manual before the exam. > > 5. **Unit Conversion:** Forgetting to convert kJ/s to kW (numerically equal) or TR. $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min}$. Memorize this constant. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **COP is NOT efficiency.** It is a dimensionless multiplier showing how many units of heat are removed per unit of work input. - ✅ **The master COP formula** is $COP_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$. - ✅ **The throttling process** is **isenthalpic**: $h_3 = h_4$ (no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE change). - ✅ **Compressor** increases both pressure and temperature of the refrigerant. - ✅ **Condenser** rejects heat to the surroundings (high-temperature side). - ✅ **Expansion valve** drops pressure without any work extraction (irreversible). - ✅ **Evaporator** absorbs heat from the cold space (low-temperature side). - ✅ **Energy balance:** $\dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q}_e + \dot{W}_c$ — must always hold for any cyclic device. - ✅ **Heat pump relation:** $COP_{\text{hp}} = COP_{\text{ref}} + 1$. - ✅ **Units:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ — useful for sizing problems. - ✅ **Lab instrument connections:** Wattmeter → compressor input; Pressure gauges → high and low sides; Thermometers → at all four state points. - ✅ **Refrigerants used in lab:** R-12 (older), R-134a (modern), R-22 (transitional). Always check which refrigerant your lab uses before picking property tables. - ✅ **Ideal cycle assumption:** Isentropic compression, no pressure drop in evaporator/condenser, no superheat, no subcooling. Real cycles deviate from these. - ✅ **Common viva question:** *"Why does the refrigerator feel hot at the back?"* — because the condenser rejects BOTH the heat extracted from inside AND the work input, all dumped at the back of the fridge.

topics.title: Determination of Effectiveness and LMTD of Parallel Flow and Counter Flow Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL508 - THERMAL AND INTERNAL COMBUSTION ENGINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Effectiveness and LMTD of Parallel Flow and Counter Flow Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Heat Exchanger:** A heat exchanger is a device that facilitates the transfer of thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix directly. In the KTU 2024 scheme lab context, we deal with **concentric tube (double-pipe) heat exchangers** using water as both the hot and cold fluid.

**Parallel Flow Heat Exchanger:** A configuration in which the **hot fluid** and the **cold fluid** enter the heat exchanger at the **same end** and flow in the **same direction** through the length of the exchanger. The temperature difference between the two fluids is **maximum at the inlet** and **decreases continuously** along the length.

**Counter Flow Heat Exchanger:** A configuration in which the **hot fluid** and the **cold fluid** enter the heat exchanger at **opposite ends** and flow in **opposite directions**. The temperature difference can remain **relatively constant** along the length, providing a more uniform driving force.

**LMTD (Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference):** The LMTD is the **logarithmically averaged temperature difference** between the hot and cold fluids along the length of the heat exchanger. It is the **effective mean temperature driving force** for heat transfer in a steady-flow heat exchanger.

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

**Effectiveness (ε):** The ratio of the **actual heat transfer rate** in the heat exchanger to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate** that could be achieved under the given inlet conditions. It is a dimensionless parameter ranging from **0 to 1**.

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{max}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In this experiment, students are required to determine both the **LMTD** and the **effectiveness (ε)** for both **parallel flow** and **counter flow** configurations. The performance comparison is a **mandatory viva question** topic.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Corridor:**

Imagine two corridors in a hospital:
- **Parallel Flow:** Two stretchers carrying patients enter the corridor from the **same door** and move toward the **same exit**. The "hot" stretcher (freshly from the operation theatre) and the "cold" stretcher (going into the OT) are next to each other. As they move, the temperature of the hot patient drops, and the cold patient warms up — but they **approach a common middle temperature** and can never cross each other.
- **Counter Flow:** The two stretchers enter from **opposite ends**. The hot stretcher keeps meeting **freshly arrived cold patients** throughout the journey, maintaining a strong temperature difference. This is why **counter flow is more effective** — like a continuous handshake between two streams of opposite temperatures.

**The Tea-Cooling Analogy for LMTD:**

Suppose you pour hot tea into a cup. The temperature difference between tea and room air is **maximum initially** and **minimum at the end**. You cannot simply average the two — you need a **logarithmic mean** because temperature drops **exponentially** with time. This is exactly why engineers use **LMTD** instead of arithmetic mean temperature difference (AMTD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The heat transfer rate follows Newton's Law of Cooling: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$, where $U$ is the **overall heat transfer coefficient** (in **W/m²·K**) and $A$ is the **heat transfer area** (in **m²**).

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water | $C_p$ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** | Used for both hot and cold water |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** | At standard lab temperature |
| Overall heat transfer coefficient | $U$ | **500–1500 W/m²·K** | For double-pipe exchanger |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** | Measured via rotameter |
| Effectiveness range | $\varepsilon$ | **0 to 1** | Dimensionless |
| Heat exchanger length | $L$ | **1–3 m** | Standard lab setup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (KTU Lab Record):** All temperatures in **°C** (or K — the difference is identical), flow rates in **LPM (liters per minute)** converted to **kg/s** for calculation. The conversion factor: **1 LPM = 1/60 kg/s ≈ 0.01667 kg/s** (assuming $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment rests on **three fundamental principles** of thermal engineering:

**Step 1: Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics)**

Under steady-flow, adiabatic conditions, the heat lost by the hot fluid equals the heat gained by the cold fluid. This is the **principle of energy conservation** in the heat exchanger.

$$
Q_{\text{hot}} = Q_{\text{cold}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})
$$

> **Why?** No heat is lost to the surroundings (ideal assumption in KTU lab calculations). The subscript $h$ denotes hot fluid and $c$ denotes cold fluid.

**Step 2: Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**

For a **double-pipe concentric tube heat exchanger**, the temperature profiles of both fluids vary **continuously** along the length. The integration of the differential heat transfer equation leads to the **LMTD formula**.

For **Parallel Flow** at the two ends:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}}
$$

For **Counter Flow** at the two ends:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}
$$

> **How?** The temperatures are simply paired at the **same physical location** in the heat exchanger — at the inlet-end and the outlet-end.

**Step 3: Effectiveness (ε) — NTU Method Foundation**

The maximum possible heat transfer occurs when the fluid with the **smaller heat capacity rate** undergoes the **maximum possible temperature change**, i.e., it reaches the inlet temperature of the other fluid.

$$
C_{\text{min}} = \min(\dot{m}_h C_{p,h},\ \dot{m}_c C_{p,c})
$$

$$
Q_{\text{max}} = C_{\text{min}} \cdot (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{max}}} = \frac{C_h (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})}{C_{\text{min}} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_c (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}{C_{\text{min}} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}
$$

> **Why divide by Cmin?** The fluid with the **smaller heat capacity rate** is the **bottleneck** — it limits the maximum temperature change achievable. The other fluid can never be heated/cooled beyond this limit.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table for KTU viva and lab exams. The piping-color convention: RED = Hot, BLUE = Cold (standard industrial practice).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Actual Heat Transfer | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | **W (J/s)** | Use hot side; verify with cold side |
| 2 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | **W/K** | Hot side: $C_h$, Cold side: $C_c$ |
| 3 | Maximum Heat Transfer | $Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | **W** | Theoretical upper limit |
| 4 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ | **dimensionless** | Always between 0 and 1 |
| 5 | LMTD (General) | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | **°C or K** | If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, use AMTD |
| 6 | LMTD Parallel — $\Delta T_1$ | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | **°C** | At common inlet end |
| 7 | LMTD Parallel — $\Delta T_2$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ | **°C** | At common outlet end |
| 8 | LMTD Counter — $\Delta T_1$ | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | **°C** | At hot-inlet end |
| 9 | LMTD Counter — $\Delta T_2$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | **°C** | At hot-outlet end |
| 10 | Overall Heat Transfer | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | **W** | Used to verify with $Q$ from step 1 |
| 11 | AMTD (Arithmetic) | $\text{AMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}$ | **°C** | Only valid when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ |
| 12 | Flow Rate Conversion | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{LPM} \times \rho}{60}$ | **kg/s** | $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ for water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** All temperatures are absolute (°C), and $\Delta T$ is always **positive** (T_hot > T_cold at every point). If you get a negative value, swap the indices.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Counter flow is used in **boiler economizers, condensers, and feedwater heaters** for maximum efficiency.
- **HVAC Systems:** Heat exchangers in air-conditioning systems use cross-flow and counter-flow designs.
- **Chemical Industry:** Reactor cooling/heating, crude oil pre-heating in refineries.
- **Automotive:** Radiators are essentially **cross-flow** heat exchangers; EGR coolers use counter flow.
- **Refrigeration:** Evaporators and condensers in vapor-compression cycles.

> **Why Counter Flow Wins:** The counter-flow heat exchanger can theoretically bring the cold fluid outlet temperature **above** the hot fluid outlet temperature — a feat **impossible** in parallel flow. This makes it the preferred choice in nearly all industrial applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD Formula

**Starting Point — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction across the tube wall:**

Consider a differential element of the heat exchanger of length $dx$. Let the hot fluid temperature be $T_h(x)$ and the cold fluid temperature be $T_c(x)$ at that location.

The differential heat transfer through the wall area $dA = \pi D \cdot dx$ is:

$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot [T_h(x) - T_c(x)] = U \cdot \pi D \cdot [T_h(x) - T_c(x)] \cdot dx
$$

**Energy Balance on the Hot Fluid (cooling):**

$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h
$$

**Energy Balance on the Cold Fluid (heating):**

$$
dQ = +\dot{m}_c C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c
$$

**Forming the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:**

Taking the differential of $\Delta T$:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c
$$

Substituting the energy balance expressions:

$$
d(\Delta T) = -\dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \dfrac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}
$$

$$
d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}\right)
$$

$$
dQ = -\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}}
$$

**Equating the two expressions for $dQ$:**

$$
U \cdot \pi D \cdot \Delta T \cdot dx = -\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}}
$$

**Separating variables and integrating from inlet to outlet:**

$$
U \cdot \pi D \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}\right) \int_0^L dx = -\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T}
$$

The left side simplifies to $U \cdot A \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$, and the right side is $-\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1) = \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$.

**Solving for $Q$:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}}
$$

The first factor is the **LMTD**. The second factor, upon manipulation, can be shown to be $1/\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right) = C_h \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_h}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}$ which equals $C_c \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_c}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}$ — both yielding $Q$ from either side.

$$
\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} = U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2024 lab problem):**

In a double-pipe heat exchanger, hot water enters at **80°C** and leaves at **50°C**. Cold water enters at **30°C** and leaves at **48°C**. The hot water flow rate is **600 LPH** (liters per hour) and cold water is **1200 LPH**. Take $C_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K for both fluids.

**Calculate:**
1. Heat transfer rate $Q$ (in kW)
2. LMTD for both parallel and counter flow
3. Effectiveness in both configurations

---

**Solution — Part (a): Heat Transfer Rate**

**Step 1:** Convert flow rates from LPH to kg/s.

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{600 \text{ LPH}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \times 1 \text{ kg/L} = 0.1667 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{1200 \text{ LPH}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \times 1 \text{ kg/L} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply energy balance on hot side.

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

$$
Q = 0.1667 \times 4.186 \times (80 - 50)
$$

$$
Q = 0.1667 \times 4.186 \times 30
$$

$$
Q = 20.93 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification on cold side:**

$$
Q = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times (48 - 30) = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times 18 = 25.12 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why the discrepancy?** In a real experiment, the small mismatch (≈ 4 kW) is due to **heat losses to surroundings** and **measurement errors** in flow rate and temperature. The KTU lab record typically takes the **average** of both, or uses the **hot side** as the standard reference.

**Average Q:**

$$
Q_{avg} = \dfrac{20.93 + 25.12}{2} = 23.03 \text{ kW}
$$

---

**Solution — Part (b): LMTD for Parallel Flow**

For parallel flow, the common inlet is on the left, common outlet on the right.

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out} = 50 - 48 = 2°C
$$

$$
\text{LMTD}_{PF} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{50 - 2}{\ln(50/2)} = \dfrac{48}{\ln(25)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD}_{PF} = \dfrac{48}{3.2189} = 14.91°C
$$

---

**Solution — Part (b): LMTD for Counter Flow**

For counter flow, the hot fluid inlet is paired with the cold fluid outlet.

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 80 - 48 = 32°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 30 = 20°C
$$

$$
\text{LMTD}_{CF} = \dfrac{32 - 20}{\ln(32/20)} = \dfrac{12}{\ln(1.6)} = \dfrac{12}{0.4700} = 25.53°C
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** $\text{LMTD}_{CF} > \text{LMTD}_{PF}$. This is the mathematical proof that **counter flow is more effective** — for the **same heat exchanger area** and the **same flow rates**, counter flow transfers more heat.

---

**Solution — Part (c): Effectiveness**

**Step 1:** Compute heat capacity rates.

$$
C_h = 0.1667 \times 4.186 = 0.6977 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_c = 0.3333 \times 4.186 = 1.3953 \text{ kW/K}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify minimum heat capacity rate.

$$
C_{min} = C_h = 0.6977 \text{ kW/K}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum possible heat transfer.

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} \times (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 0.6977 \times (80 - 30) = 34.88 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** Effectiveness (using hot side heat transfer).

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{20.93}{34.88} = 0.60
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Effectiveness ε = 0.60** means that the heat exchanger achieves **60%** of the theoretical maximum possible heat transfer. A well-designed industrial heat exchanger typically has **ε > 0.7**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| LMTD | 14.91°C | 25.53°C | **Counter Flow** (1.7× higher) |
| Heat transfer for given $UA$ | Lower | Higher | **Counter Flow** |
| Cold outlet can exceed hot outlet? | **No** | **Yes** | **Counter Flow** |
| Effectiveness (same geometry) | Lower | Higher | **Counter Flow** |
| Temperature profile | Crossover at common temp | No crossover (in theory) | **Counter Flow** |
| Industrial preference | Rarely used | **Universally preferred** | **Counter Flow** |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Define Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is it preferred over Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD) in heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**LMTD Definition (1.5 marks):** The Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference is the **effective mean temperature driving force** for heat transfer across a heat exchanger, defined as:

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between hot and cold fluids at the **two ends** of the heat exchanger.

**Why LMTD over AMTD (1.5 marks):** The temperature difference between the two fluids varies **exponentially** along the length of the heat exchanger, not linearly. Therefore, the correct mean is the **logarithmic mean**, not the arithmetic mean. AMTD gives a **significant error** (often > 40%) when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 > 2$. AMTD is valid **only** when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (i.e., the ratio is close to unity, typically < 2).

---

**Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**What is the effectiveness of a heat exchanger? Write its mathematical expression and state the limiting values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The effectiveness $(\varepsilon)$ of a heat exchanger is defined as the **ratio of the actual heat transfer rate** $(Q_{actual})$ to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate** $(Q_{max})$ that could be obtained under the given inlet conditions.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min} (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} \quad \text{(when } C_c = C_{min}\text{)}
$$

**Limiting Values (0.5 marks):** $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$. A value of $\varepsilon = 0$ means **no heat transfer**, and $\varepsilon = 1$ represents the **theoretical maximum** (infinite area heat exchanger).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**In a double-pipe heat exchanger, water is heated by a hot oil stream. The following data are recorded in a parallel flow configuration:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Hot oil inlet temperature, $T_{h,in}$ | **150°C** |
| Hot oil outlet temperature, $T_{h,out}$ | **100°C** |
| Cold water inlet temperature, $T_{c,in}$ | **30°C** |
| Cold water outlet temperature, $T_{c,out}$ | **80°C** |
| Hot oil flow rate | **800 kg/hr** |
| Cold water flow rate | **1200 kg/hr** |
| Specific heat of oil | **2.1 kJ/kg·K** |
| Specific heat of water | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** |

**(a) Calculate the actual rate of heat transfer and verify using energy balance. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the LMTD and the effectiveness of the heat exchanger. If the same heat exchanger is now operated in counter-flow mode with identical inlet conditions, find the new effectiveness. Comment on the results. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert flow rates to kg/s. (1 mark)**

$$
\dot{m}_h = \dfrac{800}{3600} = 0.2222 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \dfrac{1200}{3600} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2: Heat transfer from hot oil side. (2 marks)**

$$
Q_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})
$$

$$
Q_h = 0.2222 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 100) = 0.2222 \times 2.1 \times 50
$$

$$
Q_h = 23.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer from cold water side. (2 marks)**

$$
Q_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

$$
Q_c = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times (80 - 30) = 0.3333 \times 4.186 \times 50
$$

$$
Q_c = 69.76 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Observation and conclusion. (2 marks)**

The two values differ significantly ($Q_c > Q_h$). This is because the cold water outlet temperature (80°C) **cannot physically exceed** the hot oil outlet temperature (100°C) in a real parallel-flow system — the temperatures must converge. The **hot-side value (23.33 kW)** is the actual heat transfer. The cold-side over-estimation is due to **measurement errors** or **non-idealities**; in the KTU lab, we use the **average** or the **hot-side** value as the standard.

$$
\boxed{Q_{actual} = 23.33 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often ignore the **physical impossibility** of cold outlet > hot outlet in parallel flow and report the higher value blindly. The KTU examiner **deducts 2 marks** for this oversight. Always check the **physical validity** of your temperatures first.

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: LMTD for parallel flow. (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} = 150 - 30 = 120°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out} = 100 - 80 = 20°C
$$

$$
\text{LMTD}_{PF} = \dfrac{120 - 20}{\ln(120/20)} = \dfrac{100}{\ln(6)} = \dfrac{100}{1.7918} = 55.81°C
$$

**Step 2: Heat capacity rates and $C_{min}$. (1 mark)**

$$
C_h = 0.2222 \times 2.1 = 0.4667 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_c = 0.3333 \times 4.186 = 1.3953 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_{min} = C_h = 0.4667 \text{ kW/K}
$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness in parallel flow. (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} \times (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 0.4667 \times (150 - 30) = 56.00 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{PF} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{23.33}{56.00} = 0.4166
$$

**Step 4: Counter-flow LMTD and effectiveness. (2 marks)**

For counter flow:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70°C
$$

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 70°C$:

$$
\text{LMTD}_{CF} = 70°C \quad (\text{AMTD = LMTD in this special case})
$$

The counter-flow LMTD (**70°C**) is **higher** than the parallel-flow LMTD (**55.81°C**). Since $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ for the same heat exchanger, the counter flow transfers **more heat** for the same area.

Assuming the **same heat transfer rate** (or computing the new effectiveness from the same inlet conditions):

$$
\varepsilon_{CF} = \varepsilon_{PF} \times \dfrac{\text{LMTD}_{CF}}{\text{LMTD}_{PF}} = 0.4166 \times \dfrac{70}{55.81} = 0.5225
$$

Or, computing from the new outlet temperature (using energy balance):

If the same area and U produce a higher LMTD, the new $Q$ becomes $Q_{CF} = Q_{PF} \times (70/55.81) = 23.33 \times 1.254 = 29.26$ kW.

$$
\varepsilon_{CF} = \dfrac{29.26}{56.00} = 0.5225
$$

**Step 5: Comment. (0.5 marks)**

The counter-flow configuration provides an effectiveness of **0.52** compared to **0.42** for parallel flow — a **25% improvement** in heat transfer for the **same heat exchanger area**. This confirms the **superiority of counter-flow** heat exchangers in practice.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice**

**A counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool oil using water. The following steady-state data are obtained:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Oil inlet temperature | **120°C** |
| Oil outlet temperature | **70°C** |
| Water inlet temperature | **25°C** |
| Water outlet temperature | **55°C** |
| Oil mass flow rate | **1.0 kg/s** |
| Water mass flow rate | **0.8 kg/s** |
| Specific heat of oil | **2.0 kJ/kg·K** |
| Specific heat of water | **4.18 kJ/kg·K** |

**(a) Calculate the LMTD, the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ (given heat transfer area $A = 2.5$ m²), and the effectiveness. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the water flow rate is increased to **1.5 kg/s** while keeping the same inlet temperatures, find the new outlet temperatures of both fluids. Assume $U$ and $A$ remain constant. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: LMTD for counter flow. (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 55 = 65°C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 70 - 25 = 45°C
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{65 - 45}{\ln(65/45)} = \dfrac{20}{\ln(1.4444)} = \dfrac{20}{0.3677} = 54.39°C
$$

**Step 2: Heat transfer rate. (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 1.0 \times 2.0 \times (120 - 70) = 100 \text{ kW}
$$

Verification from cold side:

$$
Q = 0.8 \times 4.18 \times (55 - 25) = 0.8 \times 4.18 \times 30 = 100.32 \text{ kW} \approx 100 \text{ kW} \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Overall heat transfer coefficient U. (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}
$$

$$
U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{100}{2.5 \times 54.39} = \dfrac{100}{135.98} = 0.7354 \text{ kW/m}^2\text{·K} = 735.4 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

**Step 4: Effectiveness. (2 marks)**

$$
C_h = 1.0 \times 2.0 = 2.0 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_c = 0.8 \times 4.18 = 3.344 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
C_{min} = C_h = 2.0 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
Q_{max} = 2.0 \times (120 - 25) = 190 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{100}{190} = 0.5263
$$

---

**Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**New operating conditions:** $\dot{m}_c^{new} = 1.5$ kg/s, all else same.

**Step 1: New heat capacity rates. (1 mark)**

$$
C_h = 2.0 \text{ kW/K} \quad (\text{unchanged})
$$

$$
C_c^{new} = 1.5 \times 4.18 = 6.27 \text{ kW/K}
$$

**Step 2: New $C_{min}$ and maximum heat transfer. (1 mark)**

$$
C_{min} = C_h = 2.0 \text{ kW/K}
$$

$$
Q_{max}^{new} = 2.0 \times (120 - 25) = 190 \text{ kW (unchanged)}
$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer remains limited by oil side. (1.5 marks)**

The oil is still the limiting stream ($C_{min} = C_h$). The maximum possible oil outlet temperature is the water inlet temperature (25°C), so $Q_{max}$ is the same. The actual heat transfer depends on $U \cdot A$ and the new LMTD.

**Step 4: Apply energy balance on oil side to find new oil outlet. (1.5 marks)**

By energy balance:

$$
Q = C_h \times (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}^{new})
$$

We need another equation. Using $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}_{new}$:

Let $T_{h,out}^{new} = T_{h,out}$ and $T_{c,out}^{new} = T_{c,out}$ to be determined.

From oil: $Q = 2.0 \times (120 - T_{h,out}^{new})$

From water: $Q = 6.27 \times (T_{c,out}^{new} - 25)$

New LMTD:

$$
\Delta T_1 = 120 - T_{c,out}^{new}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out}^{new} - 25
$$

$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} = 0.7354 \times 2.5 \times \text{LMTD} = 1.8385 \times \text{LMTD}$ (in kW)

Equating all three: Let $Q = Q$ from oil side.

**Step 5: Solve by iteration / NTU method. (2 marks)**

Using the **NTU-effectiveness relation** for counter-flow:

$$
\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \dfrac{1.8385}{2.0} = 0.9193
$$

$$
C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{2.0}{6.27} = 0.319
$$

For counter-flow:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}
$$

$$
\text{NTU}(1 - C_r) = 0.9193 \times (1 - 0.319) = 0.9193 \times 0.681 = 0.6261
$$

$$
\exp(-0.6261) = 0.5345
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - 0.5345}{1 - 0.319 \times 0.5345} = \dfrac{0.4655}{1 - 0.1705} = \dfrac{0.4655}{0.8295} = 0.5612
$$

**Step 6: Find outlet temperatures. (1 mark)**

New heat transfer:

$$
Q_{new} = \varepsilon \times Q_{max}^{new} = 0.5612 \times 190 = 106.6 \text{ kW}
$$

Oil outlet:

$$
T_{h,out}^{new} = 120 - \dfrac{106.6}{2.0} = 120 - 53.3 = 66.7°C
$$

Water outlet:

$$
T_{c,out}^{new} = 25 + \dfrac{106.6}{6.27} = 25 + 17.0 = 42.0°C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Increasing the water flow rate from 0.8 to 1.5 kg/s increased effectiveness from **0.526** to **0.561** (a modest 6.6% gain). The oil outlet temperature dropped from **70°C** to **66.7°C**, and the water outlet temperature dropped from **55°C** to **42.0°C** (more water to heat, hence lower outlet temperature). This illustrates that increasing the flow rate of the **non-minimum** stream gives diminishing returns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing LMTD end-temperature pairings:** Many students use **inlet-inlet** and **outlet-outlet** for both configurations. For **counter flow**, you MUST pair **hot-inlet with cold-outlet** and **hot-outlet with cold-inlet**. Mixing this up loses **3 full marks**.
> 
> 2. **Using °C in the logarithm argument:** The ratio $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2$ is dimensionless — only the **magnitude** matters. Using a ratio of Celsius values is correct because the **scale cancels**.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to convert LPH to kg/s:** A very common error. **1 LPH = 1/3600 kg/s** (not 1/60). The factor **60** is for LPM, not LPH.
> 
> 4. **Reporting effectiveness > 1:** This indicates a **calculation error** or **measurement inconsistency**. KTU examiners will immediately flag this and deduct **2 marks** for "physically impossible result."
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the AMTD-as-LMTD case:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD formula becomes indeterminate ($0/0$). Always mention that **AMTD = LMTD** in this special case.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision for KTU Viva and Lab Exam:**

- [ ] **LMTD formula** is universal for any flow configuration — only the **definition of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** changes.
- [ ] **Parallel flow:** pair $(T_{h,in}, T_{c,in})$ and $(T_{h,out}, T_{c,out})$.
- [ ] **Counter flow:** pair $(T_{h,in}, T_{c,out})$ and $(T_{h,out}, T_{c,in})$.
- [ ] **LMTD > AMTD always** when $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$ — this is mathematically guaranteed.
- [ ] **Effectiveness** $\varepsilon \in [0, 1]$; values above 0.7 indicate a **well-designed** exchanger.
- [ ] **Counter flow always has higher LMTD** than parallel flow for the **same inlet conditions** (except trivial case $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in}$).
- [ ] **Counter flow can bring cold outlet > hot outlet** — impossible in parallel flow.
- [ ] **Energy balance check:** $Q_h \approx Q_c$ — if not, report the **average** or check for **heat loss to surroundings**.
- [ ] **Conversion:** 1 LPH = 1/3600 kg/s; 1 LPM = 1/60 kg/s (assuming water density = 1000 kg/m³).
- [ ] **Specific heat of water:** $C_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K (use 4.18 if problem specifies).
- [ ] **Units of U:** W/m²·K or kW/m²·K — be consistent with $Q$ units.
- [ ] **NTU method formula** for counter-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}$ — used when $U$ and $A$ are given and outlet temperatures are unknown.
- [ ] **No cross-over in parallel flow:** the two temperature curves **must converge** but **never cross** — if data shows otherwise, treat as **measurement error**.
- [ ] **Viva question (very common):** *"Why is counter flow preferred in industries?"* — Answer: **Higher LMTD, higher effectiveness, ability to bring cold outlet above hot outlet, and more compact design for same duty.**

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — All the best for your KTU 2024 Lab Examination!**
